At the complete dumbfounded look on her face, sitting there at her desk with her glorious PC setup glowing in the faint lighting of her painfully pink bedroom, Mark started snickering like a caught school girl. "Sorry," he's offering, "it was cute."

Cute, he said. She furrowed her brows in confusion, hearing far too many things at once.

Her phone buzzed next to her, and she saw the Instagram icon. Although she already knew, she clicked to open the notification anyway.

"Bold," she hums, shaking her head as she looks back up at him, "you don't get tired of the frat boys bullying you in the comments?"

"Never," he shrugs, "totally worth it."

It was cute, domestic, the kind of thing people would see and immediately build a story around. Mark posted her like she was someone he knew well enough to tease publicly, someone he was allowed to catch mid-practice, mid-life.

Of course, it wasn't the first time he had done this. She'd been a topic on Mark's Instagram for a while now, but there was something about it being in her bedroom, clear as day for anyone to realize, that made her bite down on the inside of her cheek. Why was she bothered? It wasn't like she was worried about who would see it.

It didn't matter. People were going to speculate anyway, and somehow SUnews already knew everything whether someone posted about it or not.

The only thing was that Hyein and Hanni were only going to further lose their minds and freak out about all the people that apparently liked her.

"Are you okay?" Mark had asked after the silence settled, suddenly concerned, "Hey, if it bothers you..."

"No," she cuts him off, gently, "I don't care, people will be people, you know."

He looked unsure if he believed her, but she had looked away because her phone vibrated in her hand again. Automatically, she presumed it was one of her friends downstairs seeing the post.

It was not.

She could feel it, and she could also feel the way everything suddenly felt too colorful, too bright.

She didn't know why every word he typed to her did something concerning, it's not like they were romantic, or even coherent. Often times, he was speaking in riddles and she had no idea what he was talking about in the first place. Yet, there was something about them that made her chest tighten, the way he noticed everything, the way he always seemed to be right, to catch the smallest things she hoped no one would notice.

"Pastry?" her head jerked up too fast to see Mark standing there in front of her, holding out a pastry to her with a careful smile on his face, "Sorry, didn't mean to freak you."

"It's fine," she shakes her head, practically throwing her phone down, "thanks for all of this."

"Don't mention it," he shrugs, "now, show me how you play this game."

31: where to look

Things were starting to feel too familiar.

Like she'd taken a one way trip back to her childhood, to the last several years of her life, where her entire purpose was to be stared at, to be photographed, to smile and nod her head. It was what you wore, the way you spoke, the tone in your voice, if your smile was white or genuine, everything you did was suddenly a spectacle on the daily telegram.

But, she was no longer in the Upper East Side, she was at a college she came to in hopes that maybe things would feel a little different, while still feeling a little similar, too.

This was too similar.

Solar thought she'd gotten used to all of the stares, the weird looks, the glares, the whispers that they pretended no one else could hear. But there was something different in the air as she sat down in her Intro to Business class, trying not to hyper fixate on the way it felt like every single person in the room was staring at her.

"So," the voice of a demon entered her ears, the chair scraping against the floor as Danielle sat next to her, noticing the way she was blankly staring down at an empty page in her notebook and deciding it was her perfect chance, "which one?"

The only thing she could seem to do was bite the inside of her cheek. She knew what she was having the audacity to ask, the same way she always seemed to either mention them, or her brother. Danielle was obsessed with frat boys, apparently.

Either way, she was going to act like a complete idiot. "What are you on about today?" she exhales, not in the mood for her passive aggressive bullshit, still refusing to even spare her a glance.

"Oh, come on," she snorts, "girl, you know who I'm talking about, Mark or Haechan? Don't tell me you're messing with them for fun."

"Do you really think I would tell you?" she felt the need to turn to her, asking in complete genuine shock.

She just shrugs, unamused, "Word travels either way."

Solar just sighed, turning back to face the front again, praying to God the professor would enter that door right then and save her.

"Weird, I thought Jaehyun kept better track of things he cared about."

It took everything in her not to turn and slap her, every bone in her body was fighting the urge. She tried to look around, only to see various girls in the room still looking at her like she was a science project.

Things were beginning to sound too loud.

Much to her dismay, going to a Statistics class with Mark Lee plopping into the seat next to her, passing her a piece of strawberry gum like nothing was wrong, did not help. Actively, it made the situation much worse. The way people were staring and acting like they weren't trying to sneak a photo of the two of them simply talking was nauseating, on it's own.

Solar tried to keep her cool, she just leaned back in the chair, tapping her foot on the ground, pretending she was listening to whatever Mark was talking about.

"Sol," the sound of her name threw her back into reality, giving her whiplash, as she blinked furiously to catch back up, head turning to meet his eyes, "yo, you good there?"

She wondered, then, if Haechan was right. He was oddly right about other things.

What exactly wouldn't she believe? she thought then, is it really on purpose?

No, her brain told her instantly. It would be inconsiderate of him to do it on purpose, he wasn't that kind of person. He'd brought her food because he was worried she hadn't been eating.

He'd also lost her a party a couple of times, even though she had kind of ran away the second.

"Yeah," she finally mutters, but her eyes kept glancing around the room, noting all of the people that kept stealing glances.

Too loud.

Mark caught it, the way she was chewing on her bottom lip, how her brows were creasing slightly, eyes bouncing from side to side like she'd taken a drug. "Don't worry about them," he offers after a minute, "they're just jealous, you know."

"Speculation's just getting old," she exhales, "as if dealing with my crazy brother wasn't enough."

Even though, she still hadn't talked to him since the football game. It felt like a distant memory now.

"They're bored," he hums, not caring if anyone heard him as he leaned forward on the desk, on his elbows, "I can stop if you want, okay? I just like to post things I like."

He was proud of himself for that one, with the way his hair fell in his eyes and he started giggling. Solar scoffed, shaking her head at how shameless he was becoming, it made her think of someone else.

Which was why she couldn't stop herself from blurting, "your friend thinks you're doing it on purpose."

Maybe she was adding fuel to the fire, but it almost felt fun as Mark shook his head, "yeah, he would think that."

"Watch it," she teases, "sounding a little saucy there, Mark."

-

He was already there when she sat down.

She could've gone to a different seat, could've made an effort to sit somewhere where it wouldn't feel so intentionally obvious. But by then, she was exhausted, and convinced the whispers and stares wouldn't stop no matter what she did.

The thing was, if she sat somewhere else, it'd look like she was avoiding him, but if she sat next to him, it looked like something entirely different. She was beginning to think her existence was a lose-lose situation.

So, she slid into the seat beside someone she swore she hated but then thought about anyway, with as much indifference as she could possibly force into one moment, dropping her bag on the floor and refusing to look at him immediately. She could feel those deep eyes on her anyway, irritatingly, as she was learning Haechan had a way of looking without making it obvious, observing rather than staring.

"Rough morning, sweetheart?" Because he could never help himself, and probably never would.

Exhaling through her nose, she closes her eyes, still not allowing herself to turn to him, "don't start, Haechan."

"Not starting," he hums, crossing his arms over his chest and leaning back in the chair. His biceps were flaring again, and she only knew because she coulds see the veins in her peripheral. "Unless you say my name like that again."

"Haechan," she snaps at him, before realizing she had said his name again. She jerks her head away, embarrassed at the fact she fell into his trap like a complete idiot. "Close your mouth."

She snuck another shameful glance at him, only to find him looking absurdly put together for someone living in a fraternity house stinking of beer and terrible decision making skills. His black hoodie hung loosely off his shoulders, hair messy in the way that looked accidental but wasn't. He had a pen in between his fingers now, spinning it lazily, like he was one of those people who could easily pretend not to care about anything yet still manage to know every detail.

"You sure?" he raises a brow, "Sure looks like you have something to say, princess."

"Nope."

"That face says something," he shakes his head

"I'm tired," she bluntly states, making eye contact with him again.

"Of me?"

"Among other things."

The corners of his mouth were doing that thing again, the one he was so good at hiding, "Mm, good morning to you, too."

She couldn't look at him, she couldn't. There was something happening she could only ignore, causing her to face the front of the room again and stare at the whiteboard, trying very hard not to think about the scent of vanilla and the way she had drunkenly blurted too many truths out sitting in his bedroom next to his PC setup.

Everything was wrong.

Only then, did her eyes notice the question the professor had wrote on the board for today's warm up, something she was very ashamed to say did not get left behind in high school.

How context teaches an audience what to see.
Does an image have meaning before someone captions it?

She sat there, peering at those words, with her mouth slightly open. Quite frankly, everything was starting to feel like a really poorly timed joke.

Haechan followed her gaze to the board, before looking back at her, with that stupid smirk spreading across his lips. "Looks like even the professor's a fan," he snorts.

"Don't," she hissed, not looking at him still.

"I think it's a good question," he says anyway, because apparently she cared about what he thought. Did she?

She still didn't believe him for a second. The room around them continued to slowly fill with students, chairs scraping against the floor, the sound of dropping bags, voices overlapping in tiny pockets of conversation. A few girls in the row ahead of them turned once, too quickly, pretending they'd been looking around the room casually when Solar met their eyes. Someone else glanced from Solar to Haechan, then down at their phone like they were about to post on Twitter.

"Well, what's your answer, sweetheart?" he asked, noticing the way she was chomping down on the inside of her cheek rather hard, pulling her out of her deep thoughts.

She didn't think about it in the slightest, as she stated confidently, "I think this guy fucking hates me."

"Valid interpretation," he chuckles.

"Well," she huffs, "what's yours then?"

He'd stopped spinning the pen swiftly, causing her to suddenly regret asking. Those irises peer at the messily written marker again, expression settling into something not teasing or playful, a rarity she had only ever seen in a couple of moments, like at the game or in his room on Friday. "An image has meaning," he mutters after a second, like he didn't want anyone but her to know his answer, "a caption tells people the meaning they want to convey, whether it's true or not."

That was the problem with him. Sometimes, he said things that sounded like they were coming from someone with the capability to think, making everything that much worse.

"Did you actually do the reading?" she found herself asking, because she still refused to believe he could do things, even if she had seen them for herself by now.

"I'm capable of literacy."

"Right."

His eyes are on her again before she could process, "you disagree?"

"I didn't say that."

"So, you agree, then."

"No," but her word came out too fast, she could feel the heat on her cheeks.

"Sounds like you agree," he hums.

She crosses her arms, not wanting to give him the sense of satisfaction, "I think people see what they want to see, caption or not."

"Sure," he shrugs, "but the caption frames their judgment."

Something in her dared her to think about the way Mark captioned all the photos he'd posted of her, the way he acted like it wasn't a big deal.

She wanted to make a comment, some kind of dry joke, because that was what they did. But one wasn't coming to mind. So, she just sat there, letting his words sink into her skull like a virus she was starting to believe didn't have a cure.

From the front of the room, their professor entered with a stack of papers tucked against his chest, calling for everyone to take a seat. "Before we begin," he'd began, setting the papers on the podium and pushing his glasses up his nose, "please refer to our warm up, does image have meaning before someone captions it?"

The professor clicked to the next slide. Two identical photos appeared on the screen: a student sitting alone on a campus bench at night, head tilted down, phone glowing in their hand. The text under the first image read, freshman struggles to make friends during first week at SU, and the second one, student journalist waits outside late night protest for exclusive interview.

"Same image," the professor begins, "two frames that do not change, but the caption, the caption teaches you how to feel about the subject before you've had time to form your own interpretation."

She could feel the shift of his attention next to her.

It was fine, she told herself, everything is fine.

"The question today is not whether media lies," the professor continued. "That is too simple. The more useful question is: what does framing make easier to believe?"

The question repeats in her head, and she closed her eyes for a second to release a deep exhale. Too many things popped into her brain, far too many she wasn't interested in thinking about, Mark being sweet, Haechan apparently dangerous, Solar is messy, Minji is her girlfriend, Jaehyun is perfect.

Her head was starting to hurt.

Haechan noticed the way her jaw was tightening, leaning over so slightly to mumble, "a caption is a leash if people are bored enough to follow it."

She whipped her head to peer at him before she could stop herself, knowing it was a bad idea. His words managed to get under her skin in the worst way, hitting too directly, pulling her right back into his messages, to the way he'd told her Mark was making the attention worse on purpose, to the way he always sounded like he was warning her and insulting her at the same time.

"What's with you and leashes?" she snorts to him, immediately resulting to a deflection.

His eyes flickered somewhere else on her face, other than her eyes, and a smirk spread over his annoying face, "I'm sure you'd like to know, sweetheart."

Something caught in her throat.

The professor continued to explain framing, but Solar was only catching pieces of it now, something about selection and emphasis, the way stories become recognizable once the same frame is reused enough times. She wondered, for a split second, if someone like him had a frame too, the way people constantly put her in the same box of rich girl, Mark's girl, Minji's girl, Jaehyun's sister.

He did, the truth was clear, people constantly did the same to him, fuck boy, bad influence, Mark's best friend, Sigma Chi's problem child.

"Watch it, Hyuck," she didn't know where the words came from, but something in her would always take on a challenge.

Especially one like this.

He went still then, for the slightest of seconds, not dramatically or enough that anyone else would notice, other than her, because she was looking at him then and trying to act like she wasn't. She saw the tiny pause in his hand, the pen no longer moving between his fingers, the almost imperceptible shift in his expression before he forced it back into something nonchalant.

"Aish," he shook his head, "dangerous game, princess."

She tilted her head, feigning ignorance, "that's what Mark calls you, isn't it?"

His eyes didn't leave her for a second, unworried about the other people in the room, about whatever the professor was talking about because he was already confident in his knowledge, anyway.

"Uhuh, sure is."

For once, it almost seemed like he didn't know what to say, causing her to play into it further, because it finally felt like a win. "What, only he's allowed to call you that?"

"No, he just thinks a lot of things are his," he deadpans, "it sounds a lot better coming from you."

The words settled heavy between them. Somehow, Haechan always got the last laugh.

And she hated it.

Before she could even begin to pick apart anything he said, he'd leaned back in his chair, spinning his pen around like nothing happened.

"Pay attention," he urges, like she was crazy for still looking at him, "you need this class."

Her glare was sharp, narrowing her eyes as she snapped, "for what?"

He smiled then, a rare one from him. At first, he almost hesitated, before humming, "for figuring out when someone's telling you where to look."

 

 

32: night calls

By the time Media and Society ended, she'd had enough, she was done with thinking, whether it was naturally true or not. Solar was exhausted with the words she'd just heard constantly repeating in her head, Mark this, Mark that, the way the two of them always had the weirdest pointed comments about each other, like they were communicating telepathically.

Packing her bag with a little too much force, she shoves her notebook into the front pocket, pretending she didn't feel Haechan still beside her, moving as slow as he could probably manage, the stature of a man with far too much time on his hands. The room had become loud again, students standing, chairs scraping, girls pretending not to look at her while looking right at her.

Mark thinks a lot of things are his, she squeezes her eyes shut and grips the strap of her bag.

She didn't want to, or perhaps didn't have the capacity, to say anything else to him, she just spun on her heel and headed down the steps towards the door, all before Haechan could utter a word.

Her brain was closed for business, she'd decided.

But by the time she entered the gaming lab for the evening eSports practice, she knew she was only lying to herself.

The lab was already glowing when she entered, RGB lights bleeding across the rows of monitors, the familiar hum of PCs buzzing under the low chatter of her teammates, something that she otherwise would've found relaxing, on any other day. Usually, the lab was where everything felt lighter, easier, where she could bury her cares and click heads. But the millisecond she sat down, logging in, she could feel Minji's gaze next to her.

"Hey Sol," she offers, likely after watching the girl type rather angrily, taking out some frustration on the keyboard.

"Hey Ji," she returns evenly, but her words didn't exactly sound normal.

She knew Minji noticed the way she always did, but since she'd already pretty much told her what was going on, she didn't ask any questions. She just sends her that soft, reassuring smile, like everything was going to be fine.

Practice started quickly after that, Doyoung wasting no time throwing them into a scrim block, enjoying watching the five of them scramble. The first map was decent, possibly slightly messy, but workable. Chenle complained about getting discorded, Olivia laughed so hard she nearly missed a game chaning callout, Haerin said legitimately six words in total.

During the second fight, Minji took one of her infamous off angles on Ashe, slipping toward the high ground with that horrible yet gorgeous confidence that made Solar's fingers twitch automatically. For half a second, she almost mashed that teleport key again. She knew the rhythm, Minji took space and she followed, it had worked so many times her body wanted so do is so desperately.

Heartwarming but dangerous.

It tasted like poison in her mouth, causing her to wrinkle her nose at her screen, like his face was on it. She climbs up a wall next to where her team is, chunking a few angry kunais and dinking one of their supports.

It's fine.

Minji took damage, but Solar held her suzu and stayed with the core of the team. She instead cleansed the enemy dive the second it would've landed, flipping the fight entirely.

"Good hold!" Doyoung called from behind, voice as flat as ever.

The game ends, flashing the POTG screen, but Solar wasn't watching as she leaned back into the leather. Minji's eyes were on her again, voice soft as she takes off half her headset to lean over, "nice discipline."

She met those soft eyes for a second and tried really hard not to look at her lips, "thanks."

Everything was fine, everyone was complimenting her again, the way it was meant to be. Really, it was a good thing, it meant her VOD reviewing and endless practice over the weekend helped, it meant the thing she had been most annoyed about had not been useless, because somewhere, somehow, Haechan had gotten into her gameplay and fixed a piece of it.

She would not be giving him any credit.

After practice, the team gradually scattered, everyone tired in the Monday post-scrim fog that made your legs feel broken. Solar lingered near her setup, pretending to check her settings while Minji was gathering her things next to her, glancing up a few times before she hums, "you walking back?"

She turned to tell her yes, of course, because the two of them walking together had become comfortably second nature. Their walks had become routine, one of the few parts of campus that didn't feel like performance. Minji would always talk about film angles, or their team comp, or some weird thing Chenle said. They would laugh, everything would feel easy, and she would pretend she wasn't thinking about the plush mattress underneath her as Minji came closer and closer to her face.

Still, she would say yes even if she was afraid of walking beside Minji right then, with everything floundering in her mental, complicated in a way she didn't have the energy to unpack. But, before she could open her mouth, Minji had peered down at her phone, a wince covering her face, "Okay, I lied, rain check, apparently my film group is tweaking."

You could tell she was genuine by her choice of words, causing her to chuckle. "It's okay," she assures the concerned girl, "go take care of your film group, Ji."

"Sorry," she pouts anyway, her hand lingering over the shoulder of her leather chair, "I'll make it up to you, kay?"

"Mhm."

"Text me if you need me, Sol," she urges, "mean it."

Solar nods, sending her a soft smile. Minji left with one last small wave, disappearing through the lab doors with her camera bag bumping against her hip. She watched her go for half a second longer than necessary, guilt and relief tangling together until she couldn't tell which feeling was winning.

By the time she made it back to Alpha Phi, the sky had already gone dark thanks to the end of fall weather dawning down onto them.

The house was warm when she stepped inside, closing the ginormous pink door behind her, all pink light, laundry detergent, and Hanni's fruity candle burning somewhere it probably shouldn't be. Hanni and Hyein were in the living room, tucked under the same blanket, two nosy little gargoyles waiting for her return.

Hyein snapped her gaze first, scanning up and down Solar before she pondered, "that is a face."

"Good observation," Solar sarcastically hums to her as she makes her way to the stairs.

"Uhuh, that's totally your questionable decision making face."

Solar stopped at the bottom of the stairs to turn to her. Hanni had sat up straighter, preparing to rise to her feet and go into mom mode. "Want some tea?" she calls gently, like that of a worried parent, "more fruit?"

She thought about her words for a moment, about the way she wouldn't be able to not practice if she walked in her bedroom, about the reminder of everything going on sitting in plastic bags next to her bed. She thought about freedom, and how she wanted it so bad yet ran right where she should've known she would not fully find it.

And suddenly, she spun on her feet, heading right back to the door she had entered from moments ago. Hand on the handle, she turns to the two girls, confidently announcing, "I'm actually going on a walk."

The two exchange confused glances from whatever they witnessed just now. "Okay," Hyein deadpans, "with Minji?"

"No," she exhales, "alone, I need air."

"Alright," Hanni nods, grabbing Hyein's arm to tell her not to say anything rash, "be safe, and let us know if you need us, okay?"

Solar nods, sending them that same reassuring smile that was starting to feel a little heavy that week. And then, she swings open the door again, heading out into the SU air, as if she never left.

-

For a while, she walked without deciding nor thinking about where she was going. It felt important, in a weird way, not having a destination or someone waiting for her at the other end, no caption slapping itself over an image of her merely existing and trying to live life.

It was just her, Solar Lee, brunette hair messily tied behind her head and clipped with a claw clip, loose sweatshirt hanging over her hands, her hair flying in the slight breeze of September. She passed the library first, then the dining hall, then the stretch of sidewalk where the campus lights turned softer and the few crowds of students thinned out further. The cold air hit her cheeks in waves, sharp enough to make her feel more awake, but not enough to quiet her brain.

It took her a minute to realize where her feet walked, back to the Extracurricular Building where it felt like everything here at SU began. Her eyes peer up at it for a moment, the tall windows glowing faintly from the inside, the way it felt so different at night, less impressive, but so much clearer. The first time she'd stood here, she was barefoot, holding her red-bottomed heels with exasperation and determination alike, Mark existing beside her like he always had, awkwardly sweet yet nervous, showing her around campus like he was proud of it.

Her irises drift toward the side of the building where the gaming lab was tucked away, probably dark now, all the expensive PCs sleeping behind their locked glass. How strange it was, how quickly a place could become so important, it felt like yesterday it was simply a room, before it'd turned into tryouts, Minji, the team, VODs, and the one version of herself nobody could compare to Jaehyun.

Something in her stomach told her to turn around and go back to her room, lay there in her confusion and confliction, but something else told her to walk closer. Her sneakers scrape against the pavement, the front doors appeared to be locked, but the smaller side entrance near the music rooms still had the gentlest sliver of light glowing through the glass.

Her footsteps slowed, a gush of wind pushes through, sending goosebumps up her skin, causing her to pull her sleeves down further, if possible. For a second, she thought it was probably campus security or a professor, even a janitor would've made sense.

That was, until she heard the faintest hum of music.

It wasn't from inside the building, it was far too faint for that and the studios were packed with sound proof tiling. More so, it sounded like sound leaking from someone's headphones, a low beat looping over itself.

Confused, she turns her head side to side, trying to figure out where such a random melody was coming from if not from inside the building.

Everything else seemed to slow down, then.

At the bottom of the side steps, half-hidden beneath the shadow of the building's overhang, Haechan was sitting with his laptop open on his knees, headphones around his neck, one hand pressed against his mouth like he was trying to listen harder. The screen lit his face in pale blue, catching the tired line of his eyes, the crease beneath his brows, the lack of performance in his expression.

All she could seem to think, as she stood there gawking at him in the dark, was that this didn't look like Haechan, not the one she knew.

Maybe, it was the one she was starting to know, the one that was underneath that chilled exterior.

Underneath the frame.

He was just a boy, then, someone sitting alone in the cold, listening to something that clearly wasn't matching what he was hoping for.

And painfully, he was one of the most observant people she had ever met, because his eyes jerked up so quickly, she had no time to prepare herself or attempt to hide.

"Aish, sweetheart," he shakes his head, making that annoying ts sound he always made, "if it isn't my favorite aggressively positioned support."

"You're favorite, huh?" she crosses her arms, knowing she had no choice but to give into the banter, the challenge, "Starting to sound like you're obsessed."

"Don't flatter yourself," he returns, eyes glancing back down at his laptop.

She walked closer to him then, even when her brain was telling her it was a horrible choice. "What are you doing, anyway?" she pended, "You're sitting outside at night with a laptop when the studio's literally right behind you."

"Working," he deadpans, not looking up from where he was intently clicking and dragging on his screen.

Solar was close enough now to see his laptop, watching as he adjusted different things that simply looked like colorful bars to her. "At night?"

"It's called work ethic, princess."

"You have a private room."

He stopped what he was doing to turn his head, "mm, still thinking about it, aren't you?"

"Stop deflecting," she snaps in irritation, trying to do anything other than show that he knew the best way to crawl under her skin.

"You know the individuals that live in Sigma," he casually states.

His honest answer made her pause, though she should be used to the few ones he'd given her by now, it always shocked her how random they were. It was too quick to be a joke, she could tell the way he said it, dryly and bluntly, without having to think twice.

He seemed to realize that fact, looking back down at his laptop before shutting it with one hand, the music cutting off abruptly. At the sudden silence, the air began to feel colder, even in her sweatshirt.

"What about the studio?" she inquires anyway, "Closed?"

"Done being useful," he answers, shoving his laptop into his bag. Once he zips it, he leans forward, elbows on his knees, and looks at her again. His eyes, those eyes, were enough to make the largest lump appear in her throat. "Your turn, sweetheart, why are you wandering the campus like you're in a sad indie film?"

"Needed air."

He didn't believe her, "uhuh."

"That's the reason," she defends, "you're deflecting, still, why don't you want to be around your frat boys?"

She kept telling herself it wasn't her business, because it wasn't. But there was something about the moonlight casting the slightest warmth on his toned skin, the way his black hair was falling in his eyes untouched, the way he just felt like a different person in the September air. She wanted to know, was the hard reality she refused to face.

Why?

Haechan stands then, pulling the strap of his bag over one shoulder, headphones still hanging around his neck, cord brushing against the front of his hoodie. He was less put together than he was in class, like the night had peeled something off him that daylight kept in place. "They're loud."

"On a Monday?" She knew it was a dumb question when the words came out. That much was confirmed by the look on his face, and she found herself looking at him for a second too long, the realization settling strangely between them.

Until he suddenly starts walking away. At first, she figured he was just going to leave, to go back to that house full of loud boys and call it a night, before he'd called behind him, without turning around, "Come on."

She watched him walk for a second, blinking as her brain was practically melting onto the concrete. He was walking the opposite way either of them would need to go, beginning to disappear into a darker stretch of campus she'd never been to.

"Yeah, that feels really safe," she hums, still not moving from where she was standing, next to those same side steps.

Haechan stopped then, turning to face her, the breeze blowing his black hair up enough to see his forehead, brown eyes twinkling underneath the daunting stars. "Didn't say you have to," he shrugs, "but if you really want air, sweetheart, I know the place."

She hated the way her feet started walking towards him before her brain could remind her how terrible of an idea it was.

"Okay," she exhales once she's standing right in front of him, enough for the vanilla to seep into her nostrils, "where might this magical place be?"

He smiled then, quiet and dangerous in the dark, "somewhere no one captions you."

33: life's never fair

For someone who always had something to say, Haechan was shockingly quiet when he wanted to be. It was seemingly the only thing she would allow herself to ponder as she followed him.

He didn't look back every few seconds to make sure she was still there, didn't tease her for her hesitation, didn't explain where they were actually going, which may have been the most obnoxious part of it all. No, he just walked with his laptop bag hanging off one shoulder, headphones still draped around his neck, hands tucked into the front pockets of his hoodie like this was the most natural thing in the world, as if he'd taken women to unmarked corners of the campus in the middle of the night multiple times before.

And she was nosy.

"You do this a lot?" she hums curiously as the silence slowly began to turn into another challenge.

"Do what?"

He still hadn't turned around, as she monotones, "lead women to random cryptic places?"

"Aish," he snorts, shaking his head, "I'm sure you're curious, princess," she thought he was going to leave her with that, before he'd continued dryly, "no, not too often, they're not too worried about where we are in those moments."

Gross.

"Good God," she mutters in disgust, "I take back when I said you weren't like they say."

He was smiling, though she could only see his side profile, and she was still walking a bit behind him, sneakers scratching against the concrete in sync. But he didn't say anything, as they rounded a corner, going into a weird back alleyway she had never been to, somewhere behind some random building on campus.

She found herself feeling oddly uneasy as they walked, the lighting becoming lesser the further they got. Before she could open her mouth and make some kind of stupid joke about him being a murderer, the boy had stopped next to a ladder. He didn't even look at her as he began climbing it, like it was no big deal.

All she could do was stand there and watch him in utter bewilderment. He stopped a few feet up, looking down to see her gawking up at him. "Starstruck?" he teases in that blunt voice, snapping her out of her gaze and causing a look of irritation to spread over her face.

"I... Fuck off, are we even allowed to do that?" she snaps.

Haechan snorted, shaking his head in disbelief, before continuing up the ladder. "We both know you don't care about the answer to that question, princess."

He was right, she was maybe half avoiding the obvious. Something about sitting on a rooftop with Haechan was not where she imagined her Monday night going, and she couldn't seem to mentally decide how she felt about it. She bit down on her lip, her mind thinking about Mark, about Minji, about the devious smirk on Danielle's lips as she tilted her head to the side and went, "which one?" as if it was the most innocent question in the world, the way she had yet to speak to her brother since the football game, but seemingly still felt like there were at least ten pairs of eyes on her at every waking moment.

It took her a minute, to think about how she really felt right then, when she stopped considering everyone else around her. In her memory, she recalled the way Haechan caught her in the hall, drunkenly, and made her sit down long enough to collect herself, how he noticed the smallest things about her gameplay, what was on the board, all of the things she never thought anyone could be observant enough to figure out. She thought about the way Mark looked at him like he was ready to kick him, and how he looked back at Mark like he'd seen the play before and already had the entire ending in his head.

But, there was something underneath the feelings, underneath the basic tension of two boys that liked the same girl. There was something behind the way he knew about Danielle, the way Danielle constantly alluded at something behind Haechan and Mark, the way that stupid news account said deja vu like this was something everyone had seen before. She wondered, perhaps, if she was living in some kind of simulation where everyone knew what the future held except her.

Though, she pondered, looking back up at Haechan, who was almost to the top of the ladder now, about to climb onto the roof of the Language Arts Building, maybe this was her time to figure it all out.

So, she exhales deeply, her hands gripping the cold metal sides of the ladder and beginning to climb. She'd admit, it felt daring, like she was just a stupid teenager breaking the rules.

There was a slight breeze that hit the skin of her face once she reached the top, climbing onto the rooftop swiftly. Haechan was already up there, sitting on the ledge, legs dangling off the sides like he'd done this a hundred times before. His dark hair was unkept and messy, falling in his eyes, though he remained unbothered as he stared up at the sky, pulling a blunt out of his pocket, along with a lighter.

Ignoring all of the alarms going off in her head, she hesitantly climbed onto the ledge herself, sitting a few feet away from him, letting the breeze hit her face and accepting it like pleasure. The moonlight casted a yellow gaze across the Earth, the skrtch of Haechan's lighter the only noise between them as he lit his blunt, taking a hit before blowing smoke into the air.

She was trying to think, then, of what to say, where to start, how to not make herself look like an idiot, or show that she possibly slightly cared. But he'd dissipated all of her thoughts, blowing another bubble of smoke into the air, before he reaches into the pocket of his hoodie, pulling out a Monster energy drink. He didn't make eye contact with her as he held it out, just kept staring up at the sky.

He didn't say anything. Her eyes cautiously stare down at the drink for a moment, it was her favorite flavor, the Pink Punch. "How'd you know I like this one?" she asks, confused.

"You always have cans around your setup in the lab," he hums, like it was normal.

Her lips form a straight line as she takes the can from him, cracking the top and taking a drink because she was scared of what she might say if she didn't distract herself. He continued to smoke his blunt, not saying anything, just enjoying the quiet of the night for a moment.

"Really sounds like you're my biggest fan," she muttered after a couple of minutes.

"I notice," he deadpans, "the things other people don't."

"Have you ever noticed how absurdly large your ego is?"

"Mm, proud of you for that one, sweetheart," he responds, unfazed and blank, and the slight condescendence was enough to make her realize he was permanently under her skin, whether she liked it or not. Another beat of silence falls between them, with her focusing on drinking her Monster as an excuse for not responding. Until finally, he hums into the night air, "I started coming here freshman year," he starts, taking another hit, before continuing, "when they get too fucking annoying, or girls wouldn't leave me alone, or when the bullshit piled up too high..." he trails off, clearly thinking back to a specific instance, "feels surreal, like no one else exists."

She bit the tip of her tongue a few times, thinking about how to respond, if she was going to be vulnerable or rude. "No one knows," she decides to ask, "that you come here?"

"Well," he exhales, putting out his blunt before shoving his lighter back in his pocket, "you do."

Her eyes started wandering because she was too afraid to look at his face, especially when she felt those deep eyes peering into her. They wander, across the rooftop, scattered with leaves from the nearby trees, to the lamps lining every few feet of sidewalk on the ground, to his bag, that he'd thrown lazily next to him.

"Why'd you choose music?" she blurts out another question, trying to fill the silence and run away from whatever it was that he just said to her. She didn't know why she was asking, it was something she'd never really even asked Mark.

You'd never know if he was genuinely surprised at her question or not. "Audio engineering," he corrects, before looking back up at the sky, "there's something about being the reason something sounds the way it does, the aspect of music that no one knows is doing all of the heavy lifting."

She was looking at him then, brows slightly furrowed as she thought about his answer. He seemed to feel her staring, but didn't look back at her, those brown eyes remained on the sky, dark and unreadable, like he'd decided it was easier to tell the truth to the moon than her face.

"I chose it," he goes on blankly, "because I already knew the rest, I hear a room, I hear when the vocal's too try, when the bass eats the kick, the reverb makes something sound too hollow, all the shit that makes people feel without knowing why."

He was passionate, she realized, and she didn't know if the blunt he'd smoked was started to take it's toll on him, or if this apparently special rooftop only she knew about was the key to him becoming a normal person. Thinking back to the way Mark spoke about music, there was a clear difference. Mark loved music, that part wasn't a lie, but his reasoning was vastly separate from Haechan's.

"That's the nerdiest thing you've ever said," she deadpanned, before she started giggling, because it was far too difficult to hold it in, "wow, I should've recorded that."

"Pipe down, princess," he snorts, "you play Overwatch competitively, let's not get too brave now." His eyes burned holes into her skull, and he always seemed to know exactly what to say to make her shut up.

The silence that followed after wasn't quite the same, not sharp or challenging, but merely existing, sitting between the two of them with the smoke and the night air. She was, almost, too scared to ruin it.

Haechan's gaze fell to his bag for a second, where his laptop was tucked away, like he was debating something. "I used to make stuff all the time," he hums after a second, "it was the only thing I did, couldn't sleep because I'd hear a melody and have to get it down before it left, couldn't sit in class because some dumb noise from the hall would turn into a snare in my head, shit like that."

"Used to?" she raises a brow, "what changed?"

There was the slightest shift in his jaw, so slight she barely caught it.

"Mm," he ponders, dragging the sound out like he was deciding how much of the truth he was going to allow her to hold, "I learned making something means nothing if the wrong person gets to tell the story first."

Her fingers tightened around the half empty Monster can in her hands. The sound of birds cawing in the distance became far too loud for her to have mental space for. It felt like one of those moments where she was meant to understand something, where some invisible wire had been pulled tight enough for her to hear the faint hum of it, but she couldn't quite hear the lyrics of the song.

"You gonna give me context this time?" she puzzles, because they both knew well enough there was a lot he wasn't telling her.

The boy just shakes his head, the breeze blowing his fringe up just long enough to show the glow of oil on his forehead, "lost my spark for awhile," he offers, "stopped finishing tracks, nothing sounded right anymore."

She was staring at him again, at the way there was the faintest twinkle in his right eye. Her nostrils twitched from the strong scent of vanilla, and her chest, her chest was pumping more than she would ever allow herself to admit. She thought then, that she was going to ask what happened that would make him lose his spark with music, thinking that it had to be connected to Mark, to what everything kept pointing to. She thought, she was going to get to the bottom of it, have her questions answered, and figure out why the two of them seemed to act like they'd been through this before.

Although, what did a girl have to do with music?

But she was too scared to ever ask something like that, like knowing the truth, the history behind all of the weird things, would be too much for her to handle. So, instead, she asks something else, something that would still give her a hint of an answer she seeked.

"Did you find it," she hums, "again?"

He looked at her then, the twitch of the corner of his lips, the way his eyelashes fluttered when he blinked.

"Could say that," he returns, "the quiet helps, the night helps, things like that."

"So, that's why you were working outside the studio?"

"Partly," he hums, looking down at the ground now beneath them, voice almost bitter, "and tuition doesn't pay itself, sweetheart."

"Tuition?" she repeats, like it was a new word for her. She guessed, she had never had to think about anything like that, so the word felt foreign on her tongue, brows creasing as she contemplated that, "but you're in Sigma Chi," she assessed, like that should mean Haechan was rich.

"Aish," he snickers, shaking his head, "not every guy in a frat is waiting on a trust fund."

At the realization of what he was saying, a part of her started to feel bad. It was enough to make anyone feel the urge to vomit, feeling bad for Haechan, what kind of reality was she suddenly living in?

Still, she pursed her lips as she mumbles, "I didn't mean..."

"I know what you meant," he cuts her off, though it wasn't cruelly, "it's not your fault," he adds, noticing the way she was staring at her hands in her lap like she'd committed a felony, "I get by like anyone else, sell beats, mix tracks, tune vocals for dudes that think singing half a note flat is some kind of artistic talent," he snorts dryly, "you'd be surprised how much money some dipshit with a Soundcloud and an ungodly ego will spend to still sound like ass."

She laughed then, a real one that poured from her lips before she could stop it, before she could keep pretending that he wasn't genuinely sort of funny sometimes. After a second, she grew embarrassed at the sound of her own laugh, seemingly too aware of the pitch of it, the freedom in it, and covered her hand with her mouth, turning her head to peer back down at the campus because she abruptly felt entirely too afraid to meet his eyes.

There didn't seem to be any good response to what he said. Haechan was too honest, more honest than she ever expected him to be. Right then, it didn't feel like she was sitting next to the same boy she'd seen highly walk out of his bedroom upstairs at her first Sigma Chi party. It was the same way he had looked at her while she drunkenly sat in his desk chair, not with the narrowed eyes ready to perform, but with a genuine gaze of interest, seriousness, genuity. She knew it, but she had no idea what to do with the newfound information that Haechan Lee wasn't as terrible as all of the rumors had made him out to be.

"I thought about it once, too," she musters, after a second of thought, before she decided that it was a safe space. Even though she didn't think he cared, even though it felt really dumb to sit there and talk with him like this, thinking about all of the girls in his phone right now begging for an ounce of his attention, she said it anyway. She didn't look back at him, but she could feel the burn of those irises, the pique of curiosity splashing into the air, "about music, I mean," she confirms further, clearing her throat, nails gently scratching against the Monster can like a nervous tic.

When she didn't say anything else, the boy next to her rose a brow, "Producing?"

She purses her lips, shaking her head, "no, like, singing..." she trails off, "I was in choir in school, because my parents told me I needed more extracurricular activities," she continues, seeing a vivid flash in her head, "but I really liked it, it was like... a way to release all of my pent up stress, let go of the pressure," she looked at Haechan then, and he was already intently staring at her, like it was the most interesting things she'd ever uttered, "my father could've gotten me an in, but I always felt too silly about it to ask," she tore her eyes away again, "and he'd already decided what I was meant to do without asking me first."

A wave of silence overcame them, not because he had nothing to say, but because he was genuinely thinking about it, the same way he'd sat and pondered on the edge of his bed that night as she drunkenly poured out her familial issues to him. He brings up one of his legs to the ledge, leaning his elbow on it and exhaling deeply, head tilting upward to peer at the stars in the sky. The night moon beamed down on them, illuminating the world just enough to cast the softest glow across their faces.

"Life never seems to be fair to those who deserve it the most," he mumbles after a moment, "but it's still something you can pursue, whether you tell him about it or not, you know."

She thought about what he said, before realizing he was technically very correct. For that was the entire reason she was here, on this campus, partly, was to do things she couldn't previously do. She supposed, that if she really wanted to, pursuing music in some sense could be one of those things, just like her being on an eSports team.

"That's true," she whispers lowly, still thinking about what that could even look like for her, "I don't know how to do any of that stuff, though."

"Well, luckily, I'm the best around, sweetheart."

She wrinkled her nose at the cockiness, twisting her head at him, "oh, so you'll help me?" she pends, tapping her finger to her chin in fake thought, "I do recall Mark saying he's better than you, though."

There was a flash of something behind those brown eyes, and he shook his head, chuckling, "Mark has no idea what he's doing half of the time, princess."

The venom in his words wasn't entirely obvious, but it was present nonetheless. She had to hold in her laughter at the ridiculousness of it all, though she found herself thinking that perhaps, this was the way she could finally get some answers about all of this.

"What makes you say that?" she asks, "he seems to do well in his studies, or whatever you people actually do in your classes."

That earned another chuckle from him, though it was sounding less and less humorous. However, it only made her presume she was getting closer to the wound. Usually, he laughed like he'd already decided where the conversation was going, like he knew the exact shape of the reaction he wanted to pull out of her and was waiting for her to catch up. This time, the sound was flat, dry, like it was scraping against something on the way out of his throat.

"He's good at it," he returns.

"At what?"

"Making everyone think he knows what he's doing," he discloses, almost bitterly.

A part of her was telling her to let it go, to not say anything else and leave it at that. It was what a normal person would have done, they'd have noticed the shift in his tone, deciding that whatever was hidden underneath it wasn't worth digging up on a rooftop in the middle of the night, nonetheless on a Monday.

But Solar Lee never stood down when it came to a challenge.

"He's your best friend," she recalls, "isn't he?" A stupid question, but seemingly the only one she decided to ask in that moment, to see the way his face changed.

His lips formed a straight line. "Yeah," he nods, "which is why I can admit his faults."

"You just sound bitter to me," she jokes, resulting back to the banter like a bad habit she couldn't break.

"Sure," he shrugs, "it's a tough industry."

"Wow, didn't get nominated for a Grammy this year?"

He shook his head again, a smile playing on his lips at the weird ways she always managed to make fun of him, and the way he was starting to allow it more and more, which was very unlike him.

And she was secretly very surprised at the lack of rebuttal from him.

"There was a scholarship," he monotones after a minute, "it was why I wanted to come here, big, fancy summer program, if you got it, you graduate with connections... big in to the whole thing," he continues, "they asked for an original demo."

She was staring at the side of his face, the mole next to his nose, the sharp outline of his jaw. "And?" she blinks, "You didn't win?"

His jaw shifted, like he was biting down on words that tasted sour. He wasn't quite smiling anymore, his brown eyes stayed focused on the campus below them, like he was disappearing into his own head, another world. "Worked on it for weeks," he drawls, "months, probably. I thought this was it, that I had the path laid out in front of me."

She thought back to what he said earlier, about someone else telling the story first. Her teeth bite down on her bottom lip for a moment. "What happened?"

His tongue pressed against the inside of his cheek, his raised leg tapping on the concrete ledge. For a second, it didn't seem like he was planning to say anything, until he chuckles dryly, quiet, humorless, "wrong person got to tell the story first."

The words settled over her skin, poking, daring. She looked at the stars in the night sky again, pondering, thinking back to the way her face kept showing up on everyone's screens like she was a worldwide celebrity worth talking about, the way girls stared at her, the way the whispers travelled. The way that people decided who she was before they ever had the chance to really see her clearly.

It tasted more familiar than she planned.

"Someone stole it?" she bluntly puzzles, brows furrowing.

His eyes cut to hers abruptly, almost causing her to flail off the ledge from the shock. A few beats of silence, before he cleared his throat, looking away again, which somehow seemed to make it worse. "Something like that," he mumbles, "by the time they got it, the name attached wasn't mine."

More questions popped into her head like a wildfire, the first one she desperately wanted to ask was who would do that? Why would someone do that to you? Did you say anything? It felt wrong, even to do to someone like him, right?

The campus below them suddenly felt eerily quiet.

"Haechan..." she utters softly, "that... really sucks... I'm sorry."

He didn't seem too phased by her gentle words, at least not that he showed. But deep down, there was something moving inside of him he refused to name.

"You don't have to say that," he deadpans, "I came to terms with it, you know." He drug a hand through his hair, messing it up even more, along with that slight fall breeze, before letting out a deep breath that sounded almost like a laugh, but not quite, "you asked why I said Mark doesn't know what he's doing."

She was going to say something, but he'd cleared his throat again, "making people believe you made something and knowing the pieces behind it are two very different things."

The words hit with force, she bit down on her lip, thinking back to everything she knew about these two friends, the way they spoke to each other, all of the weird little comments they made. Partly, things made a little more sense, but they also only seemed to get more confusing. She still had no idea, in the grand scheme, what any of this had to do with her.

But, there was one other realization.

"Was it Mark?"

He smirked then, black fringe in his eyes, eyelashes fluttering at he ran his fingers across the rough concrete texture of the ledge. "I didn't say that, princess."

"I'm not that dumb," she scoffs, "I mean, it seems pretty clear that's what you were getting at."

His gaze returned back to her, but she didn't nearly fall off the ledge that time. The air, however, seemed to shift between them. For a second, she thought he looked almost tired of the way she seemed to understand everything he said without him actually having to say it, that maybe the difference mattered to him.

"Careful, sweetheart," he coaxes, "starting to sound like you're the fan here."

She didn't say anything after that, didn't take the bait like she always would otherwise. Right then, Solar was too deep in thought, pondering the possibility that Mark had done that to his own friend. It made her wonder then, how much truth was behind the other words he'd said to her regarding his friend.

Haechan seemed to notice her deep thoughts, as he hummed out, "keep it on the rooftop." When she looked at him, about to ask what he meant by that, he'd added, "it's not worth mentioning, alright? I can tell what's going through that little head of yours."

"I'm not..." she trails off, because he was entirely correct on what she was thinking about. But, she decided that it was the right thing to do if that was what he wanted, "alright," she sighs, "what happens on the rooftop stays on the rooftop."

He snorted, finding her nerdiness endearing as he looked down at the ledge again.

Though, before either of them could say anything else, Solar's phone buzzed from where it was sitting between them. She didn't think much of it, figuring Hanni or Hyein noticed her absence.

That was, until she looked down at the message.

jaehyun:\ Hope you've been doing well.

jaehyun:\ And remaining focused on your studies.

jaehyun:\ Remember, it's about your future, Solar.

34: an easy tuesday

Solar woke up on Tuesday with far too many things on her mind.

There was no way in hell she was going to give her brother the satisfaction of a response, she had decided, as she padded across the carpet of her bedroom, chunking her bag on the floor, and lazily flopping onto the plush pink mattress. A sigh of relief left her lips as she sprawls out her arms, glad that she had at least gotten her Tuesday classes out of the way.

As casual as she was trying to be, it was hard to not think about the way the breeze had hit her skin last night, the way the moonlight illuminated the boy next to her like he was an inhuman life force. There was, perhaps, something about the crinkles at the corner of his eyes when he laughed, that she was finding particularly irritating that day, as she laid there, staring up at her ceiling.

She still couldn't seem to wrap her head around everything he'd said, about the scholarship that had seemingly been stolen from him, about the indirect confirmation it had a little something to do with Mark. And about the way he'd shaken his head as he hummed to keep it on the rooftop.

Her teeth nervously chew on the inside of her cheek, should she even tell Hanni or Hyein? She didn't know what the right thing to do was, not really. All she could seem to gather, was the harsh reality that it was bothering her more than she wanted it to.

But her thoughts were stolen by her obnoxiously formal brother every time she picked up her phone.

Heaving out a sigh, she buried her face in her hands with a grumble of agony as she sat there at her desk, the hum of her PC the only sound in the room. She had been trying to pre-warmup for practice that evening, but it was like her brain wasn't working at fully capacity.

"Sol?" she hears a soft, gentle knock on the door, followed by two heads peeking through the crack, "Hey, we brought grapes... again."

Knowing the two girls would enter whether she said no or not, she spins in her chair to watch as they come in, closing the door behind them. Hanni was already beaming like everything in her life was just perfect as she brings the glass bowl to her desk, setting it down next to the mousepad.

Thankful for the gesture, she doesn't waste anytime to pick up a green grape, popping it into her mouth. Only mid chew, did she finally speak, "thanks guys."

"It comes with a price!" Hyein pipes, "Now you gotta tell us the tea."

Solar narrowed her eyes instantly at the devious girl sitting there on the edge of her bed. Hanni, who looked at Hyein like a disapproving yet slightly proud parent, shook her head, "ignore her, that's not why we came in here," she insists, "we wanted to check on you, you seemed a little out of it today."

She exhales then, wondering how obvious it had been that she was in deep thought today. She wondered if anyone else had picked up on it, too, especially since there was so many more eyes on her these days. For all she knew, there were already tons of new SUanon posts about it.

"I'm okay," she shrugs after a second of thought, "just thinking a lot, trying to figure it all out, you know."

"With Mark?" Hyein blurts before she could stop herself, "Or Haechan..." she pauses again, eyes widening, "Or Minji?!"

"Hyein!" Hanni cursed at her under her breath.

Still, she wasn't mad, she knew the girls never had any bad intentions. "Not really that," she deflects, sighing, "my brother texted me, some stupid shit about how he hopes I'm focusing on my studies, which I know is a weird indirect threat."

The two girls were quiet for a moment.

"Are you sure?" Hanni pends, considerately, "You think he's trying to hint to you that he's watching you?"

She shrugs then, "maybe, or that he's just threatening me to not hang around his frat boys." Huffing, she spins back around to eat more grapes, mumbling, "it just pisses me off, the way he talks down on me like I'm a child."

"I get it," Hyein sympathizes, "that would make anyone mad."

"You're your own person, though, Sol," Hanni assures with a grin, "never forget it!"

"Yeah, screw him, he can't dictate your entire life!" Hyein practically yells, getting far too hyped up over the matter.

"You're both right," she utters, biting her bottom lip. Her mind was thinking, hard, on a few things then.

Like a particular party that happened every Friday night.

-

The lab was already glowing when she pushed through the doors that evening, the familiar bloom of RGB light pressing warm against the dark of the hallway behind her. The hum of the PCs filled the room before she'd fully stepped into it, that particular kind of white noise that she had, against all odds, grown comfortably fond of over the last several weeks.

Minji was at her setup already, headset resting around her neck, scrolling through something on her phone with the lazy focus of someone who'd been there long enough to feel at home. Her eyes came up the second Solar walked through, which was a thing that has quietly became more present the longer the two got to know one another.

Oddly enough, she didn't quite mind it. Solar caught her gaze, offering her a small, welcoming smile as she slides into the gaming chair next to her, "film group survive?" she hums, dropping her bag to the side.

The dark haired girl's expression shifted into something mildly pained, "barely," she returns, setting her phone face down on the table, "they spent twenty minutes arguing about whether the shot list needed to be chronological."

Knowing well that she had no comment on this sector, Solar raises a brow, "well, did it?"

"Nope," Minji exhales, flatly.

Solar almost smiled, cracking her knuckles before focusing back on her screen, fingers flying across the keyboard to log into her game account. In the meantime, steps shuffled into the room, monitors blooming to life around them as the rest of the team filtering inside, Olivia with her headphones already blasting something Solar could only faintly hear, Haerin quiet and specific about exactly where she placed her mouse on the pad, Chenle dropping into the chair next to her with the dramatic exhale of someone surviving a personal tragedy.

None of them really had the chance for hello's, as Doyoung pattered inside, his voice cutting through the soft hum of the room as he settles between them. He wasted no time to pull out a chair, notebook setting on his knee, "alright, going straight into scrims, no warmup block."

A few of them exchanged somewhat surprised glances, Minji looked rather pleased with this fact. "Rude," Olivia had muttered.

No one else said anything. Solar pulled her headset on, the world outside them compressing down into background noise the second the cushions settled over her ears, the usual chaos of her own head going mercifully muffled along with it. Rolling her shoulders, she grips the mouse, eyes narrowing at the screen in concentration.

She was entirely collected and cool, there was nothing else possibly infiltrating her thoughts, other than thinking about where she was Monday night, the crinkles at the corner of his eyes.

It was difficult to resist the urge to punch herself.

All she could do was take a deep breath and click to join the group, watching as they were instantly thrown into a scrim without hesitation.

The first map, as always, was King's Row.

She'd loaded in, settling into her crosshair in the spawn while they waited, finding her rhythm than she expected to. Doyoung had been rather sharp about rotations all week, about the tendency to cluster when the enemy had range, Haerin getting caught without a pocket, she could feel the adjust in how the team set up. They weren't as clumsy, not anymore.

Minji slipped to the high ground early, her Ashe angle opening the sightline Solar had been watching. Meanwhile, Solar tracked her without breaking her own position on the objective, a part of her hand twitching towards the teleport key. But she didn't do it, self control.

Chenle pushed the cart forward, Solar held her suzu, and four second later the enemy practically spammed their Q keys into nothing. She cleansed the five the second it landed, flipped the fight, and the next thing any of them knew, Victory was flashing across their screen when it felt like the game had only just started.

"Easy!" Olivia is belting, throwing her arms into the air.

"Chillax," Chenle bluntly snorts, "this isn't the finals."

"Nice hold, guys," Minji had offered through the comms, like she expected nothing less.

But the brunette simply exhaled, not saying a word. The noise in her chest had gone quiet, which was embarrassingly the most relief she had felt all day.

By the second scrim, Doyoung had stopped making comments between rounds. She'd learned, by now, that this meant he was making an egregious amount of notes, which was either very good or very concerning, and the flatness of his face was giving her nothing to work with nonetheless.

Leaning back in her chair between maps, Olivia tilts her head at Solar from the other side of Chenle, "you're locked tonight, Sol!"

Meeting the girl's eyes, Solar shrugs, "I'm always locked in," she sassily snaps.

"Nah, not like this," the girl shakes her head, Solar wasn't sure if she should feel offended or not. "You know what I mean," she adds, before scooting back into the desk to look at her monitor again. She did not have any idea what she meant, in reality. Perhaps there was a version of her that played well because she needed to avoid something else happening inside, yet it wasn't something she was ever going to allow herself to acknowledge.

Haerin had mentioned something quietly that the rest of them had to reconstruct into a full sentence among them, Chenle complained about his monitor for reasons no one was nerdy enough to understand, and Olivia had started spinning slowly in her gaming chair while they waiting for the second team to ready up.

"Stop," Doyoung urged sternly, without looking up at her. She'd stopped, eyes wide from how she hadn't expected it, and had to hold in her giggle as she turned to face her monitor again, because the second game was finally starting.

Practice had finally concluded at nine sharp. Solar, as always, was the second-to-last to leave, clicking through her settings panel when she heard the other heading out, muffled goodbyes and zipping bags, the glow of the lab slowly hollowing around her.

Minji was waiting by the doors when she got up, leaning against the doorway with her bag lazily hanging off her shoulder, a red jacket loosely on her arms, her hat facing forward today, black hair as straight as ever, flowing down her back.

She didn't say anything as they fell into step naturally, making their way through the doors of the building, the October air cutting straight through her jacket the second they swung shut behind them. It was sharper than last night, and she was already pulling her sleeves down further over her hands. The campus at night had it's own particular quiet, amber, unhurried, the kind of stillness that made everything feel so much further away than it was.

"Well, film group aside," Minji hums into the night air, "how's today been for you? Any weird comments from the creepo sorority girl?"

She snorted at that, keeping her eyes on the lamplights that pooled across the sidewalks, "I have been free of her weird ass for..." she trails off to pretend she's thinking in depth, "I don't know, maybe a few days," she shrugs finally, "it's been fine," she finishes, though the words sounded a bit flatter than usual.

Minji seemed to notice, but didn't push her, she just nodded, hands in the pockets of her red jacket, breath fogging faintly in the cold, and let the silence sit between them in the easy way she always did. It was one of those things she'd stopped noticing and then started noticing again, the fact that Minji never made her fill the quiet.

"Practice was good, though," Minji offers after a few seconds.

"Yeah," she agrees, before leaving it at that.

Their sneakers fell back into that rhythm against the concrete, and Solar let herself not say anything else. The noise in her chest was returning now that the headset was off and the lab was behind her, slow and creeping, the way it always did when she ran out of things to occupy her hands with.

Her mind wanders, then, to a certain party happening no Friday, before she swore to herself that she was totally not conjuring any kind of plan.

Totally not.

35: a personal invite

Haechan and Mark had not spoken much since the last Sigma Chi party.

Not because they were deliberately avoiding each other, but because they each felt there wasn't much for them to say, that it would get ugly too quickly before either of them could stop it. Haechan was, quite frankly, unbothered by the entire thing, he remained seemingly level headed, going about his days like he had no cares in the world. Though, it was becoming subtly clear that he wasn't being seen with girls anymore, he was lessening the amount of drugs he'd typically do. He was becoming less Haechan, and Mark saw him around the house often enough to pick up on it.

It was Thursday morning when Haechan lazily padded into the tiled kitchen. Renjun was there already, sitting at the island, eating a bowl of some weird protein cereal he simply claimed was the right choice. The other presence in the room, Haechan hadn't noticed only until he'd closed the fridge after grabbing a water and spun around to say something to Renjun, only to see Mark Lee standing there on the opposite side of the kitchen, trying to make it less obvious that he was blatantly glaring at him.

"No need to hold in the words, Marky," Haechan deadpans, unimpressed, "might as well let it out."

Mark crossed his arms over his chest, caramel hair falling in his eyes, "nothing, man, just hoping your being careful, is all, with Jaehyun on your radar."

"Mm," Haechan returns, casual, "could say the same to you."

The words seemed to catch Mark off guard, realizing he had no idea what that meant coming from Haechan, other than one thing. A flash of realization spread across his expression, before he wiped it away and rolled his eyes, "I'm gonna assume your bullshitting, Hyuck."

Renjun, who was simply trying to finish his cereal so he could go sit outside and have his morning smoke, annoyedly looked up at the two boys, "is this really the conversation to have at 8 in the morning?"

"Apparently," Haechan hums, "poor Marky looks like he's about to cry."

Mark was growing increasingly irritated with his best friend's antics, and he shook his head as he made for the doorway, deciding he needed to excuse himself from the situation. "Not gonna end well for you, Haechannie," he mutters first, before stopping right by the doorway, turning to face him again. "She'll be here Friday," he informs, like it gave him the upper hand, "so don't bother her."

Haechan had been drinking his water, the plastic crinkling under his fingertips as he twists the bottle cap back on. It hit the counter with a clack, and he rose a brow as he met Mark's eyes, monotoning, "I know, Marky, I'm the one who invited her."

"Good Lord," he could hear Renjun mumble under his breath, knowing he hadn't expected Haechan to even say such a thing. He realized, to himself, that this was truly feeling like the worst version of deja vu.

-

haechanah:\ sigma fri

haechanah:\ you'll be there

sollee:\ if that's your way of sending an invite u failed

sollee:\ maybe try actually asking

haechanah:\ no need

haechanah:\ i know you'll come

sollee:\ well now i dont want to

haechanah:\ so you wanted to, didn't you, sweetheart?

sollee:\ no

haechanah:\ mhm

sollee:\ something is wrong with u

sollee:\ btw

haechanah:\ you clearly pay a lot of attention to even notice, huh

sollee:\ yeah im not coming

sollee:\ fuck u

haechanah:\ see you friday, princess

sollee:\ fine

sollee:\ i was going to come either way

sollee:\ just so u know

Solar had chewed down on her bottom lip rather angrily that evening, looking up at her ceiling, as if it would have the answers she was seeking written across it.

It did not.

She didn't know why Haechan Lee, the campus heart throb, would bother inviting her to the party that she probably would've gone to either way, just to secretly keep spiting her brother. Nonetheless, she was sure Mark would be inviting her again, either way. She wondered, if it was just another poke for him to get at Mark.

But she knew it had to be more than that, the way they'd sat on that rooftop Monday night was not something you did just to get back at your friend. A part of her almost felt bad, like she was doing something bad, like she was hurting Mark, but then she thought about it, about the weird hints, every word Haechan said regarding his friend and everything that seemed to be buried underneath them.

Still, she figured she owed him the courtesy of letting him know she would be there, since they were still friends at the end of the day.

sollee:\ hey mark

sollee:\ im coming to sig fri

sollee:\ my brother pissed me off

markylee:\ lolll well no complaints from me

markylee:\ bodyguard mark lee's always happy to be on duty

markylee:\ whatd he do this time??

sollee:\ just opened his mouth tbh

sollee:\ texting me some fake nice shit

sollee:\ Ik it was just an empty threat

sollee:\ so im gonna have fun and he can go fuck himself

markylee:\ nothing wrong with that

markylee:\ thats the perfect mindset for a sigma chi party

sollee:\ ikrrr!!!!!

sollee:\ and im rly stressed with the tourney coming up so fast

markylee:\ ya its next wk right?

markylee:\ feels like time flies

sollee:\ i know

sollee:\ crazy that its october already

sollee:\ ull come to the tourney right?

markylee:\ ofc haha i would be a jerk to miss it

sollee:\ u better

sollee:\ ill see u fri mark

markylee:\ meet u at the side entrance

sollee:\ sounds like a plan

There was, surprisingly, a grin playing on her lips as she set her phone back down. Climbing off her bed, she made her way to her bedroom door, deciding to go break the news to Hyein and Hanni that they would be attending yet another Sigma Chi party this week. From what she saw, the two of them seemed to have a pretty good time hanging out with Mark and Chenle, after all.

She opened the door, heading out into the lounge area of the second floor. Her feet pad against the light pink carpet, and she was about to knock on Hanni and Hyein's door, as the two shared a room together, when she noticed a certain someone standing there in the middle of the hall, arms crossed over her chest. She wasn't trying to hide the fact she was watching Solar, she was just standing there, peering at her with that creepy grin on her lips.

So, her hand falls back to her side, and she turns to meet Danielle's gaze, "Danielle," she deadpans, "does there happen to be a reason you're standing there staring at me?"

The girl shrugs, her expression suddenly becoming that of a casual one, flipping her brunette hair over her shoulder. "Hm, just thinking about how fun Friday's going to be," she exhales, longingly, "aren't you?"

She didn't know where to start with that, she decided to assume that Danielle was trying to be clever with guessing she was going to the party, and she just so happened to be right this time.

"Uhuh," she dryly returns, "sounds like you'll be there then, too, what a joy."

However, Danielle never seemed to be phased by her retorts. She just smiles, one that full of no sense of genuineness, "Of course, I like to keep an eye out for Jaehyun, you know," she beams, "in case anything seems odd."

"Did he ask you to do that?" Solar snorts, "Or is that your excuse to be a creep?"

"Solar," she hisses, shaking her head in disappointment, "that's not how we speak to our sorority sisters." She thought, that surely this girl was bothered at the fact she was seeing through her antics, that she was too scared to say anything else. But Danielle plastered that fake smile back on her lips like nothing in the world was wrong, "Just helping out, like Chungha always says to do." Then, she spun on her heel, like she had only ever been there due to coincidence, calling over her shoulder, "You should try it sometime, Sol, your brother really is trying to look out for you."

And then she was gone, down the stairs, back to whatever someone as weird as her did in her free time. Solar listened to the click of her shoes against the staircase, pursing her lips as she ran over what she'd just heard in her head a few times.

How did she always know? She found herself wondering, suddenly growing the urge to itch at her skin, like there was something under it she desperately needed to rip out.

"It's fine," she assures herself under her breath, turning to face the door again, "she's just a fucking weirdo, that's it. A weirdo obsessed with your brother."
