At the very least, it was very clear that Danielle had issues. Secondly, it was deeply clear that she had to be stalking her and her brother. She found herself thinking, maybe she should ask Jaehyun herself.

Though, what did she really care?

Shaking it off, she lets out a deep exhale, raising her fist again to knock on the door of her friends, knowing that if anyone would understand, it would be them. She couldn't let Danielle bother her, that was what she wanted, and she also had to talk to Hanni and Hyein before heading to eSports practice in approximately thirty minutes.

Seconds later, the site of Hanni met her eyes as the short girl had swung open the door, brown eyes glowing at the sight of her beloved friend. "Solar, I thought that was you I heard," she ushers the girl inside, shutting the door behind her hurriedly, "was that Danielle?"

"Yeah," she mutters as she falls onto the edge of the bed, "being creepy again."

Hanni shakes her head, padding across the carpet to sit next to Solar on the mattress. "I don't know what her problem is," she ponders, "I know she likes Jaehyun, but the way she's watching you is... very abnormal."

"Maybe we should tell someone," Hyein pipes up, she'd been sitting at the desk with her nose in a textbook she wasn't actually reading, spinning around in the chair, "get her in trouble, you know? Taste of her own medicine."

"Nah," Solar sighs, "it's not worth it. I don't need anymore spotlight."

"Fair," Hanni agrees softly.

Laying back onto the mattress, Solar stares up at the ceiling, letting her thoughts run wild for a second. "I might talk to my brother," she offers after a few moments of silence, "as much as I don't want to, she's getting too comfortable."

"Do you think they're friends?" Hyein asks no one in particular.

Hanni crossed one of her legs over the other, leaning forward and tapping her chin with her finger in thought. "I don't know, I don't think I've ever seen them interact," she voices, "I've only ever seen Jaehyun around the frat boys or his football teammates."

Solar bitterly snorted, "yeah, sounds about right."

"Do whatever feels right," Hanni decides confidently, placing a calming hand on Solar's knee to try and ease her nerves, "she's just messing with you on purpose, don't let her win."

"I'm not," she groans, sitting up again abruptly to peer at her friends, "but, I do come with news," she pauses for impact, and the two girls look at her expectantly.

"Guess who's going to another Sigma party tomorrow night?!"

"Not us, right?" Hanni tries.

"Definitely us!" Hyein practically screamed, pumping her fists in the air as if this was the best news she'd ever received.

"I wasn't going to," Solar explains, chuckling at the excitement of her friend and the differing reactions from the two, "but Jaehyun annoyed me, and..." she cut herself off, realizing the reality of what she was about to say. The two stared at her, waiting for her to continue.

"Haechan invited me," she finishes, awkwardly ripping her gaze away from their faces, because she knew the overreacting was about to happen.

"What?!" Hanni gawked.

"Oh my God!" Hyein squeaked, "he's like... never done that!"

"Shush!" Solar lectured in a pleading tone, "the stalker will hear you!"

"Right," Hanni nodded, bringing her voice down to a whisper, "he really seems to like you, Solar."

"Yeah, that's not what I wanted to hear."

"Holy shit, Mark is going to kill him!" Hyein was still speaking rather loud, earning an irritated look from the both of them and causing her to place a hand over her mouth, speaking muffled, "God forbid a girl is excited."

"It's not like that," Solar exhaled, "it's just to have fun, okay? That's the point."

Neither of them believed a word she was saying.

"Sure, Sol," Hanni hums, patting the girl's back comfortingly, "we believe you."

"Psst, yeah right," Hyein scoffed.

She was fine with that.

36: all the wrong reasons

*NEW POST\ \@jen0l33*

![\@jen0l33: ALREADY LIT AF SIGMA NEVER SLEEPS TRY TO GET IN IF U DESIRE](media/image13.jpg){width="6.5in" height="8.625in"}

*\@jen0l33: ALREADY LIT AF SIGMA NEVER SLEEPS TRY TO GET IN IF U DESIRE*

*-*

*There was a different flavor in the air of Sigma Chi, and it shockingly wasn't Jaemin's newest vape flavor of the week. The house, as always, was bustling with the pre-party chaos, a few people that were always early stumbling in, getting it going. Jaemin was running around, making sure everything was going as it should, and making the rounds, saying hello here and there, getting new supply from a few certain individuals. Jeno, on the other hand, was doing somewhat of the same thing, already several drinks in and one molly deep, he had that big, goofy grin on his lips as he was already surrounded by a group of girls in the corner, saying something to them that was clearly* working.

Renjun was standing in the doorway of the kitchen with Chenle, smoking a blunt he'd rolled for the occasion, and watching the chaos begin. The kitchen had quickly become his safe zone, he was never exactly someone who enjoyed the party scene, he did not want to be touched by anyone nonetheless smell their sweat. But, he did enjoy getting high, doing drugs, and watching people make terrible decisions. It was a great source of entertainment for him, like watching a movie in real time.

Chenle was leaning there, drinking a soda and pretending he had no interest in any of the drama. In reality, he did, because how could you not?

The two of them were watching Mark, who was lingering by the backdoor, presumably preparing himself to go outside and let Solar and her friends in through the side gate, just like he did last week. Renjun had watched him then, too, the way he nervously shifted his weight from foot to foot, checking his phone every few seconds. A girl tried to talk to him, but he sent her that awkward smile before likely muttering something about how he's waiting for someone and didn't have time right now.

"He's embarrassing," Chenle observes after a few seconds, growing tired of watching their fellow fraternity member make a complete fool of himself, "does he really have no idea how stupid he looks?"

"Has he ever?" Renjun retorted, not unkindly, but bluntly. "He sure likes her, doesn't he," he didn't frame it as a question, in fact, it was more sarcastic, "for all of the wrong reasons."

"Some of it's genuine," Chenle hums, a rather optimistic sentiment from him which was rare, "the way he speaks, there must be some level of real in it, it's bad, Renjun."

"Right, but Jaehyun's little sister?" Renjun snorts, shaking his head, taking another long drag of the blunt before blowing the smoke into the air. The music was playing now, though it wasn't quite loud enough to cover the sound of voices yet. "It's morbid, really, you'd think he'd have learned his lesson."

"Hyuck told Jaehyun," Chenle adds, "about Mark."

"I heard," Renjun nods, "it's curious he hasn't done anything though."

"He's got eyes everywhere," the blonde boy monotones.

The casual conversation faded, because the two boys noticed a certain someone coming down the steps. Haechan hadn't been very present at the recent Sigma parties the way everyone was used to him being. After all, his entire thing was how hard he liked to party. People had grown confused at his abrupt absence from them, but no one ever really had the guts to ask him about it, if he would even answer a random.

Which he wouldn't. Haechan didn't offer his words to just anyone, it was like you had to be special, a certain breed to allow for him to talk to you.

He reaches the bottom of the steps, running a hand through his dark hair, brown eyes raking across the party scene that was already unfolding. He was looking for something, or rather, someone, with the way he pursed his lips in concentration, beginning to walk through the living room area.

Meanwhile, Mark had been looking down at his phone as he stepped out into the evening air. Solar was apparently arriving rather early this time, which wasn't a problem in his eyes. That goofy smile was across his lips as he padded across the green grass of Sigma Chi, wind blowing through his caramel hair. Within a minute, he reached that same side gate, where Solar, Hyein, and Hanni were standing.

"Welcome back," he offers as he swings the gate open. Hyein and Hanni brightly bid him their greetings, and Solar stopped for a second, meeting his eyes, before she sent him a small smile and made her way through the gate.

The two friends walked ahead of Mark and Solar. Mark had reached out, instinctively, placing a hand on her shoulder as if he was a football coach, "good to see you, Sol."

She snorted before she could stop herself, shaking her head at how nerdy he was, "gee, thanks coach," she deadpans, and he seemed to grow nervous from her calling his awkwardness out, as he nervously smiled, eyes diverting to the grass.

Mark was actually finding it harder to be normal due to the sight of the girl before him. Solar was never ugly, but tonight, she nearly looked like an entirely new person. Her dress was hugging her sides, so closely you could see every curve. It was enough to make him clear his throat, trying to look at anything other than her as they made their way across the grass.

"Sorry," he blurts after a moment, "you just look..." He turned to face her again as they walked, but the words wouldn't seem to come out.

Still, she got the memo, as she chuckled, "thanks, Mark, message received."

His cheeks were slightly pink as he looked ahead again, muttering, "Man, I need a drink."

They reach the pool area of the backyard, which shockingly was much less crowded than it was last time, which she guessed was due to how early they'd been. Her eyes scan the area, but she didn't see Hyein or Hanni at the same table. Mark shoved his hands in his pockets, following her gaze before he hums, "they probably went inside, not many people want to hang out in the cold."

As if on queue, a slight breeze blows across both of their skins. Solar shivers due to the fact she was wearing a dress with nothing over it, and nods in agreement, "yeah, I don't blame them, let's go inside."

She didn't want to think about the last time she'd stepped foot in Sigma Chi, but she was going to anyway. She could still feel the soft graze of Haechan's hand in hers as they walked through the house back outside, the way the drunken girls loudly gawked at them like they were witnessing a murder in real time. Her teeth instinctively chewed on her bottom lip as her and Mark enter the house through the back doors, the music already sinking into her eardrums like bad news.

Though there weren't as many people as her first couple of encounters at a Sigma party, it was clearly starting to pile up, despite how early it was. Mark's hand went to her upper back, though he didn't touch her, just to make sure he didn't lose her in the mess of bodies.

Mark leads them to a corner of the living room where it was quieter. There were a couple crowds, but he'd gone over to them, leaning over their shoulders to utter something, and they'd glanced at him before leaving the area. Solar didn't say anything to that, she witnessed it and thought, well, it is his house, I guess, and moved on. The music felt too loud in her ears, and the scent of marijuana was already beginning to give her a headache.

She had to remind herself that she was here for a reason, she wanted to have fun, even if her friends had disappeared.

Don't think about Haechan.

But he was the one who invited her, wasn't he?

"Same as last time?" she'd been jolted out of her thoughts as Mark was standing in front of her again, looking down at her from his slightly taller height, raising a brow in question.

It took her a second to recall he was talking about drinks. "Yeah," she nods, "that'd be good, but make it stronger."

He chuckled, playfully rolling his eyes, but this time he didn't try to tell her what she should or shouldn't do. Instead, he just salutes her, "got it, be right back, okay? I'm sure Hanni and Hyein are here somewhere, you want me to try to find them?"

His thoughtfulness made her smile, she shook her head, assuringly, "nah, it's okay, I'll find them later."

Mark had nodded in agreement, before assuring her he would be quick. She'd watched him disappear into the crowd of sweaty bodies, thinking about the way he always had that same goofy grin across his lips like everything was perfect. But he was an entirely different person the second he looked at the boy who was supposed to be his best friend.

She had to try very hard to not think about that scholarship.

Solar tried to distract herself by swaying along to the music, letting her hips do the talking, bouncing her head a little. Though some may think she looked like an idiot, she was trying to loosen up, relax a little bit after everything, after Danielle, after the rooftop. She needed a moment to simply exist and enjoy her life.

Like a bad habit, her eyes can't help but rake across the bodies filling the living area. It was hard to really make out faces due to the dimmed lighting, casting a purple glow across the room. Every few seconds, you'd hear screams over the booming music, people cheering each other on for various reasons, though most were related to drinking. She spotted Jeno and Jaemin first, as they were the two who were quite difficult to miss with the way they stood out entirely on purpose. Jeno already had his shirt off and had thrown it to a girl, who had cheered like she was winning an award, as he climbed on the couch and began to do circular hip motions.

It was a rather painful sight.

Tearing her eyes away, she looks around more, wondering where Hanni and Hyein had ran off to and why they didn't say anything to her. It didn't take a genius to decipher they'd done it on purpose, those two had a major obsession with her love life, after all. She figured they were somewhere with Chenle, maybe upstairs away from the chaos since they weren't outside.

The thought of upstairs made a lump form in her throat.

Something suddenly told her to look at the left side of the room, she didn't know what, as she was simply standing there by herself in a corner, but she couldn't seem to fight the urge. Her eyes landed on nothing out of the ordinary, at first, it was just a bunch of girls, crowding around something. They looked like they were admiring, almost, and she guessed Jeno had to be in the middle of it, since everyone seemed to fawn over him so much.

Though, one of the girls had shifted the way she was standing, allowing Solar to see a clearer view of who was actually in the middle.

Haechan.

The lump in her throat grew by a few sizes. As badly as her eyes wanted to look away, she couldn't get them to. There was something about the sight of him, the way his leather jacket was hanging loosely off one of his shoulders, the shimmer of the silver chain around his neck under the lights, the way his hair was middle parted to perfectly frame his jawline. It was criminal, the way he seemed to always fit the perfect part, and it suddenly began to make far too much sense as to why people said what they said about him.

He wasn't smiling, not in the slightest. His face was completely blank, like he had no interest in what was happening around him, but his mouth would move every few seconds, like he was responding to whatever the girls were asking him. Despite the crowd, he kept his distance, like he'd be sick if any of them actually touched him.

"Delivery!"

She nearly jumped, clearing her throat and snapping her head to meet Mark's eyes, hoping he had not noticed what she had been paying attention two only seconds prior. In fact, she hoped no one noticed, because it wasn't her finest moment by any means.

She grins as she takes the cold drink from his hands, "thank you," she offers over the music, "I need this after this week."

Mark leaned against the wall next to her, sipping his own drink, "Jaehyun?"

"More than that," she sighs, "him and Danielle."

And the rooftop on Monday night with your best friend.

But she wasn't going to say that.

"Still?" he asks, concerned, before taking another drink of his alcohol, "she still on about your brother?"

"Uhuh," she grimaces, "I'm like... a hundred percent positive she's stalking me, Mark, seriously."

Her words seemed to catch him off guard, and he sat there, thinking for a moment. "You know, she's always kinda hung around the frat," he recalls, "like, lingering wherever Jaehyun is."

"Have you ever seen them interact?" she questioned then. Mark had a lot more information on this than she did, after all.

The caramel haired boy had to ponder that for a second.

"Maybe," he decides, just before the music had taken over her brain again, "don't think he actually cares for her, but she's definitely tried."

Solar pursed her lips, imagining the sight of someone like Danielle attempting to talk to her brother, of all people. It almost made her laugh then, there was no way her brother would ever give someone like her a chance. A part of her almost felt sad for her, before she remembered the type of person she'd shown herself to be.

Mark was thinking about it, too, she could tell from the slight crease that formed between his brows, the way he picked at the side of his cup like he was trying to remember something specific. "She's always at his games," he adds, which was a detail she hadn't quite expected from him, though it made her think back to Danielle's comment in the kitchen about Jaehyun missing his game, "like, I'd see her there but never thought too much of it, ya know. She's easy to ignore, I guess."

A laugh left her lips from the way he dismissed her existence, she appreciated him catering to her. She could tell he had more to say, but he'd stopped himself. Her eyes land on him, but he was looking down at his cup.

"And?" she hums, expectantly.

After a second of staring down at his drink, Mark looked up to her, meeting her eyes with a shrug, "nah, it's probably nothing more than a creepy crush, Jaehyun is like the most popular guy on campus."

He'd seemingly talked himself out of whatever else he was going to say, and Solar didn't really have the energy to press it. So, she nods, wrinkling her nose, "don't remind me."

The two continued to talk about random things, Solar finished her drink and focused on chewing on the ice cubes from the cup instead. She was listening to Mark tell some kind of story about the last time Jaehyun was actually at a Sigma party, when she noticed his eyes trailing away from her and focusing on something across the room.

Little did she know the boy that was making his way towards them. His eyes had landed on Solar and without a second thought, he'd pushed past the girls that were surrounding them, abruptly deciding he was on a mission. Some of them tried to call out to him, but they were met with no reply.

He knew Mark was staring at him, but he wasn't looking at Mark.

"Look who made it."

Solar's head whipped up to see Haechan standing there before her, the lump returning to her throat and causing her to cough a few times, regaining her composure. She could feel the lasers from Mark next to her, and she tried to decipher what the best course of action was right now, thinking back what happened last week.

He was looking at her, not at Mark, not at the room, like the rest of Sigma Chi was just the thing surrounding the specific point he'd been trying to reach. "You told me to," she offers finally.

"Haechan," Mark's voice warned from beside her, measured, carefully leveled, and he'd straightened slightly from where he was once casually leaning against the wall, "didn't see you come downstairs."

"Mm," Haechan spared him no more than a mere glance, barely an acknowledgment, before his attention returned to Solar, unhurried, with that particular steadiness she'd learned to recognize as something very distinct from disinterest. "So," he monotones, "you do know how to listen, sweetheart."

She put another ice cube in her mouth to ease her anxiety, and to try to not focus on the way he was staring down at her, the way Mark looked ready to burn the house down, the way that same group of girls were angrily whispering to each other while watching with creased brows.

"Generally," she returns, "not to you, though."

Her retort had come out before she could help it, the dry shorthand that existed between them and no one else, and she could feel Mark shifting beside her, the way he always did when whatever it was between her and Haechan became even slightly visible and harder to ignore. She thought about pulling the conversation back, including him, making the awkward triangle feel more like what it was meant to be.

But she didn't.

Haechan's eyes moved briefly over the room, something passing through them she almost didn't catch, and then he looked back at her and didn't say anything, which was typical with him, the silence not absence, but more like a held note.

Mark cleared his throat, "I'll go get us more drinks, Sol," he tells her confidently, before his phone had buzzed in his pocket. A subtle frown took over his lips as he pulled it out.

Though, in that same moment, Jaemin had come out of the crowd somewhere behind them. His eyes were wider than usual, pupils dilated from whatever drug he was on that night. His hand flew to Mark's arm like he'd been specifically looking for him, "Haechan, Mark," he offers to the both of the frat boys, "either of you got a minute? Jeno is... doing that... again, you know how he gets," he'd censored whatever it was, because apparently it was confidential information. His eyes landed on Solar for a split second, and he flashed that pearly smile, "sorry, frat situation, it happens."

He was clearly expecting one of them to follow him, but Haechan didn't look like he was planning to move. Knowing exactly what he was doing, Mark sighed, running a hand down his face in stress as he muttered, "alright, I'll go," he turned to Solar, that soft expression falling on his face again, "don't go anywhere, okay? I'll be back."

She just sent him a nod and a reassuring smile, trying to show him that the world wasn't going to end if she stood here with his best friend.

Even though she wasn't entirely sure of that herself.

Mark held her eyes for one second longer than he needed to, which was enough to tell her his instruction hadn't been meant for only her. Then, he was gone, swallowed by the crowd behind Jaemin, and the space he left felt both emptier and fuller than it should've.

The music had gotten louder since they'd been standing there, or maybe it'd always been this loud and she'd only just let herself focus on it. The bass was pressing against the inside of her sternum in a way that felt increasingly unignorable. She tried to remind herself that she was meant to have fun, though she couldn't help but think she'd need more than one drink to do such.

"You hate parties," Haechan observes dryly from beside her, eyes having scanned her face enough times to feel positive about such a conclusion.

"I don't," she corrects him sternly, though her eyes were still staring down at her cup.

"You hate this party," he asserts.

"Maybe I hate you," she retorts, lifting her head up to meet his dark eyes.

"Mhm," he chuckled, not believing a single word, in the tone that meant he'd filed something away and wasn't going to say anything else about it. He was already looking at her when she met his eyes, he was always already looking.

"The noise hurts my head sometimes," she exhales, "I'm guessing that's why everyone immediately gets shit faced the second they enter this house."

The corners of his lips tugged, and he shook his head in disbelief, "does your head hurt now?"

She knew why he was asking.

And she couldn't seem to stop herself from giving in.

"A little."

That seemed to be all he'd needed, as he'd spun on his heel. "Come on," as if the decision had already been made, like he knew everything without saying a single word.

The lump in her throat never fully went away whenever she was near him.

After a second of debating with herself, she reminded herself as to why she was here.

And then she followed him.

37: no coming back

The house was more crowded now, the kind of crowded where moving through it meant accepting that you'd be touching countless amounts of strangers. Solar kept close behind Haechan's shoulder as they moved through the living room towards the stairs, trying to not pay attention to the eyes that were following them all too knowingly. She could feel the way people were looking, though most were likely too drunk to remember it in the morning, or maybe she was aware that nothing in this house ever went unnoticed for long. Perhaps, she'd decided, somewhere between standing next to Mark and following Haechan through the crowd, that she didn't care anymore.

The staircase was loud at the bottom and quieter at every step above it, and by the time they reached the second floor the bass had compressed into something she felt in her bones but no longer had to shout over, and she exhaled without quite planning to.

She hated the way she remembered so much about the layout of the upstairs, despite how she had only ever been up here drunk. Right then, she was entirely sober, it was like she'd drank nothing as they'd reached the top floor.

His room looked different now that she was sober.

The scent of vanilla was strong as she entered behind him, he'd pushed open his door casually, stepping back enough to let her through, and she'd crossed the threshold and padded her way across the carpet to fall into that same gaming chair she'd sat in and drunkenly rambled from that night. Through the floor, the boom of the bass continued, almost loud enough to shake the floorboards, and she focused on the pulse of the music on the pads of her feet, trying not to look to the boy that fell onto the edge of his bed, peering at her like he was thinking for once.

She didn't know what to say, for some reason. By now, she thought, it should be easier to talk to him, but the words didn't seem to flow now that she was entirely sober.

Where things different now, after the rooftop, she wondered, or were they supposed to act like it never happened?

"Well, spit it out," she exhales, deciding there was no use in beating around the bush, "I already know you're going to make fun of me for following you up here."

"I wasn't planning to do such a thing, princess."

He'd said it as if she was crazy for thinking such a thing, causing her to scoff at him, "really? I didn't think you'd be able to help yourself."

"I invited you, sweetheart," he monotones, as if that should answer everything.

Her eyes broke away from his, because the sentence felt too heavy in her chest. Instead, they trail over to the single window in his room, the moonlight casting that same yellow through the glass panels. Outside, she could briefly see a the shadow of a streetlight down below. Underneath their feet, you could hear the yells and cheers of the party downstairs, people jumping around, screaming the lyrics to whatever song was playing, having the time of their lives.

The silence between them was the kind that could go various ways. Pressing her lips together, she swivels slightly in his gaming chair, looking around the room the way she always did when she didn't know what else to do with her eyes or her hands. The monitor glow caught the edge of the things she wouldn't notice if she wasn't sober, a half open notebook on the desk, his letterman jacket draped over something in the corner, a cable she kept almost putting her foot through. Shockingly, his bed was made, though his desk was the definition of a mess.

She turns to face the PC setup this time, imagining the sight of him sitting here during late nights, queuing a game nobody really knew he even played that he was genuinely skilled at. When he'd appeared in her match and she'd thought, there is no way, God is punishing me, before realizing someone like him had other talents than drugs and women.

Drugs and women, she repeated the words in her head, pondering the way he'd shrugged those girls off in utter disgust. Maybe she'd seen him smoke, but other than that, he seemed tame compared to the things she saw some of the other frat boys do.

Maybe it was time she stopped using that stereotype. It didn't make her any better than those girls on campus spreading rumors about her love life, did it?

Her eyes trail to the desk, where next to his keyboard that was pushed aside, sat musical equipment. An audio interface, the kind she didn't know the name of but recognized instinctively from the knobs and inputs, with a pair of headphones sitting beside it that looked much more expensive than a gaming headset and a MIDI keyboard pushed to the other side, towards where the PC sat.

There was no logical reason as to why her hands reached out for those headphones. Solar would tell herself, that she was someone who was attracted to things that looked expensive, it made sense when you looked at her closet, after all. Perhaps a part of her was still thinking about the way he'd said life's never fair for those who deserve it the most before telling her she could still pursue something if it's what she wanted to do, he'd said it in his own way, indirect but genuine, reminding her that she still had the power to do what she aspired to do at the end of the day. It was a different flavor from Mark, from when he'd heard her humming and chuckled that they could go viral if she was on a song with him, there wasn't as much passion behind it, the feeling was more of a desire.

The cushions felt soft under her fingertips, smug against her ears as she placed them on her head. There's a devious, maybe part shy, smile playing on her lips. She could feel his eyes on her, confirmed when his observation hovers through the air, "those are calibrated," he said it like it was a boring fact.

"I won't break them," she assures him, turning to meet his eyes a single time before looking back at the equipment. Her eyes scan across the keyboard, trying to rack her brain for what to do. She was in choir, though, she'd never actually played an instrument, if this even counted as one.

"I know," she hears that low voice due to the fact one of the headphones was half off her ear, just in case, but nearly jumped out of the chair from how close he suddenly was. Solar had no idea when he'd moved from the mattress, but she tried to pretend his sudden presence didn't bother her.

There was nothing playing through the headphones, which she should've expected, but the silence inside them was a different texture than she silence in the room, dense and close, the muffled bass from the party below compressing into something further and further away.

The scent of vanilla was suddenly becoming overwhelmingly strong. Haechan leaned in from beside her, past her shoulder to click something on the laptop that was opened in front of his PC monitor, so close that the scent reached her before her brain actually registered him moving. He pulled one ear of the headphones off her head with two fingers, "don't say anything yet," before letting it settle back.

She just nodded, feeling that lump in her throat too enormously large to utter any words. All she could do was watch as he clicked through a few more applications, not having the slightest clue what she was looking at, before a track began to play in her ears.

Solar wasn't prepared for what it sounded like. Haechan talked a big game about everything, it seemed, he was incredibly, painfully full of himself. He was oddly decent at Overwatch, he somehow always knew what was happening in class even when she wanted to believe he was an idiot, was it really possible that he was going to be good at this, too?

Though, maybe that was a stupid question to ask.

The track wasn't finished, she could tell, it wasn't as polished as she was subconsciously expecting. It had exposed bones, structural, the way a building under construction was more honest than the finished thing. There was a melody, underneath it, that did something to her chest she couldn't name, as if he'd located a frequency she didn't know she'd had and tuned into it without asking.

She swore, just barely, you could hear the slightest background vocals. It was enough for her to bite down on her lip again, a terrible habit she was afraid would never be going away. All she could seem to imagine was him, sitting here in his room, playing this keyboard and creating something he said he'd lost his spark for.

It felt intimate in a way she couldn't explain. Her lips form a straight line as she slowly pulls the headphones from her ears to around her neck. "It's..." she trails off, trying to think of the right words to say then, how to sound like she had the slightest clue what was happening.

"No need," he cuts in, not unkindly yet blunt.

"I wasn't going to say anything bad," she defends, the slightest pout playing on her lips. Her guard was coming down, and she couldn't seem to stop it. Suddenly, she wasn't immediately only thinking of retorts or insults, it was like she'd walked through a new door, into a world where Haechan wasn't a self absorbed, pretentious, asshole, and she could genuinely view him as a person without feeling so irritated all of the time.

Even if it wasn't really irritation she was feeling.

"You were going to say something," he hums, the corners of his lips twitching, "you don't have to pretend you have the words."

She looks at him sideways then, he was still close, bending down to mess with his laptop that was, technically, between them. Those brown eyes were fixated on the screen rather than her, which was the only reason she allowed herself to look directly. His jaw was loose, hair fallen forward, he looked like himself, the version she was starting to think was the real one, the one that existed when he stopped performing Haechan for an audience.

"Well, I distinctively remember you saying you'd help me," she teasingly thinks aloud, trying to mask the nerves underneath the words then, like she was putting it down rather than really saying it.

Still, he didn't look at her, he just kept clicking away on that laptop. HIs jawline was sharp, she noticed, the way it curved at the perfect angle, the way the bridge was shaped in a way people paid to achieve. His lashes were long, fluttering, eyes sparkling from the glow of the moonlight through the window, his honey toned skin looked soft to the touch, like he was careful with it, he looked like a man with a several step skincare routine he refused to skip, no matter what state he was in.

She was growing increasingly embarrassed of herself.

"I said I was the best around," he monotones after a moment, still not looking at her.

"Don't act like that was an implication to help me," she scoffs, displeased. In reality, she was having to tear her eyes away from looking at the flexing of his biceps as he leaned over her to reach the trackpad of the laptop, the way the veins in his arms popped out at the worst moments.

"Did it?"

"You didn't say no," she hums.

He was quiet for a second, like he was rolling over something in his head. Then, he made a ts sound, before clicking again and again, until she heard the faint melody of the track looping in the headphones from the top.

The sound distracted her for a second, that she didn't notice the tilt of his head for him to meet her eyes. She resisted the urge to cough in his face from shock, the realization that he was only inches away from her face. "Sing something then," he commands, though it wasn't harsh.

The words landed in the pit of her stomach. A part of her didn't know if it was nausea from eating something bad or if Haechan genuinely made her nervous.

"No," she automatically retorts, "are you crazy?"

He chuckled then, shaking his head, black fringe falling in his face as whatever styling gel he'd used to middle part it began to give out. "Alright."

"I can't just sing," she mutters, feeling slightly humiliated, "it's not that easy."

"Sure," he nods in agreement, his arm reaching across her again to close the laptop. The music in the headphones abruptly cut off, the room growing darker by exactly one monitor's worth, the bass from below becoming the loudest sound in the room once again. "It doesn't have to be anything," he offers after a moment of silence sat, "I'm not recording you."

It was almost sweet that he felt the need to reassure her of that, due to the fact it felt like her life was constantly under surveillance. Still, she pursed her lips, "I know you're not."

"Then?" he returned.

Her eyes trail to the window, the streetlights below were doing that same thing, amber and unbothered, and somewhere underneath her, the people continued to scream along to a song she didn't know. And she, was sitting here, in Haechan's gaming chair out of all places, trying to convince herself not to do the thing she was already going to do.

Solar, who was by most people's accounts, typically fairly full of herself, was for once, struggling to come out of her shell.

She closes her eyes, even though she couldn't see his expression that well anyway, exhaling through her nose a single time. She tries to take herself back, to standing in her choir class, belting out notes like it was her single purpose, back before she had turned to video games instead, and she sang. It was short lived, only a few words, the opening line of a song they used to sing in choir that she'd never been able to fully erase from her head, delivered quietly, barely above speaking, like if she did it quiet enough it wouldn't count.

The room felt very still when she opened her eyes again. She was afraid to look at him, instead, she waited for the dry remark, the raised brow, something with that ridiculous pet name in it that would let her put the armor back on and pretend the last ten minutes were a figment of her imagination.

But his eyes were pouring into hers with something beneath them she had never seen before. It was all he did, then, was look at her, only then did she notice the fact he was still standing there, next to the chair, just not leaning now since he'd closed the laptop. It wasn't with the steadiness he used as a shield, not with the particular blankness she'd learned to read like a second language, but with something buried so deep below she couldn't comprehend a word for it then, something that looked uncomfortably like the face someone made when something confirmed something they'd already believed.

"Yeah," he deadpans, finally, which was barely a sound.

"Don't," she warns.

"I didn't say anything."

"You were about to."

His mouth did something that wasn't quite a smile, "I was going to say yeah," he repeats, the same word, the same register, and she understood then that it wasn't exactly a compliment, but more like recognition, confirmation.

Her defense mechanisms were setting off thousands of alarms inside of her head then, telling her to get something ready, throw out a dry comment, something that would put the appropriate distance back between whatever just occurred and what may occur next. Solar's eyes dart back to the desk she was sitting by, trying to ponder what she could say, what the correct words were, if she should make an excuse or just run for it like a crazy person.

But she didn't do any of those things, because the sudden touch of his fingertips slightly grazing the skin of her neck was enough to send her into whiplash, his hands reaching over and gently pulling the headphones from around her neck and setting them back where they once were. One of his hands remained, trailing along the side of her jaw, barely there, like he was afraid to really touch her, like she would break. She could feel the calluses on his fingertips, the party below them continuing, indifferently, unaware of what was happening in this room.

She should look away, open her mouth and make a poor excuse, say that she felt bad for leaving Mark down there probably wondering where they'd run off to. The thought was brief, sensible, but not something she was going to do. She sat there, instead, with his hand at her jaw, the party below them, and the silence of a room that'd stopped needing words several minutes ago, and looked at him the way she hadn't let herself look at him all semester, full and direct, without the armor of irony sitting between them like a parent.

He was watching her, intently, with that same expression she couldn't put her finger on, perhaps it was patience, the particular patience of someone who was used to waiting for things to become true.

The angle of his head shifts, only slightly, a recalibration, not towards her yet in her direction. His thumb moved along her jaw, the barest drag of it, a question rather than a statement, and she felt it travel from her jaw down to her collarbone, the way a sensation sometimes moved, as if her body didn't know which part of itself was being touched.

Solar's breath was unsteady, shamefully. She hadn't meant for it to be that way, she watched the slight change in his eyes when he heard her trying to fix it, clearing her throat, something flashing through the brown irises, like he'd been given permission.

She was entirely too aware that he was coming closer to her.

Not quickly, in the way she may have expected it, if she'd allow herself to ever expect such a thing, but slowly, like he was giving her every chance to decide otherwise, the hand at her jaw barely guiding, his face dipping toward hers by degrees, and she could smell vanilla and yet, something else underneath, something that was just him, warm and close in the way proximity became overwhelming when you'd spent months trying to not notice someone.

They were close enough now that she could feel the warmth of his breath on her skin, the tingle it scent across her face, the way the air between them had stopped being quite air, and her fingers reached before that voice in her head could tell her not to, gripping the hem of his leather jacket without asking. Her head subconsciously tilted upward, allowing her to see him from a different angle she'd never quite imagined him from before, the long line of his lashes, the way his eyes had dropped to her lips and stayed there.

Her breath hitched in her throat, like the world was sending her one last reminder that this was a terrible idea.

Solar Lee loved terrible ideas.

His mouth pressed to hers in milliseconds, so careful it almost undid her entirely, right there in his black, leather gaming chair. That was the thing she had expected the least, that someone who moved through the world with that particular brand of assurance would kiss her like he was still asking a question. His mouth barely parted against hers, the hand at her jaw pressing with intention now, the calluses on his fingers at her skin, registering differently this time, something intimate, specific, real in a way she wasn't ready for and couldn't do anything about.

She kissed him back like she'd decided the world could go fuck itself.

Something loosened in her the moment she did, like a thing that'd been held at tension for too long finally releasing, the sound of the party beneath becoming more distant than ever. Her fingers tightened against the leather of his jacket, feeling his exhale against her cheek, long and quiet, through his nose, like he'd been carrying something for a while and had finally been able to set it down. His other hand found the arm rest of the gaming chair, not trapping her, simply making a suggestion, and she tilted her chin up further to close the last fraction of distance, the kiss deepening and the room rearranging itself to fit this new point of view.

There was no pretense left in it, no performance, she'd spent months attempting to decode what was real with him, peeling pack the layers of Haechan Lee the way you peeled back the layers of something that'd been wrapped up carefully to survive being handled by too many people. She'd tried so hard to play it off like she didn't care for him, like she hadn't thought about what it would feel like before to have him staring down at her like that, like she was still asking herself a question when she'd had the answer the first day he'd sat next to her in her Media class and proved to her that he was not the same boy everyone kept claiming him to be. Underneath, Haechan was unhurried, certain, like she was the only frequency he had ever successfully cared to tune into.

His thumb traced the line of her cheekbone. She could feel her own pulse in her throat, at her wrists, everywhere that was suddenly too aware of itself. And most of all, she could feel the feeling in her chest, the twist, the pull, the undeniable bliss that was sparking through her veins from the feeling of his lips moving against hers.

When he finally pulled back, he was still close, close enough that she could see the slight flush at the top of his cheekbones, the rise and fall of his chest, the way he was looking down at her like the confirmation had turned into something entirely different, something that he didn't have a clean word for.

Only then, did she process the fact her fingers were still gripping the hem of his leather jacket. But she made no effort to let go.

"You okay there, sweetheart?" he rose a brow at her, unable to keep himself from teasing.

The pet name tasted different in her mouth then. Her eyes narrow at him in annoyance, hand uncurling from his leather jacket, flattening it back against his chest. For a split second, she felt the slow thud of his heartbeat beneath her palm, faster than she would've expected, though she couldn't help but wonder how many times he had been in this position.

"I hate you," she deadpans with a sigh, because she didn't know what else to say, and she was afraid of what else there was to say if she allowed herself to actually think about it.

He'd taken a step back then, allowing her to rise to her feet. Her legs, however, felt a little more unstable than she'd planned. Trying not to show it, she recovers, though it was clear he'd witnessed her entirely sober near stumble from the smirk that was across his lips.

"You don't mean that," he hums, cockily, so cocky that she wanted so badly to run out of that room.

Instead, she stood there, only feet away from the door that would take her back into the lounge area of the second floor. She could go back downstairs, find Mark, pretend none of this ever happened.

She could do that.

In reality, that was the very last thing Solar wanted to do. Haechan was still standing there in the middle of his room, like he waiting for her to make a decision, to figure out what she was going to do. Even though, from that smirk, he already seemed to know it well.

"I don't," she whispers out once she was standing in front of him again, those brown eyes daringly peering into her own.

He didn't say anything in response, just turned his chin, leaning forward to kiss the corner of her mouth, then her cheek, then the edge of her jaw, following the line his hand had once made moments earlier. The difference between his mouth and fingers was something she felt in the back of her knees, and the exhale she let out stopped sounding controlled.

There was nothing careful about the way their eyes met again.

His hand slid from her jaw to the back of her neck, fingers pressing into her hair, lips slamming onto hers in an entirely different capacity, more consuming rather than urgent, like he'd decided something and was taking his time with the aftermath. His hand found her waist, fingertips pressing into the skin, pressing the fabric of her dress into her, before they slid, down until they were messing with the hem of the short dress that rested on her body. His other hand remained on the back of her neck, fingers running through her hair, his lips moving with the perfect intention of someone that had done this before, many times, and felt undeniably sure of their abilities.

He'd moved them in between, a few inches, enough for his leg to reach out and kick his bedroom door shut. The sudden sound of it closing was enough for her heart to skip, she could feel it dropping to her knees as her mind tried to catch up, tried to remain level headed.

He was deliberately careful, with the way he did everything. His hands only gently gripped her upper arms, guiding her towards the mattress, but their lips didn't break apart a single time. His tongue licked the bottom of her lip, demanding entrance rather than asking, and she allowed it without a second thought, their tongues dancing together in harmony. He tasted sweet, similar to the way he smelled, and she hated how much she liked it, the way his teeth grazed her bottom lip how she often did when she got too much anxiety, biting down enough to make her nearly whimper.

She hit the edge of the bed, the mattress sinking underneath her, and only then did he finally stand there before her, looking down at her, the way she'd never let herself imagine he looked at things, like he wanted something, like he'd imagined it before.

The bass from the floorboards continued to pulse through the mattress under her palms, the amber from the streetlight cut through the room into shadow and gold. She could hear her own breathing, humiliatingly so, which had stopped being something she was focused on managing.

He reached down and tucked a strand of hair back from her face, the same unhurried certainty in his fingertips. Her hand catches his wrist without planning to, holding it there, his pulse beat steadily against her fingers.

"Careful, princess," he coaxes lowly, "starting to look obvious."

It was such a deeply Haechan thing for him to say, she hated the way the nicknames flew off his tongue like he'd used them thousands of times before. And shamefully, she found herself wondering if that was true, if he smirked at other women and called them the same things, and how often he had done this game before, the banter battle that leads into something else. She was ashamed, at the fact it bothered her to think about.

So, she rolls her eyes, "shut the fuck up, Haechan."

His mouth curved at her sassy retort, and it seemed to be enough for him, because he leaned down, his knees hitting the side of the mattress, legs on either side of her, and kissed her again, slower this time, hands cupping her cheeks like he was holding the world's most delicate flower. Her fingers habitually grabbed back at his leather jacket, pulling, the careful distance that had existed between them for months collapsing entirely, as if it never existed in the first place.

Suddenly, there was no more reason behind anything, maybe there never was.

His tongue was in her mouth again, his saliva becoming one with her own like it belonged together. He'd gently pushed her to fall back onto the soft of the mattress, climbing atop of her and holding himself up on one arm. Her hands left the hem of his jacket to wrap around the back of his neck, trying to pull him closer, as if it was even possible.

When he'd pulled away, she pouted, until she felt his lips on her neck, teeth nibbling away at the soft of the skin. She bit down on her lips then, they were swollen from the impact, much more swollen than they'd been that day at the Honors Dorm.

He knew what he was doing, and there weren't any slight questions about it. The slightest whimper left her lips as he sucked on the skin of her neck, her fingers running through his once perfectly styled dark hair, pulling at it as if to give him further instructions.

"You're eager," he grumbles in her ear, "no more pretending you don't want it, huh?"

"You're so fucking annoying," she musters, though her breath was shaky and it was impossible to not show it.

And he knew it well, from the smirk on his lips and the way he licked them before he'd slowly brought himself down to the hem of her dress. She tried to remain calm, as calm as she could manage, but the mere touch of his fingers going up her thigh was enough to send her entire body into a frenzy. There was something about him, that sent shockwaves up her body, like she'd been waiting for his touch her entire life and didn't know what to do now that she had it.

Even more so, she didn't know what to do with the fact Haechan was in between her legs right now.

His fingers teasingly ran across the hood of her heat, playing with the fabric, causing her to whimper out, her head twisting against the sheets of his bed. She knew he wanted her to beg, but it was the last thing she'd let herself do.

Though, he was eager, too, which he'd never admit aloud, as his hands slowly pushed her dress up to expose her lower body. Head leaning down to kiss her stomach, trailing kisses until he reached her sensitivity again. There was the most painful tickle of his fingers on her skin as he pulled her thong down, causing her fingers to tangle themselves in the silk sheets, trying to keep her cool, which was becoming so much harder by the second.

As her fingers tangle in his hair, his tongue dancing across her clit, she knew it then. She knew there wasn't any coming back from this.

Yet, she didn't regret it a single bit.

38: denial is a river

**NEW POST** \@SUanon: anyone else see solar going upstairs with haechan at the sigma chi par??? someone check on mark LMFAOOOO

-

Mark didn't need to see the SUanon tweet to know where Solar had disappeared to, it didn't take a genius to come to the simple conclusion. Though, it would take one for him to keep his cool, as he shoved his phone into the back pocket of his jeans, pushing through the crowds of people. He was in the living area now, where the couches were scattered across the walls, people were smoking, snorting, making out, among other things. But he had a feeling this was where he would find Solar's friends, like there was the faintest bit of hope that she had wandered here instead, even if he knew deep down that was not going to be the case.

Hyein and Hanni were in fits of laughter, lounging on the leather sofa that sat in the farthest corner, a bit away from all of the sweaty people. With them, was none other than Chenle, which he expected, however, what he didn't expect was to see Renjun of all people sitting there, too.

He was slightly out of breath from all of the people he'd just frantically pushed through to reach them. His abrupt presence caused the group to stop chatting, Hanni looking to him first, eyes widening in concern from the look on his face. Even though he was trying to hide it, Mark looked rather distressed, standing there like a dog that had lost it's owner.

"Mark," Hanni calls out over the music, waving her hand cheerfully, though her voice was riddled with concern, "are you alright? Where's Solar?"

"Don't we already know the answer to that?" Chenle groaned.

Mark sent Chenle a rather unappreciative glare, before looking back to Hanni, "uh, yeah, she's... upstairs," he musters out, hating the way the words sounded coming off his tongue. The girl sends him a look of sympathy, motioning for him to join them on the sofa.

"You don't gotta look so down," Hyein leaned over Hanni, as Mark was sitting next to her on the far edge, "it's a party!" It was clear she had actually drank that night from the tint of red on her cheeks, as she threw her arms into the air and started moving them in circles, which was apparently her version of a dance move.

"Nah, I'm not down," he assures them with a light chuckle, "it's cool."

Renjun snorted from where he was on the far end, though the music was so loud that only Chenle could hear him, "he's really bullshitting through his teeth right now."

He was right, Mark was very far from cool, but he wasn't going to tell Solar's friends how irritatedly, increasingly angry he was becoming from Haechan's antics. With his personal goals aside, it wasn't that Mark didn't like Solar, even if the frat boys were being suspicious of him and his true intentions. She was a nice girl that he enjoyed being around, he didn't do these kinds of things for just anyone.

And he was deeply offended at the fact his best friend refused to leave her alone, even after her older brother had gotten onto him. Not to mention, Haechan alluding at the fact he potentially told Jaehyun that Mark was hanging around her, too.

He was very, very far from cool.

"Here, man," Chenle offered, reaching over to hand him a beer, "you need to relax."
