-

She didn't know what time it was when she finally stirred, nor would she allow herself to process the fact it had been the best sleep she'd gotten since she left the Upper East Side for Syracuse. The morning sun was pestering her through the blinds of the single window, making her irises scream in agony from the unforeseen light. Blinking a few times, Solar tries to come back down to reality, not because she had been drunk last night, but because she knew her brain was going to have a hard time facing what happened.

Though, before she could sit up, she realized, she was going to have to do something about the arm draped across her waist. She could smell that familiar, lingering vanilla, turning her head slowly to look at Haechan. His dark, once middle parted, hair was messy across his forehead, that silver chain he always wore still shimmering against his honey skin. He was beautifully, deeply annoying.

Only then, did she ponder the fact he was already awake, those brown irises peering into hers before she could rip her eyes away and disguise the fact she'd been admiring him.

"Good morning, sweetheart," he rasps out, making it clear he hadn't been awake for too much longer than she had. His fingertips are soft against her cheek as he used two to push her messy hair out of her face, "still tired after a long night, aren't you?"

She narrowed her eyes at him. "No way you're already going to piss me off."

He laughed then, not the one covered in a facade, but just the laugh that belonged to him, one not many people ever got to really see. His arm left her so he could sit up, throwing his legs over the side of his bed. At first, he looked determined, like he had a mission he needed to complete. But, then he halted, second guessing himself, running through something in his head. It was the first time she'd ever seen him unsure about a decision.

"Solar," he monotones slowly, causing her eyes to meet his in an instant. Something in her chest felt uneasy, almost nauseous about whatever words he was going to say. Would it be an insult, was he going to take it back, say it was a mistake? "Donghyuck," he deadpans finally, like it was something he couldn't seem to get out.

She blinks a few times at him, rolling the name over in her head. "What?"

"My real name," he hums, casual now, "only Mark uses it."

"Oh," her voice falls flat, because she had no idea how to begin to understand what to do with this newfound information or why he was deciding, suddenly, that she deserved to know it, "and you want me to..." she trails off, still unsure.

"Mm," he nods, "up to you, princess," he rises to his feet then, the normal Haechan attitude returning in an instant, somewhat, "I'll make breakfast," he informs her, entirely normal, and now allowing her to say another word before he spins on his heel and heads for the door.

She watched him go, feeling far too speechless, and not knowing any words that would possibly fit what she just heard. The door closed gently behind him, leaving her sitting there, in her bra in Haechan's bed, the very last place she ever imagined she would be.

Jesus Christ.

Shaking her head, still in complete denial and disbelief, she turns to scan the room for her clothes. After a moment, she spots her shirt next to the gaming chair, so she throws her own legs over the side of the bed to get it. There was the slightest ache in her inner thighs, one she refused to let herself fully acknowledge.

Once her shirt was over her head, she stood there in the middle of the room for a second. Her eyes catch her reflection in the dark of his PC monitor, staring back at herself. What are you doing? she could feel it asking her.

I don't know, she shook her head at herself, I seriously don't know.

But she knew the real reason.

-

Mark had been up for a long time.

Never would he admit that he was waiting for Haechan. He'd still had fun at the party even though he was irritated about the fact Solar had somewhat ditched him for his best friend again. He wasn't mad at Solar, per se, but his anger was indefinitely targeted towards his best friend, who he firmly believed was doing all of this on purpose.

Haechan had always had better luck with girls. They all flocked to him like he was some kind of prize they were winning, like he had all the answers to life they'd been longing for. He didn't know what it was about him that seemed to act like a magnet for women. And he had never expected his ridiculous antics to work on a girl like Solar. The fact that it felt like it was right in front of him made him livid, along with the fact that Haechan was spoiling all of his plans, entirely on purpose and right in his face.

He'd never call himself someone who got mad or started fights. Mark was never really that kind of guy, but there was something about this being what felt like his last real chance that twisted his veins in ways they'd never been twisted before. He knew Haechan knew it, too, maybe that was what hurt the most.

The last thing he needed was for Haechan to spoil everything, even if it was with the truth.

He knew Solar never left or came back downstairs, that much was clear by the time the party had come to a close. Hanni and Hyein, who had been freaking out at the beginning, suddenly became very chill with the fact their friend was upstairs with the campus playboy. They acted like it was no big deal, though Hyein had been partially a bit intoxicated. The two girls had a blast with Renjun and Chenle, seemingly making new friends, as if they had never been anxious to ever come to a party in the beginning of it all. Mark tried to enjoy himself, the best he could.

None of the other boys seemed to be awake yet. They never were on days like these, when the party had ran late because Jaehyun wasn't coming home until morning, it meant they were doing triple the drugs and likely going to have multiple women in their beds by morning. Haechan was someone very famous for this fact, yet he was notably very absent from what was once called a tradition.

Mark scoffed to himself the more he thought about it, tapping his fingernails against the granite countertop as he leaned there on the kitchen island.

Seconds later, the pattering of footsteps against the tile could be heard. He didn't move, just waited, only to see Haechan drowsily entering the kitchen doorway, hair in his eyes, tee hanging loosely on his body, a pair of varsity sweats he'd lazily thrown on himself.

He didn't notice Mark at first, he was too far in his head, opening the top cupboard where the skillets were and taking one out. Leaning against the counter with his hip, he began to hum to himself as he turned on the stovetop.

Mark had once loved to listen to his friend sing. As much as he always wanted to believe that he was the more musically gifted one, Haechan was a phenomenal singer, one that turned heads the second a note left his lips. The worst part about it, he didn't have to try to be good at it, he just was.

Right then, the sound of his voice irked him.

He stands up straight, about to finally say something, when Haechan's voice cuts through the atmosphere first. "You know standing there has no effect on me, Marky." He didn't even look at him when he said it, that teasing, blunt tone of his. He just bounced off the edge of the counter before opening the fridge, taking out some eggs and milk, before casually going back to the stove and continuing his task.

"Breakfast, Hyuck?" Mark finally finds his retort, crossing his arms over his chest, "Someone must be special." He was playing stupid, of course.

Haechan knew that well.

At first, he didn't seem like he was going to say anything, as he poured some oil into the skillet. Until he'd suddenly spun around, crossing his own arms over his chest and shrugging, "she needs the energy," like it was normal, no big deal, like he was turning it into some kind of joke. Mark knew it was all just a ploy, just to get under his skin and piss him off more, but he couldn't deny the fact it always seemed to work. His rage had only been growing since he'd gotten back with her drink last night only to realize the reality of where she'd gone.

His arms fall back to his side, hands bawling into fists as he tries to keep his composure, not wanting to let Haechan get the upper leg. But his veins were boiling, his cheeks were growing red, and he couldn't seem to help the way everything felt like it was bound to burst any moment now.

"Do you think is a fucking joke, Hyuck?" he blurts, before he could consider stopping himself, "Really, I told you that I like her, and this is what you do, to your best friend?"

He was still just standing there with his arms crossed. Nothing in his facial expression changed to even slightly indicate he was impacted by a word Mark said.

"You don't like her," he deadpans after a moment, "you can cut the act, Mark."

"Oh, I don't like her?" he snorts, in utter disbelief, "We both know you don't fucking feel that way about her, Haechan, because you don't like girls with brains."

Haechan's eyes were narrowed, scanning Mark's face like he was reading his mind. He pursed his lips for a second, pretending to be deep in thought, "how do you know what kind of girls I like?"

His tone was still teasing, like this was all some kind of hilarious skit to him, it was only worsening the anger Mark was trying so hard to keep inside. He shook his head then, gawking, "Because I know you, Hyuck, take a look in the fucking mirror."

"Yeah?" he retorts evenly, "News flash, Marky, you're deflecting."

Mark's stomach was starting to turn. He almost wanted to vomit, the way that his friend was being about all of this. What kind of friend is he? he couldn't help but wonder, because the boy standing here in front of him was starting to become unrecognizable. It wasn't that he encouraged Haechan to be the way that he was, but him continuing his typical antics made everything a lot easier for Mark in the end.

In that moment, he ran over the words to say in his head. He thought back to everything that had happened, all of the interactions he had seen, heard about, all of the rumors and the gossip and what the hidden truth beneath them could possibly reveal.

And it hit him.

"Why are you doing this, anyway?" he hums then, mockingly, "I'm starting to think maybe you do like her, Hyuck, is that it?" He chuckles, throwing his hands in the air like it was a marvelous revelation, "Haechan's caught feelings, wow. What would Jaehyun think about that after you talked your bullshit way out of it the first time?"

"Could say the same to you."

Mark's nails were cutting into his skin now from how hard he was clenching his fists. He knew Haechan had said something like this before, but he'd assumedly taken it as a joke. Hearing it a second time, after last night, did something else to him.

"I swear to fucking God, Haechan, you've lost your fucking mind," he snaps.

"It's fair game," he monotones, "you're the one with the ulterior motive here, Marky, don't you think Jaehyun should know that?"

He'd done it then.

Mark stepped forward a single time, bringing his arm back and swinging it cleanly with one fell swoop. Haechan didn't seem to see it coming, or at least, he never expected him to really do such a thing, only until Mark's fist collided with his jaw, a soft crack echoing through the air.

But Haechan didn't move, he didn't reciprocate the way you'd expect, didn't immediately swing a punch right back at Mark. He just stood there, taking it all in, like he was in a state of shock.

"What, are you in fucking love?" Mark hissed at him, knowing the words would only send him into a deeper state of contemplation, because he knew his best friend. He knew the things Haechan feared, he knew the deep rooted horrors that lived underneath his perfect, calm facade that he put on ever since they came to college. So, he knew exactly what to say to send him into a mental shutdown. "Be serious with yourself, Hyuck."

Still, he didn't say anything. A sense of victory started to flow through Mark's veins, he started to think about what this could mean.

But, the second he'd grown too distracted in his own thoughts, Haechan's fist slammed into his cheek. The impact sent Mark stumbling back into the countertop, the granite edge catching him at the small of his back, sharp enough that he hissed through his teeth. His hand flew to cover his cheek instinctively, feeling the already blooming ache spreading through his jaw.

His eyes dared to meet Haechan's, who still wasn't yelling, which was perhaps the worst part of it, the way he just stood there with his chest rising and falling, eyes dark, the hand he'd thrown the punch with hanging at his side like it hadn't done anything at all.

The adrenaline began to take over.

Mark launched himself forward, not graceful in the slightest, but it was the perfect definition of what happened when someone with far too much to lose finally felt like they had nothing left to protect. He grabbed Haechan by the collar, the momentum sending both of them into the kitchen island, the skillet clattering somewhere off the stove. Haechan shoved him back with both hands, hard, and Mark's shoulder caught the edge of a cabinet on the way down, though it didn't seem to slow him down.

"Wanna talk about ulterior motives?" he bitterly laughs, breathing through his teeth, "you think I don't know what you've been doing, sabotaging me since the beginning?"

Haechan's voice dropped to something flat and cold, "I didn't do anything, Mark, you did this to yourself," he pauses for a second, "all of it."

"Fuck off," he spat, "don't even fucking start."

"You've been lying to her since the day you met her," he deadpans anyway, the same way he said everything like it was a fact being read from a page, like the blood beginning to run from his nose wasn't an issue, "you want to make it my fault because that's just what you do, Marky."

Mark's next swing connected with Haechan's cheekbone. He didn't go down, just turned his face back slowly, something in his jaw tight, looking at Mark the way he'd looked at him across a thousand rooms over the years. Never had Mark been afraid of his best friend, but he was starting to feel something now.

"Say it again, Hyuck, I dare you," he breathes.

He wouldn't say it again.

Instead, he grabs Mark's forearm before the. third swing landed, holding it there, while the both of them kept breathing harder, the kitchen smelling like that of burning oil due to the forgotten stove. But no one threw another punch.

The silence, however, was the loudest it had ever been between them, maybe louder than the scholarship incident, even.

Mark wrenched his arm free, taking a few steps back, as the rage began to bleed into something else, something he was less equipped to handle, that sat behind his sternum and felt uncomfortably like grief. He could see his own reflection in the stainless surface on the fridge, staring back at him like a stranger, he looked like hell.

Haechan turned away from him, that was the thing about him, and he knew it better than anyone. When someone like him was done, there were no take backs. His expression was blank, leaning against the kitchen counter with his arms crossed, eyes looking at the ceiling, the bruising already beginning to bloom at his cheekbone.

Haechan wasn't going to say another word.

Mark wanted to keep going, to say something else, to tell him to stop doing that thing where he became avoidant. He wanted to demand he stay present for this, that they hash it out like men and acknowledge what happened and the meaning behind it. In reality, he wasn't sure if he came out of it with any answers he had been searching for, and he was very far from satisfied with that conclusion. He'd known this boy for the better part of his life, yet had no clue how to exist in a version where they were standing in a kitchen with split knuckles, unknowing how to speak to each other.

He opened his mouth to say something, but the footsteps on the stairs came first. Jeno and Jaemin appeared in the kitchen doorway with seconds of each other, both of them sleep-rumpled, each stopping at the exact same moment. Jeno's eyes moved between the two twice before landing on the splattered oil all over the stove and the skillet that was not in a normal spot, Jaemin's landed on Haechan's face.

"What the hell..." Jaemin muttered, not exactly a question but more like the beginning of a sentence he didn't exactly want to even finish right then.

None of them had the chance to say anything else, not even a smug comment or a poorly timed joke, because the front door had opened behind them in the entryway, the particular sound of it that meant someone who lived here and not someone that overstayed their welcome. Everyone was seemingly frozen as the footsteps, measured and unhurried, grew closer.

Jaehyun appeared in the doorway, next to Jeno and Jaemin, seconds later. His face was blanker than Haechan's, always calculating, eyes scanning the room in the way he always did, without any visible reaction to what he was cataloguing. His eyes moved from Mark to Haechan, from the skillet to the oil still smoking faintly on the stove, to Jeno and Jaemin frozen in the threshold to the very specific quality of silence that filled the kitchen like a ghost.

Another few beats of silence pass.

"Living room," he exhales finally, stern, "all of you."

39: the escape of shame

The second Jaehyun told them to meet in the living room, a lot of things were clear. Haechan would know, before Jaehyun even uttered a word, that there was no version of the next hour that ended well for anyone in this house.

However, he also knew well would easily turn into hell if he didn't tell Solar to get out of Sigma Chi before anyone opened their mouths and Jaehyun became too aware of his blood relation that was sitting in a certain bedroom upstairs.

He followed the others out of the kitchen without a word, the particular silence he'd put on sitting heavier than usual, the bruising at his cheekbone a dull and constant reminder of where his morning had gone. Entirely haywire, is what it was, painfully, excruciatingly opposite from how he planned for it to go. But of course, no one would ever know that now, especially not Solar.

Jeno and Jaemin, shockingly, had enough sense to not ask any questions then. Jaehyun had already moved ahead of them, down the hall, creepily far too composed. That was the thing about Jaehyun Lee, when he was like this, his calm was very far from being reassuring, it was the kind of calm that meant he'd already finished thinking and had arrived at his conclusions, that the conversations they were about to have was less of a discussion than a verdict.

Mark fell into step beside him for approximately three seconds, not saying a word. Instead, his eyes met Haechan's for a mere moment before glancing upward, toward the ceiling, the second floor, the room that was directly above them, then back to Haechan's face.

He didn't meet his eyes, just stared straight ahead and pulled out his phone the second no one seemed to be paying much attention.

\@haechanah:\ your brother is here

\@haechanah:\ probably best if we call it here

\@haechanah:\ wait until you hear us go into the living room then come down

\@haechanah:\ front door

\@sollee:\ you're shitting me

\@sollee:\ i totally forgot abt that

Solar had been sitting on the edge of his bed when the texts came through, still in the process of attempting to organize her thoughts into something that made sense. Hearing the voices downstairs hadn't bothered her at first, they were muffled through the floor, and she'd told herself it wasn't her business. It'd grown quiet before she could fully process who the voices had belonged to.

Something in the register of those texts made her chest do a thing she didn't have the time to examine, the way it read like a message from someone she didn't know, impersonal, like it was a one night stand they'd never see again. Something tasted rather sour in her mouth, her mind wandering back to all of the different things she had heard over the course of her time here. She pushes it away, reminding herself it wasn't what mattered right now.

Her dress was on in thirty seconds, before she'd spent the rest of her short time searching for her heel. She found one instantly and spent another twelve humiliating moments locating the second before deciding she'd carry them. Her bag was where she'd somehow left it by the chair, and she slung it over her shoulder and pressed her ear to the door, listening.

The sound of voices, male, plural, she tracked them through the floor as best she could, kitchen to the hallway, hallway towards the front of the house, then the low specific register of a voice she recognized from a thousand family dinners that made the back of her neck grow cold. She waited until the voices settled, hearing the opening of a door somewhere, the sound of people finding seats.

And finally, she opened the door, letting out the deepest breath and tip toeing into the hallway of the second floor. It was empty, but she kept to the wall anyway, the carpet plush underneath her feet. The stairs came up on her left, she stopped at the top of them, pressing herself tighter against the wall and listening again.

She could hear Jaehyun's voice now, clearly enough to track the cadence of it even if not the words, coming straight from the living room. Far enough, she shrugged internally, knowing this was probably her best chance. Slowly but surely, she descended the steps one at a time, weight to the outside edge of each step the way she'd learned at sixteen when she decided she was no longer going to just be a doll that her parents pranced around.

The third from the bottom creaked, causing her heart to skip a beat and body to freeze for a split second. But the voices in the living room continued, entirely uninterrupted.

So, she crossed the hallway in four steps, finding the front door, before letting herself out with the slowest handle turn she'd ever managed in her life. In reality, it wasn't super necessary that she was so quiet, since there were various girls that had already left the house without much of a care in the world. Most of them, in fact, were in high spirits after the night they'd just had with one of the critically acclaimed frat boys.

Solar wasn't sure she quite felt that way.

The door clicked shut behind her. Outside, the sky was early and indifferent, the sunlight too bright, too cheerful for what was sitting in her chest.

She stood on the front step for a moment, heels still in her hand, and let out a deep, deep exhale. It would be two blocks before she'd pull her phone out of her bag again. The morning was the kind of quiet that existed only between parties, the street still carrying the faint residue of last night, a few scattered red cups the boys hadn't gotten during their cleanup, a string of lights someone had forgot to turn off. Her heels were still in her hand, she'd eventually stopped worrying so much about what she must look like.

Her eyes scan over those text messages again, the same way she had in his room, searching for something between the words, some version of the person who'd handed her his name that morning like it was something she'd earned. She guessed, there was something happening, though she had no clue it had anything to do with her. Maybe he's anxious, she told herself, stressed from dealing with frat business.

Huffing, she tucks her phone back into her bag. The walk back to Alpha Phi only took about ten minutes, meaning she had ten minutes to figure out what to do with everything she was carrying, and she was realizing that amount of time was not going to suffice.

-

The living room of Sigma Chi had the particular quality of a room that had hosted a party twelve hours ago and had yet to fully recover. Jeno and Jaemin were still visibly hungover and having a hard time keeping their mouths shut, they took their seats on the couch. Mark, on the other hand, took the far end, elbows on his knees and his jaw set. He'd had yet to make eye contact with Haechan again since they'd walked out of the kitchen. Haechan took the chair closest to the door, which was not a coincidence.

Jaehyun was standing, which wasn't abnormal for him, it was something Haechan had understood early on, that standing while everyone else sat wasn't something he did for effect, it was simply how he operated, like he needed his body to stay available while his mind was working. His arms were crossed, not looking at anyone in particular yet.

Nobody said anything. Somewhere upstairs, Renjun was probably washing his face and Chenle was likely already loading up Overwatch.

Until finally, he cleared his throat, "I'm not going to ask what happened," he opens, conversational, "I don't need either of you to explain something I already understand," he pauses, "what I'm going to do, is tell you what I know, then we're going to talk about what comes next." His eyes moved to Mark, then to Haechan, unhurried, "are we clear?"

Neither of them said a word still, which was close enough for confirmation. Jaehyun slid his hands into his pockets, "I'm aware that both of you continue to spend time with my sister." The words flew off his tongue, almost making Haechan wince from the way it sounded, his sister. He maintained his composure by keeping his eyes on the middle distance, feeling the careful stillness looming on the couch. "I'm not going to pretend I'm oblivious to the meaning, or that either of you had no idea I'd find out... I'm also not going to pretend, that the two of you in my kitchen this morning was unrelated."

Fuck.

Mark opened his mouth, but Jaehyun raised his hand, just barely, yet it was enough for him to shut it again. "I'm not done," he states, firmly.

He turned then, his attention moving to Mark in a way that seemed to change the pressure of the room. Jeno and Jaemin exchanged glances, telepathically talking about the situation with their facial expressions. "It's only been a year," he deadpans, matter of factly, without elaborating on it further. He didn't need to, the reference was surgical, specific, everyone already knew what he was talking about without him saying a word. The color left Mark's face in the following second, confirming he understood exactly what the president was referencing. "You know what I think, Mark?" It wasn't a real question anyone needed to answer. "I think you've known that this conversation was coming for a while."

"I'm not going to reiterate it," Jaehyun continues, "but I'm going to be clear about one thing, whatever you thought you were doing with my little sister, it's done, as of now," he was growing more demanding now, "that's not a suggestion, if you think you can use her to get something you feel you lost, you're gravely wrong."

Haechan became aware, distantly, that Jaehyun had turned to look at him, but he kept his eyes in the same spot. "Haechan," Jaehyun offers, though there was something in the way he spoke his name that was different from how he'd addressed Mark, less heat or perhaps a different kind, the specific patience of someone who knew the person they were talking to well enough to know that volume wasn't the right instrument. "I'm not going to insult you by pretending your record gives you a lot of standing."

The dark haired boy just pursed his lips in a thin, straight line. There wasn't much he could say to that, it wasn't like he could deny it.

"But you already know that," he exhales, "which is the only reason we're having a conversation instead of something else," he pauses again, "after the football game, I trusted you'd heed my words... I'm disappointed that you would ignore them." Another few seconds of silence passed, sharp enough to slice the entire building in half, "she's my sister, you should understand what I'm telling you."

He understood, far too well what he was telling him. You're bad, you've messed with too many girls, your reputation says this about you, you do a lot of drugs. None of the words had to be said because they were already constantly floating around his brain like a bad cold. Haechan looked at his hands, the bruising across his knuckles had settled into something darker since the kitchen. He thought about his room upstairs, about the particular texture of silence after someone sang something real before trying to pretend nothing happened, about the sound of his door closing behind him this morning and the odd, giddy feeling in his chest he had never quite felt before at the mere thought of making breakfast for someone that wasn't himself.

Then he thought about the way Jaehyun was expectantly peering down at him right now, the way Mark had spat in his face in utter disgust as he winced at the word love, the way he'd sent those text messages like she was simply another girl he was finished with.

"Uhuh," he monotones finally.

Still, the president's eyes remained on him for a moment longer.

"I want both of you to understand I'm not in the business of making threats," he adds then, to the room, "but I am in the business of making sure people in this house understand where the boundaries are," he didn't raise his voice, he remained entirely collected, "are we clear where the line is?"

It wasn't really a question meant to be answered.

The meeting ended with Jaehyun deciding it was over, the way the meetings always did, the room beginning to shift. Jeno and Jaemin jumped to their feet, the specific relief of having been peripheral while the weight landed elsewhere, Mark didn't move immediately.

Jaehyun turned to leave the room himself, before he seemed to hesitate for the first time. "I'm not having this conversation a third time, Haechan."

Then, he left, like everything was fine and entirely casual.

It was not.

Haechan didn't move yet, either. For a moment, him and Mark remained in the same room, in the same silence, though it was vastly different from the one in the kitchen, which was somehow much worse.

Until finally, he stands first. He leaves the room without sparing a glance at his best friend.

He didn't care to. There was one thing on his mind then, and it wasn't Solar.

It was the small baggy he had in his nightstand upstairs that he hadn't touched in a long while now, and seemingly, it felt like the only thing that was going to make any of this go away.

40: bad at being fine

As much as Solar wanted to believe she was completely fine, that no one on campus was fully aware of what happened last night other than her two trustworthy friends, that much was never going to be true.

The Saturday sky gleamed down on her, the sun still pondering if it wanted to make an appearance. She'd been walking for two blocks before she registered she was cold, the particular cold that lived in the hour before seven, her heels in one hand, bag on her shoulder, legs moving at the pace of someone trying to get somewhere before anyone could see them. Although, the campus was still mostly quiet, eery almost, as in there wasn't anyone that could see her, or so she wanted to think.

Alpha Phi's front door stuck a little in the cold, it always did, October through February, something in the frame, and she shouldered it open the way she'd learned to, stepping into the quiet of a house that was still a couple of hours from waking. The carpet was soft under her feet, hallway reminiscent of whatever Danielle sprayed in the entryway again, something too floral, too sharp, that she'd stopped commenting on weeks ago.

She lets out a breath she didn't realize she was holding as she stands at the end of the steps, shifting her heels to her other hand and slowly raising her foot to put her weight on the first. So, she'd learned, the stairs didn't creak if you stayed to the right.

Her painfully pink bedroom was exactly as she'd left it. Slipping inside, she closes the door, standing there with the lights off, the curtains letting in a pale seam of morning. Her mind dared to run through the events of last night, of the morning, back the way it did when she ran games that went sideways, methodically, in sequence, looking for where the play broke down. She could still feel his touch fresh on her skin, the callouses of his fingers gliding across her body like they'd belonged there, his arm across her waist, his voice rougher than usual like he'd forgotten to put the performance back on. There was a particular smell of him, vanilla on top of something else she didn't yet have a word for, which happened to be a detail she disposed of immediately.

But then there was the way he'd handed her his name, his real name she had only ever heard Mark use against him, like it'd costed him something yet nothing, the grin playing on the corner's of his lips as he mumbled, up to you, princess.

Something felt very wrong about it all, the way that things didn't seem to go the way they were supposed to.

Supposed to, she shook her head at herself, bitterly. Was she admitting it now? she thought next, recalling the drop in her chest when she picked up her phone and saw those text messages, that felt far too casual for everything that they'd just experienced. Is that really just him? Was it all a game?

She didn't want to believe the rumors were true, but more so, she refused to believe her brother could ever be right. But the words probably best if we call it here tasted petrifying in her mouth.

Dropping her heels to the floor, she falls onto the edge of her bed, still in the dress from the night before that she'd hurriedly thrown on that morning. Solar didn't move then, she just sat there, staring at a blank, empty space on her wall.

Nothing about the last 24 hours felt like a mistake until she read those text messages. She knew it had to be Jaehyun, right? It was always Jaehyun. Because everything that happened had felt like the complete opposite of a mistake, at least to her.

She supposed, that the issue lied in the fact she didn't know what it really meant to him, if anything.

The rest of the day, Solar would spend lost in her own head, trying to make any sense of what happened. She picked up her phone and stared down at the last messages, the ones she sent, playing it off like it was casual. The read receipt was right in her face, sending a ping through her chest. He didn't say anything else.

With a sigh, she buries her face in her hands, resisting the urge to punch herself in the face. She felt like a total, complete idiot.

-

NEW POST\ \@SUnews

![\@SUnews: solar lee spotted leaving sigma chi this morning](media/image7.png){width="6.5in" height="8.125in"}

\@SUnews: solar lee spotted leaving sigma chi this morning. early morning, isn't it, solar? we hope it was worth it!

-

Practice ran two hours and forty minutes that night, and she'd ran through it without incident, which was perhaps the most irritating thing that'd happened to her all weekend, the discovery that her body had no interest in her current emotional state, that it intended to keep performing anyway. She tracked rotations, called positioning, won the trades she needed to, and said only about thirty words that weren't callouts by the time it'd wrapped up. Doyoung made no notes to her between scrims, though she didn't know if it meant anything.

Minji was the same as she always was, effortlessly kind, not pushing but letting her know she was there. It was clear she knew something was wrong, but also didn't want to overstep, either. Solar was thankful for that, because she didn't think she had the energy or even knew how to begin to explain to her anything that was happening in her mind.

She'd gotten home that night after practice and flopped onto her bed without a second thought. Even though the tournament was this week, she didn't have the energy to practice anymore.

It was half past nine when her phone began angrily buzzing next to her on the pillow. Her eyes shot open like a wildfire, hand snatching up the screen and squinting at it through the dark of her room.

Who did she want it to be? The answer was obvious, and the name on the screen did not reflect that wish.

"You did something," she answers the call, not in the mood for greetings or any fake talk. If he was going to call her, she wasn't going to hold back anything. Her tone was accusatory, irritable, and if one looked deep enough, hurt.

"Solar," her brothers emotionless tone cuts through her thoughts, calibrated and entirely unhurried, the specific register of someone who'd already arrived at all of his conclusions and didn't care for anyone else's input, "I handled things," he corrects, "you shouldn't have further contact from anyone in the fraternity."

She didn't answer immediately, instead, she sat up straight, back against her headboard, the hand that wasn't holding her phone clenching into a fist in the mattress. "Handled things?" she repeats, in disbelief.

"Yes, whatever was going on, it's done," he continues, smoothly, "I was not making a suggestion when I told you to stay away from Mark and Haechan, Solar. They are equally trouble and not who you need to be spending your time with."

Her mind wandered back to those text messages, the impersonal register, the way they'd read like a message from a stranger. She thought about the morning in his room, the soft of his skin, the way it all felt like something so full of passion.

"You threatened them," she deadpans finally, "like you always fucking do, you threaten the people I get close to."

"I had a conversation that needed to happen," he informs, "Solar, I always do these things because..."

"...Because what?" she cuts him off, not wanting to hear the bullshit he was going to spew, because she'd heard it far too many times before, "oh, cause you know what's best for me, right? Cause you made a decision about my life, how I should live it, and called that handling," she throws her legs over the edge of her bed, facing the window, the amber paths below full of people who had no idea what was happening inside her chest, "in case you forgot, I'm not a child anymore, Jaehyun. I didn't leave the Upper East Side to be thrown right back into it."

Her brother didn't say anything for a couple of minutes. She knew it wasn't because he was reconsidering his decisions, for that was not something Jaehyun Lee was capable of doing.

"It's understandable that you're frustrated," he voices after a moment, "I've heard that you've grown very close to them."

He's heard.

"You've heard, huh?" she retorts, "I bet I can guess from who."

"Do not jump to conclusions," he snaps, "whether you like it or not, Solar, it was the right call."

"Sure," she hums, "because you're always right, aren't you?" Her words were dripping with venom, sarcasm, and her brother knew exactly what she was thinking before she even said it.

"I don't want to get father involved," he monotones, "listen to me, do not seek them out further."

There it was, the threat he had been biting down on the entire time, waiting for the perfect time to reveal. She exhaled slowly through her nose, two fingers pressed to her temple where a headache was beginning to surface. There was no version of this conversation where Jaehyun changed his mind, regretted anything, or backed down, that was never something he'd do, it wasn't in the cards. And she knew from experience that the threat he made was unfortunately far too real. She knew from eighteen years of shared history exactly what his steady, patient silence on the other end meant, which was that he'd already moved past this part and was waiting for her to catch up.

"Whatever," she exhales, not because she agreed, but because she felt she had nothing left.

She heard him exhale to say something, but she didn't wait to hear what it was, as she clicked the end call button, chunking the phone somewhere behind her on the mattress.

Her brother had always been this way, she didn't know what she expected.

But one thing still felt awry.

Jaehyun's interference had never worked before, she couldn't help but ponder it. After the football game, Haechan had never looked at her any differently, he always remained unbothered, like he himself decided on his choice and felt confident in it. She'd watched that happen over and over, the sincerity in his decisions, the particular quality of indifference to consequences, the way pressure from Jaehyun slid off of him without purchase.

So, why would he care now?

"Sol," she hears a knock at the door, a small voice calling out, "it's us, can we come in?"

"Uhuh."

The door opened, Hyein and Hanni peeking their heads through. Hanni had a small bowl of fruit, which was clearly her specialty, the expression on her face that she wore when she'd already assessed the situation and chosen gentleness over efficiency, Hyein behind her doing a rapid scan of the room that catalogued the takeout container on the desk, her turned off PC, and Solar herself, sitting there on her unmade bed with a hoodie on and her hair in her face.

Hanni set the grapes on the nightstand, sitting on the edge of the bed next to Solar without a word, while Hyein fell into the gaming chair.

"Don't ask," Solar tells them sternly.

The two girls exchange a glance, debating with each other on how to handle this. Hanni opens her mouth before closing it, hesitating, before she softly offers, "well, Sol, you haven't left your room since practice... you've been really quiet, we were just worried about you."

"I've been busy," she musters, "with homework and stuff."

It was a terrible excuse and an even worse lie, Hyein turned to look at the quiet, black PC monitor, voicing the obvious because she couldn't help herself, "your PC is off, though."

Solar huffed, picking a piece of fruit out of the bowl, popping it into her mouth, and staring off at the wall. They'd clearly caught her in the lie, and she didn't even feel like trying to get out of it.

"You don't have to tell us anything," Hanni assures her, settling in the way she did when she had decided to wait as long as it took, "we're here, no matter what, okay?"

Hyein, on the other hand, was having quite the trouble keeping her mouth shut. You could tell, from the way she was bouncing her knee and biting on the inside of her cheek. Until finally, she looked as if she was about to burst, "is it Haechan?!"

The name arrived the way names did when you'd been actively trying to not say them, something in her chest did the small, painful thing it'd been doing since Friday night, the tightening and the pull she'd been ignoring. She popped another piece of fruit in her mouth, trying to pretend she didn't hear the way Hanni was whisper lecturing Hyein like an angry, disapproving mother.

"I don't know," she finally admits dully, "something is wrong, but I don't know what."

The two look at each other again. Hyein leaned forward in the desk chair, chin in the palm of her hand, pursing her lips in a straight line. When she spoke, her voice had gone deliberate in the way it did when she was rarely being delicate rather than honest, which was an effort that was visible on her face. "He does this," she states, "like, that's what people say about him, you know, it's not you," she got louder towards the end, showing how passionate she was about her words. However, they weren't exactly the most helpful, as Hanni was still looking at her in complete bewilderment and frustration, causing the girl to snap firmly, "she needs to know that!"

"Hyein," Hanni urged.

"I'm not trying to be mean or anything, I just..." she trails off, eyes flashing back to Solar. Her voice grows quieter, then, almost that of a whisper, "I just want you to know it's not something you did."

She appreciated that they weren't saying I told you so. Really, maybe he had fooled them, too. She thought about all of the things people said about Haechan, how she'd been hearing them since orientation week, using them as reasons to not notice him. But then there was the way she'd felt so sure that none of them were true, that he was someone entirely different, from the way he laughed to how he seemed to see right through her, noticing all of the things other people never did, the way he'd brought her to that rooftop and told her things he hadn't told anyone before. He said she was the only one who knew he went up there.

She was, right?

"Well, it's already on blast," Solar sighs after a moment, "the whole campus pretty much knows now, or at least they're putting it together."

"Hey," Hanni firmly urged, placing a hand on her shoulder and giving it a slight squeeze, "you don't owe anyone an explanation, alright?"

"Yet everyone thinks it's their business," she almost laughed then, bitterly.

"Are you okay?" Hyein chimed, abruptly, "actually?"

It was the million dollar question she didn't have the heart to answer. She turned it over with more attention that she usually would've. It wasn't because she was embarrassed, per se, she was simply exhausted, furious at her older brother, confused about what it was in her chest that she felt she didn't have sufficient time to name, the part she kept avoiding looking at directly because looking at it would possibly make it real and that meant it could hurt in ways that hypothetical things couldn't.

Though, maybe it already was real, because the pain in her chest seemed to be saying enough.

"Not really," she shrugs.

Hanni patted her shoulder a couple of times, before reaching over to grab the bowl of fruit. She didn't say anything else, neither did Hyein, they just leaned in to eat the fruit together like it was a completely casual day.

41: strawberry refresher

The music was loud enough that no one could hear themselves think, which was the entire point of a Sigma Chi party.

This wasn't any Sigma party, definitely not the typical Friday parties that happened weekly without fail. Every now and then, Jaemin decided to throw what he claimed as recovery parties whenever Jaehyun was busier than usual, out somewhere relative to football or his other fancy university activities. When those few chances came, Sigma would throw an extra party during the weekend, a more private one that always somehow turned dirtier. The boys all got a certain number of invites, and typically, the invites tended to go to women on campus, or sometimes women outside of campus.

Mark didn't invite Solar. Not because he was upset with her, but because he knew, both in a caring way and a calculated way, that she didn't nor wouldn't want to be at this party.

Haechan was leaning against the kitchen counter at half past eleven on Sunday night, a cup in one hand, eyes on the middle distance, watching the people move through the house how they always did on these rare days, smaller crowd, different energy, the tail end of the weekend rather than the height of it. The kitchen smelled like beer and something burnt, two girls sat on the kitchen counter on the other side of the island, talking to each other, though one kept glancing over at him every few seconds for the better part of an hour.

Then, he couldn't really careless. Instead, he refilled his cup without even processing what he was pouring.

The thing about Jaemin's recovery parties was that they required nothing of him other than being present, which was the only thing he could manage to do then. He'd left his room around nine, when the noise had gotten too loud and it was painfully obvious the party was peaking, and his own room was beginning to feel like that of a prison, the way the project file sat open on his laptop, the cursor frozen for two hours, his room smelling faintly of that vanilla mixed with the sweet candy of the girl he was trying to erase from his memory. He needed to be somewhere else, he decided, quickly.

Those girls in the kitchen were there when he'd entered. One with darker hair, the same who'd kept looking at him that night, had finally jumped off the edge of the counter to walk over to him, so he could hear her over the booming of the music. She leaned forward, the scent of her perfume entering his nose with a burn, "you good there? You don't seem as talkative as usual."

Every girl on SU campus knew the rumors about Haechan, it was hard to miss them, with the way everyone loved to talk about him. Girls were obsessed with him, because he was good at talking to them, he knew the right words to say to get what he wanted, and there was nothing they loved more than chasing a man who's so emotionally unavailable.

"Mm," he returns after chugging practically his entire drink, "nothing to say."

He still didn't look at her a single time. She'd turned to meet eyes with her friend who was now feet away herself, leaning against the counter next to them, before turning back to Haechan and uttering out a joyful, fake laugh. She was pretty, he thought for a split second when his eyes merely glanced, in the specific way a lot of these girls tended to be, deliberately put together, peering up at him with the particular calculation of someone who'd done their research.

"Well," she hums casually after a moment, "talking's not really required."

The two girls start giggling from her comment, leaning over to whisper to each other. The second one taps his arm, in a manner that was telling him to come on without saying another word. His eyes follow them as they leave the kitchen, heading towards the steps, before stopping to peer at him expectantly, waiting for him to follow.

Haechan's lips formed a straight line of contemplation, he dipped his head to pull something out of his pocket. Turning, he filled up his cup again, before tilting his head back and taking the pills before washing them down with more alcohol.

His throat didn't usually burn like it did then.

It would take an hour for the girls to actually feel confident enough to make a move, once they realized he was seemingly entirely uninterested. Of course, no girl ever took that as a no from him, because it was partly what he was known for. Sometimes, he was the sweetest guy you'd ever meet, but it would disappear in the flash of an eye. No one ever really knew what to expect from him, but it just made the girls feign more.

He was laying there on his bed, against his headboard, arms behind his head, staring up at the ceiling. Preferably, he was waiting for the high to hit, then maybe he could fully slip back into his old habits without it feeling so bitter. But the room, his room, smelled wrong, not like something specific, but wrong in the sense that things had been sitting abnormally at the edges of his perception.

Slowly but surely, the pills were beginning to take the sharpness off everything. Someone said something funny earlier, and he'd laughed, flat, because it was the correct response that he did without thinking. The dark haired girl finally moved to crawl onto the bed next to him, hands running up his arm, nails gently scraping against the muscles of his biceps. One of her hands went to his knee, causing his eyes to meet hers. Her eyelashes fluttered at she looked up at him from where she was then, head hovering next to his hip, it was evident what she was offering. Any other Sunday night, he wouldn't have had to think twice about it, it was the entire purpose of Sunday nights, wasn't it?

Her hands go to the button of his jeans, the other girl made her way to his other side, clearly intrigued and wanting to take part in whatever fun was about to unfold.

Before he could fully think about what he was doing, he'd reached out to push her hands off of his jeans, body sitting up abruptly and causing the two girls eyes to widen in surprise as he'd suddenly looked like he'd been awakened from a deep slumber. It wasn't a decision, per se, the decision came after, though he understood from the particular crawling sensation in his stomach that something in him had made the call before he could.

They were clearly confused, making eye contact with the other and asking each other what do we do? This has never happened before.

"Get out," he deadpans, emotionless, "I need to handle something."

The dark haired girl chuckles, awkwardly, unsure of how to handle the situation and not ashamed of the disappointment written across her face. "What do you mean, Haechannie?" she gawks, "That's what we're for."

Still, neither of them made an effort to move.

"Wasn't a suggestion," he restates, ignoring her last teasing attempt, "get out."

The two girls slowly rose to their feet, and once they were standing, he got off his bed, making his way to the door and holding it open for them, as if he needed to do that. His foot tapped impatiently against the carpet, eyes not looking at them as they gathered anything they'd brought up, he was staring at the wall with the patience of someone who'd long finished negotiating.

The second one left first, while the dark haired girl was clearly lingering, running through her head on what she could do. From the look on his face, she seemed to register there was no saving the situation.

Though, she stopped at the doorway anyway, deciding whether to say the thing she wanted to say or not.

"It's about Solar," she mutters knowingly, turning to look at him once she was in the hallway next to her friend, who looked horrified that she'd even brought it up, "isn't it?"

They'd never had noticed it, but something flattened in his chest. His face remained unchanged, blank, like he had no idea who she was talking about.

"You know, people have just been saying things," she continues anyway, as her friend slapped her arm, whispering to her to stop it and come on, "I wanted to know if it's actually serious, or if it's like... cause of Jaehyun... or Mark."

The sound of his best friend's name made it a little harder to keep his mouth shut, but he did anyway.

He'd heard versions of that question before when he was the one asking it, about Mark, about the way Mark had found Solar first with his oddly specific dorkiness, about the particular warmth his friend deployed when he'd found something beneficial to him. He'd built the entire thing around knowing what the answer was supposed to be, back at the beginning, when he'd recognized that pattern and decided this is my chance to finally get some ounce of revenge.

It had slowly stopped feeling like revenge.

"Leave," he monotones, though it was quieter than he'd intended.

The second girl had practically dragged her friend away after that. He closed the door anyway, not wanting to hear another irritating word from some nosy woman that simply wanted to add to the rumors already going around.

Haechan stood there for a while, back against his closed door, breathing slightly irregular for a reason he couldn't explain. He thought about Mark, the look on his face in the kitchen Saturday morning, not surprised, far too practiced to be surprised, the expression of someone who'd run several versions of a scenario and was checking which one he was in.

And the way that Jaehyun didn't seem to be joking in the slightest when he'd said I'm not having this conversation a third time specifically to him.

Everything seemed to dissipate as he picked up that baggy on his nightstand, deciding the pills weren't doing enough for him.

-

Solar hadn't even gotten to take a first drink of her sparkling water that morning, a Monday morning at that. There were visible dark circles under her eyes, her skin dull, she'd lazily tied her hair up atop of her head, not wanting to mess with it. The only thing she'd wanted to do that morning before heading to class was get a nice drink of sparkling water.

But, of course, Danielle was there, as if she wasn't going to have to look at her in her Intro to Business class anyway.

And she wasted no time to open her mouth.

"Solar," she boasts, "wow, you look rough. Long weekend?"

She heaved out a sigh, twisting the bottle cap off and taking a drink, letting the flavor seep into her tongue. Solar had been awake since seven in the morning, which was much too early for her in every capacity, but her body refused to sleep properly with everything on her mind.

Only then, did her eyes glance up to Danielle. "I'm sure you already know," she deadpans, though her words lacked the spice and energy they usually had.

"Partly," she shrugs anyway, pouring her glass of coffee from the small machine, "people do love to talk."

"No more than you, I'm sure," Solar exhales.

Danielle was unfazed, nonetheless, taking a sip of her coffee and nodding, like it was entirely reasonable. "Aw, I hope you feel better," she coos after a moment, feet taking her to the doorway of the kitchen, "Jaehyun wants what's best for you, you know."

She was gone before she could say anything else, which she realized, was probably for the better. There was no telling the words that she'd produce when she was in this sort of mindset.

So, she shakes her head, gripping the edge of the counter with one hand while continuing to drink her sparkling water with the other. It's fine, she tells herself, everything is fine.

Hours later, her statistics class would run for an extra ten minutes, because the professor was feeling deeply passionate about the current module. Solar had her head buried in her notes, the ones that were a bit emptier than she'd like, because her mind was having a harder time focusing on the content on the projector.

But she was trying, harder than she ever had needed to, to pay attention.

Mark was next to her, though they hadn't spoken yet. He'd gotten here a few minutes late, once the professor had already started running through what they'd be talking about today. He hadn't tried to say anything as he slid into his seat next to her, he just sent her a small smile, placing a cake pop in front of her, like it was casual.

She mustered a close lipped, mediocre grin as a thanks, as she unwrapped the cake pop and began eating it in the middle of the lecture, not caring about the girls that kept staring at her like she was some kind of beast.

"Hey," Mark had offered gently, under his breath, so no one else could hear except her, leaning over just slightly, "for what it's worth, Sol, I'm sorry."

She didn't say anything at first. The words didn't seem to come to her then, all she could feel was the ache in her chest, slight but present. There was no point in asking how he knew, of course he knew, if everyone else on campus knew, it only made sense Mark himself would be aware. She was guessing, based off of Jaehyun's words, that he'd spoken to him, too.

Yet, Mark was sitting next to her like nothing was wrong.

Knowing there was no use in deciphering what he knew and didn't know, she just sighed, "I'm fine, Mark."

"I believe you," he nods confidently, "those cake pops always do the trick."

He was such a nerd, causing her to shake her head, chuckling a bit, which was something she hadn't done in a couple of days now. She stared ahead at the screen, the professor continued to ramble on about a formula she didn't understand yet.

"I thought things were... different," she mumbles finally, pursing her lips afterward because it was painful to say the words aloud, nonetheless to the best friend of the boy she was mentioning.

Mark had to think for a long moment. It took him so long, that the professor had wrapped up his lecture, closing the class down and bidding farewell to the students. People began to jump from their seats, bolting to leave the room like they'd been in a prison cell. Girls continued to stare and whisper, some even pointing like Solar and Mark were a zoo exhibit they'd never seem before. But the two kept sitting there, even when the room was nearly empty.

"I know how he is, Sol," he offers, his voice soft in the way it got when she least expected it, "this is just what he does, it's got nothing to do with you."

Nothing to do with her, not her fault, everyone kept saying that to her. She wanted to blame her brother, to hate him, to ridicule him, but she couldn't help but feel like it didn't make any sense. Why was Mark still talking to her, being her friend, bringing her a cake pop and plopping into the seat next to her like no one else in the world existed, if Jaehyun had handled it the way he said he did? Why would it work on Haechan and not Mark?

When she doesn't answer, Mark cleared his throat, "there's a lot you don't know about him," he adds quietly, the tone of someone who had much more to say but was choosing not to. "I'm sorry you got caught up in his bullshit, you didn't deserve it, any of it."

A part of her wanted to ask him about her brother, about the truth, about what he meant when he kept saying things like that, but she didn't. She just sighed, nodding once, "thanks, Mark."

Then, she'd offered him a slight wave with two fingers, before leaving the class to head to the one she was dreading the most.

Despite Statistics running a bit longer, she arrived for Media & Society slightly early anyway, her laptop open by the time other students started filtering in. She tried not to look at the empty seat next to her, thinking about the person who usually sat it in, since the first class. Even as the room filled, the seat remained empty.

She tried to distract herself waiting for the professor by reading over the notes she had for the last few classes. But every word she read seemed to make her think about him, about the oddly philosophical conversations they'd have, the moments where he felt like a genuine, real person hidden underneath the mask of someone who wanted to be someone else, who was afraid of how the world would react to the true sight of him.

The professor began at eleven fifteen, going over what they'd be talking about that day before beginning the lecture itself. She had her notes open yet her fingers didn't move across the keyboard. In reality, she was only processing a minor percentage of anything the professor was saying.

"She looks so sad, I wonder what happened."

"It was Jaehyun, haven't you heard?"

"Yeah, but did you see her with Mark this morning?"

She focused on rubbing her temples to try to tune out of the whispers, when the door to the room had opened. Solar knew who it was, she didn't have to look. The room shifted slightly, the small, collective recalibration that happened when people noticed someone coming in late, especially when it was this someone.

Haechan was making his way in the room, one of his many hoodies on, the particular register of him she'd learned to read turned down to something she'd never seen before from the time she'd known him, like a piece of him was missing. His eyes didn't look around, didn't scan the room, he just made his way up the steps, shrugging off any girl that tried to talk to him, and took a seat in the back without making eye contact with anyone.

He almost looked like a different person. She tried not to fight the urge to turn around, to look right at him, to get up and go sit next to him and ask what's wrong with you.

Of course, she wouldn't.

Two rows over, two girls angled their heads toward each other, looking back every few seconds to peer at Solar, before looking to Haechan, the posture of a conversation happening below the volume of the lecture.

"He looks bad," one was mumbling to the other, "I heard him and Jaemin had people over last night, did you get invited?"

"What? No," the other was gawking, "so he's available again? What happened to him and Solar?"

"Girl, who cares," the other scoffed, sparing her another glance of disgust, as if she wasn't even a person, "she's busy with Mark, clearly. Haechan already posted that Sigma's open tonight again."

"For real? Hold on, let me message him."

Her fingers stopped typing without her registering that fact, her eye twitched slightly, and she had to squeeze them shut for a mere second to regain the tiniest ounce of her sanity. Her eyes find the board again, where she would intently stare for the next twenty minutes, trying not to think so hard about best if we call it here somewhere behind her sternum. You knew, didn't you? her brain seemed to yell at her, replaying the conversation the girls were having in her head like a bad dream.

You should've known, Mark told you, everyone told you, but you did it anyway.

That was the problem. She'd always do it anyway.

When the room started to move, Solar was already up, bag on her shoulder, bursting out of the door before most students had even processed the lecture ended. The October air slammed into her face as she pattered down the outside steps, standing there at the bottom for a second. Finally, she could breath, though she had no idea where she was really planning to go then, since that was her last class for the day. All she knew was that she couldn't be in that room for a second longer.

-

Monday eSports practice began at six, but Solar was there by five thirty. The gaming lab had it's own particular atmosphere in the hour before a session, the hum of everything powering up, RGB settling into its usual glow, the specific smell of too many PCs in one room that she had accumulated enough hours in by now to find comforting. Minji was already in her chair, next to Solar's, and had been there since moments before her.

She didn't say anything more than a greeting as Solar sat down and got settled, didn't push or ask any questions. Minji likely already knew what was going on, whether it was from the gossip or by talking to someone like Hanni or Hyein, since she had classes with them. It was the thing about Minji that made Solar feel warm inside, a different kind, the ability to be present without requiring anything in return.

"You nervous?" she'd asked eventually, after the two girls had finished logging into their accounts and were now waiting for everyone else to arrive.

Solar exhaled, leaning back into the leather gaming chair and turning her head to meet the soft eyes of the dark haired girl. Minji, she couldn't help but ponder, the effortlessly simple Minji. She wondered then, why a part of her wasn't just happy being with someone like Minji? She wondered, why a part of her continued to long for something more?

She knew why, and she knew she could never accept what it meant to have feelings for a girl.

So, she tried not to let herself focus on the crinkles at the corner of her eyes or the way her lips were the lightest shade of pink.

"Never," she shakes her head, offering a small smile, "wanna run warm ups?"

Minji smiles in return, nodding her head a single time, before turning to invite her to the group. The two began to play like it was the only thing in life that mattered. Minji didn't ask her anything, even despite the fact they never really talked about what happened between them. She knew Minji wasn't an idiot, that she probably heard things, saw them online, but still, she never looked at Solar any differently.

The door to the lab opened behind them while they were playing, though neither of them turned around, presuming it was one of their teammates or Doyoung. Solar didn't even think about the presence in the room as she continued to flick her mouse, fingers dancing across the keyboard.

It was the sight out of the corner of her eye of a Dutch Bros being set down next to their PC setup that caused her to halt in her tracks. Luckily, the game was ending, causing Solar to whip her head up in confusion.

"Strawberry refresher," he hums, "and a bagel, I know you didn't eat."

She was entirely stuck in a daze for a second, brain lagging as it tried to catch up with what was happening. Minji's eyes were burning holes in the back of her head, and she cleared her throat as she looked down at the drink, before back to Mark, "how do you know that?" she dared to ask, feeling too awkward to address any kind of elephant.

"Hanni," he shrugs, like it was no big deal he was casually talking to her other friends.

"Shoulda known," Solar mumbles, though she was smiling anyway. The act of kindness did warm her heart, even if she hated to admit it.

By then, the others had funneled inside, whispering or talking normally. She knew Chenle was going to struggle holding in some kind of comment, and she could hear Olivia's snickers from a few seats over as she chucked her bag onto the floor with a thud.

"Thanks," she finally tells him, despite how self conscious the eyes of her teammates was making her.

Mark just nodded, leaning gently against the back of her chair and looking at her screen, clearly trying to contemplate what he was looking at. He still had practically no knowledge of the game, no matter how many times he tried watching her play.

"Yeah, this is awful," Chenle couldn't hold it in anymore, "this guy seriously drove to Dutch Bros."

Olivia, who had been holding a hand over her mouth to hold in her laughter, bursted into it anyway, the bright noise booming against the padded walls. Her laugh was so painfully obnoxious sounding that Haerin started giggling, too, which was fairly unlike her, but the situation was so awkward they couldn't help but make fun of it.

"Yo, I was in the area," Mark defended with that dorky chuckle, "it's not that deep, bro."

"Uhuh," Chenle snorts. He had another smart comment to make, but before he could, Doyoung entered the room, already carrying his clipboard and looking down at it in deep thought.

He'd stopped in the middle of the room, like he always did, opening his mouth to begin to debrief them on what they'd be focusing on that day. Everyone knew it was going to be intense, nonetheless, with the tournament coming up this week.

Until the coach had looked up and seen Mark Lee standing there in his lab.

"Absolutely not," he deadpans immediately, "Mark, no, you're aware of the policy, aren't you? Don't play stupid."

Mark had slowly turned around, feigning innocence, "Coach, I was just dropping off..."

"...Get out, Mark," Doyoung cut him off, unimpressed, pointing to the door with the end of his pen, "you can see her after, right now she needs focus."

Mark, to his credit, didn't argue, but just held up his hands like a man who'd been caught doing something technically legal, which produced another round of muffled sounds from Olivia and Haerin that could only be described as poorly hidden giggles. His eyes landed on Solar one last time, before he'd sent her a slight nod and a farewell with two fingers before making his way out the door.

"Bring me a bagel next time, too!" Chenle called mockingly as he left, in a tone that communicated he was not finished with the topic.

She was still smiling to herself as she sipped her strawberry drink.

\@markylee:\ maybe

Tuesdays were a safe day. No Business class with Danielle pointed an imaginary gun at her head, no Media & Society where she had to watch Haechan stumble up the steps and girls hound him like a lost puppy, none of the rooms that had recently become exhibits with her in them as if she was a zoo animal. She had a single morning lecture on the farther side of campus, one with complete strangers in it, making it the closest thing her week had to neutral territory.

The plan was simple, she was going to sit in the lecture and pretend that she wasn't busy thinking about a thousand other things, then she was going to go home and practice. After all, the tournament was coming up horrifyingly quickly, yet also not fast enough.

October was becoming something chillier, the type of cold that got into the gap between her scarf and jaw. She exhaled deeply to prepare herself for this truth as she shoulders open the door, leaving the building her class had been in and heading out into the Syracuse air.

She'd only made it a couple of steps down the stairs when she noticed the presence to the side of them, leaning against the low brick wall, one ankle crossed over the other, a drink carrier balanced on the ledge beside him with two cups in it. Although they'd texted earlier, there was still an element of surprise in her eyes as Mark had looked up to meet them, that dorky grin spreading over his lips as he pushes off the wall.

"Morning," he calls out, holding up the drink carrier, "I've come barring gifts."

Solar stops at the bottom of the steps, knowing well that her Tuesday lecture was a fifteen minute walk in the opposite direction of anywhere Mark Lee had a reason to be, on top of the fact she knew his schedule well enough to know Tuesdays were studio mornings for him, the same sacred ones he complained about missing if fraternity things came up instead. It floated in her mind for a moment, causing her to stand there in thought, before she shrugs it off, looking up at the boy who was now standing before her, holding out the drink to her with a grin on his lips.

"What happened to your beloved studio mornings?" she hummed casually, raising a brow as she took the cold drink out of his hand. It was a coffee rather than a refresher, he seemed to know that she didn't like to drink coffee late at night and preferred a refresher instead, whereas during the day, coffee was her go-to.

It made her smile a bit as she took a sip of the cold drink.

"Producer cancelled on me," he shrugs, "figured you'd be caffeinating alone and could use some company."

"You're not wrong," she returns, "thank you, Mark." It was a genuine thanks, because she needed someone to be kind to her right now, to take her mind off of everything else, even if it was Mark Lee, the boy who she wasn't sure she understood fully yet, the boy who was directly connected to the one she was trying so hard not to think about.

"Come on," he motions, though by the way his cheeks were slightly pink, it was clear he processed her gratefulness, "I'll walk you."

"You sure?" she pends, "It's fifteen minutes back to Alpha." The sorority was indeed on the other side of campus, but Mark didn't appear to care as he was already a few feet away.

"I'm aware," he beams, "I like walking, Sol."

He did not like walking, however. In fact, Mark had often complained about the hill by the extracurricular building, the way other people complained about their tax returns. Yet, he fell into step beside her anyway, the two of them moving down the path under the half barren trees, Solar sipping away at her iced coffee, listening to Mark talk about something that he's studying in one of his composition classes. He was passionate about it, the whole music thing, he loved to talk about what he was working on, how many listens his newest song had on SoundCloud, how a bigger artist liked one of his covers on TikTok. He was good at carrying the conversation, in an easy way, one that allowed her to tune it out and just enjoy the moment without thinking too hard.

For a rare second, she wasn't thinking so much about everything. That was, until she caught the glances from the corner of her eyes, a group of girls by a bike rack, heads angling together as they passed. Then, there were two guys outside the dining hall, one of the saying something with his eyes on her, before they started snickering to each other like it was peak comedy. A girl she vaguely recalled from a first week mixer made eye contact with her as she walked by, before deliberately ripping her eyes away and pulling out her phone with pursed lips.

Her fingers gripped the cold cup tighter without her noticing, the condensation shivering against her palm, but her face remained emotionless, entirely calm.

It wasn't like this was abnormal, she was used to being stared at by now, to being talked about as if she was an exhibit. She'd been Jaehyun's little sister since her first day in August, and before that, she was the youngest Lee daughter. But this, this was a different frequency, her body clocked the difference before she could name it, a prickle starting at the back of her neck that had nothing to do with the cold of her drink. The thing was, the looks weren't about her, they were about the evident space between her and Mark Lee, the way they were so close together, the way he looked at her with that crinkle at the corner of his eyes.

By the time she focused back on whatever Mark was saying, she was far too lost. "So, he goes, that's not jazz, and I was like," he pauses, "brother, nothing you've ever made is jazz, pipe down, man," he chuckles at his own story, his laugh quiet on the morning path, but loud enough that a girl by the library turned to peer at them in curiosity.

She giggled, though it wasn't as real as she wanted it to be. If Mark noticed the audience, it didn't show, or it showed the way things simply did on Mark, in his posture, in the way he always seemed to have that dorky grin on, like he was too good being the center of attention.

Like he wanted it that way.

-
