It wasn't like she had no idea he'd taken the photo. Mark always wanted to take photos when they were together, it was the part she kept returning to as she laid there on top of her comforter, with her phone above her face, the post open. Her eyes couldn't seem to stop staring, at the way she was smiling like she had no cares, the way he was seemingly grinning as if he was proud of himself, the suggestive caption that seemed to say far more than she would've liked. She knew he took the photos to post them, whether it was on his Instagram or Snapchat, he always seemed to have his phone out doing something. This wasn't super weird to her, he did post a lot, yet, there was something sitting at the bottom of her stomach as her eyes scanned across the various comments left under the post.

Never had she cared enough to look at any comments, even though his posts always got quite a few. All of the fraternity boys had a larger amount of followers than you'd expect, with most of them being campus girls that had crushes on them. She'd never felt compelled to look at them, to see what people were saying, but right then, something tempted her.

they're cute im crine

from one lee to another ICANTTT

wasnt she ltrly just with haechan LMFAO solar girl wyd

girlie is living the dream

sis has a type ig

solar trying to cop all the musicians or what?

nah cus sunews was right this do feel like deja vu

She closes the app before she could second guess it, turning off her phone and throwing it to the side.

It was sweet, is what she tried to tell herself, which wasn't entirely untrue. He'd brought her the right drink, walked her back to Alpha, made her laugh against her will, every piece of it was kind, she had no vocabulary for why the assembled whole sat on her chest in the way it did.

Her hand reached for her phone again out of habit, thumb swiping and tapping somewhere she tended to go when she got too deep in thought. The thread was where she'd left it, with her last texts left on delivered like nothing, her eyes peering up at the best if we call it here taunting her like a bad nightmare. She'd read the words enough times now that they no longer looked like words.

She wasn't going to admit it to anyone, but it still hurt, whether she wanted it to or not.

But she forced herself to set her phone down on the nightstand and go to sleep as if all was fine.

-

Wednesday morning, the kitchen smelled like Danielle's entryway spray had somehow gotten more ambitious, floral and sharp, reaching all the way to the refrigerator. Solar was reaching into it for her sparkling water when she heard the trickling of the coffee machine behind her. She didn't have to turn around to know, because the coffee machine wasn't the only thing in the room producing a low hum of self-satisfaction.

"You're looking better," Danielle offers, though it was very far from a compliment, delivered in the tone of someone updating a chart.

"Sleep does wonders," Solar deadpans, twisting the cap of her water to take a drink, leaning her back against the kitchen counter.

"The walks are cute," she hums, "I sure hope Jaehyun doesn't catch wind, you know, that you're still hanging around him."

She took another slow drink of her water, letting the bubbles pop on her tongue. Everything Danielle said seemed to have corners on it, that was her whole architecture, the way that she always knew more in ways that made no sense. She was trying not to be unsettled by it, though the more she learned, the more nervous it made her deep down.

"He doesn't use social media," she informs, "you know that, don't you?"

"Mm, he seems to find out either way, though, doesn't he?" she retorts, raising a brow and crossing her arms over her chest, leaning against the kitchen counter across from her. "I'm glad you made such a quick recovery, either way."

"Was I on a timeline?" she pends sarcastically, "Don't tell me, you've been counting?"

Despite her mocking tone, Danielle was unbothered. "Everyone is on a timeline at SU, Solar," she shakes her head, as if she felt sympathetic, "some of us just read faster."

Then, she pushed off the counter, after pouring her mug of coffee. Her footsteps echo against the tiled floor as she makes her way to the doorway, before she'd turned to meet her eyes on last time.

"I'd be careful if I were you," she advises, "they've all got their dark sides."

She was gone before Solar could decide the proper response to get the last word in, leaving her standing there in contemplation like she always did with her odd riddles. The floral spray hung there, in the air, the way it had been for weeks.

Her lips formed a straight line, dark sides, she thought. She thought about everything she knew about Mark, the scholarship story, the way Haechan had texted her that day saying he knows she hates the attention, that he does it on purpose.

Why should I trust him? she shook the thought away, reminding herself what he did, that all of it was fake, it had to be.

Right?

But something was telling her otherwise.

\@hyein:\ love u

Danielle was creepily silent in Intro to Business, which seemed to tell Solar enough on its own. She'd spent the walk to class that morning, after noticing she was absent from the kitchen and feeling a glimmer of hope that she'd gotten mysteriously sick and was holed up in her room. But as she sat there, in her seat, Danielle simply didn't look at her a single time, seated not next to her, but three rows up with her posture perfect, pen moving. It was somehow worse, she realized, feeling less like a ceasefire and more like someone who'd already finished the paragraph and moved onto the next one.

I'd be careful if I were you remained in the back of her head with all of the other weird things that had been said to her, along with the thought of dark sides with no explanation attached.

Still, the stares never got any better.

Statistics, at the very least, had Mark in it, who didn't mention the post for the first five minutes, which she clocked without meaning to. He'd slid into his seat with the usual greeting, throwing a granola bar in front of her gently without a word.

But then five minutes passed, and he'd hummed, "you saw that stupid post, right? Don't read the comments, people are being so dumb."

She pursed her lips for a second, shrugging once, "I glanced at them, I don't know why..." she trails off, looking back at the projector as the professor switched slides, "something compelled me, I guess."

"Sol," he urges, slightly disappointed but more worried.

"They're calling it a switch, like it's a phone plan or something," she mutters, her eyes still focused on the screen, though she could see him settle out of the corner of her eye, the specific way his shoulders cam down, "it's just disrespectful, I'm tired of people treating my life like a TV show."

"Hey," he voices softly, "it'll blow over, alright?"

It was sincere, like he really wanted to make her feel better. She could tell, or so she thought, in the way he said it, the way he looked down at her. She met his eyes and almost felt a ping in her chest, but she sighed instead. A part of her couldn't help but think there was something in the delivery that'd been ironed in advance, and she felt herself standing at a small distance from the moment, peering at it, because a boy whose face was currently all over an anonymous gossip account seemed like he should mind way more than this. Yet, it was like he didn't care at all, but it was the underneath that read as calm rather than the surface.

He does it on purpose, but you're smart enough to know that, echoed through her mind before she could stop it. She bites down on the inside of her cheek, nodding once at Mark as she'd turned her head to focus back on the projector. She had nothing more to say, because she was having a harder time ignoring that feeling in her stomach.

It was when class had ended and most of the students shuffled out that Mark spoke up again, while Solar was grabbing her things and packing them away in her bag.

"So, Friday," he clears his throat, "Sigma's doing the usual, I was wondering if you wanted to hang out... you know, away from the noise?"

Away from the noise seemed to do some kind of trick on her brain. Perhaps, something like that was what she was craving, even though the two of them had been doing a lot of hanging out that week.

"Maybe," she shrugs, though a small smile tugged at the corners of her lips, "we'll see."

Mark beamed from ear to ear at her words. He smiled then, big and dorky, "maybe happens to be my favorite yes," he chirps, causing her to roll her eyes as she threw her bag over her shoulder.

"See you, Mark."

She could feel his eyes watching her as she left, she knew he wanted to stop her, to ask if he could walk her back to her next class, but he didn't. It was like a part of him knew she still wasn't doing well, that she needed her space, that she didn't have the full capacity to be anything for him. So, he let her go, tapping his fingers against the wooden desk, lips pursed in deep thought, or perhaps calculation.

He didn't show up at all to Media & Society.

Maybe a part of her wasn't surprised, and another part was even a little concerned. She knew what people were whispering about, obviously, she'd heard the rumors, that he was back to his old ways or that he never changed them in the first place. A part of her believed Mark, at least she thought she believed him, until she'd think back to the way he'd smile in closed quarters, how he'd laughed that night on the rooftop, telling her things he'd never seemingly told anyone before, he didn't act like someone who slept with girls every night, who did drugs like their life depended on it.

Yet, it was all a game? It felt like a sick nightmare.

She tried to tune out the whispers that day, to focus on her notes and pretend she truly cared about whatever the professor was talking about.

Once class ended, she'd bolted out of the room before any of her classmates could ask her weird questions, her bag slung over her shoulder, eyes looking straight ahead, with the determination of someone on a mission. In reality, she was simply on a mission to go home, change clothes, and head straight to practice. As much as she loved Hanni and Hyein, the two of them were so worried about her that sometimes, they almost made her think about it more rather than less. So, she was glad to go spend time with her eSports teammates, who often only talked about the game or bantered with each other instead of anything real life related.

A deep exhale leaves her lips, sneakers scraping against the concrete as she walked down the path. Abruptly, she'd made the executive decision to take the long way back to Alpha Phi, deciding she needed the fresh air.

Although there may have been a deeper reason, she wasn't going to think about that. So, she walked, allowing herself to only think about the strategy Doyoung had been hammering into their skulls all week, when she felt the slightest chill run up her spine.

It was the diagonal path, the far one, by the Language Arts Building, of all buildings, the same one whose roof she still hadn't told anyone about. She wouldn't have seen him at all if she hadn't glanced up at the exact wrong moment, some ancient instinct internally announcing him before her eyes could catch up. He wasn't looking at anything in particular, his hood was up, headphones on, the expensive ones that were calibrated, as he claimed. Haechan was moving through the cold like it was someone else's problem, entirely indifferent, two girls directly behind him, carrying a conversation he was visibly not apart of.

It'd only lasted four seconds, he hadn't seen her, or he had, and simply was better at hiding it than her, which was a possibility she declined examining. On the fourth second, he was gone, disappearing behind the corner of the building, leaving her standing in the middle of the path with her drink going watery in her hand, chest doing the thing, the tightening, the disobedient pull it'd been doing since a certain Friday.

He looked thinner, it was the observation her body had collected without her permission, thinner and gray around the edges, in a way that had nothing to do with the weather, like sound was the only thing he could still stand, the girls at his shoulder registering as mere ghosts he had no care for.

Good, is what the girl from September would've thought, the one that heard the rumors, warnings, and captions and thought yeah, that sounds accurate, he's got his little fan club back.

Everyone's exactly where they belong, she tried to tell herself, Mark was right.

She stood there for another few seconds, staring at an empty corner, hating the arithmetic. Something was wrong, she could feel it in her veins, because boys who were where they belonged, doing what they've always done, being everything the rumors promised, didn't look like that. She'd seen Haechan unbothered, with months of data on him being so, the loose jaw, lazy certainty, the way he wore a room like it'd been tailored to him. Whatever had just rounded the corner was wearing the mask, the facade, the outfit, but it wasn't wearing the boy.

-

The thing about tournament week was that it compressed everything else into the margins, which was the entire appeal and exactly what she needed right then. Solar came home that evening to change, that was it. Alpha Phi by Thursday evenings was in its late day lull, someone's blow dryer buzzing upstairs, the entryway reminiscent of Danielle's floral spray, sharp enough that her sinuses filed a complaint. She moved through the house on autopilot, up the stairs, head full of composition, rotations, whether Doyoung was going to run them past ten again, citing that they had to be perfect.

What stopped her, as she brushed her hair lazily in the bathroom upstairs, was the sight of a sticky note on the mirror. It wasn't the note itself, as they'd become normal to her around Alpha, it was one of Chungha's systems, things like chore rotations, package pickups, quiet hours, a small administrative weather that everyone in the house simply lived under. This one was seemingly nothing, all it said was chapter meeting moved to sunday, ATTENDANCE MANDATORY ladies!!

She stared at the handwriting, feeling something unsettling in her bones, she recognized it, the way you recognized any of your housemates handwriting by October.

Solar stood in front of the mirror for much longer than the note deserved, bag still hanging off her shoulder. The smoke detector that had been chirping at the back of her skull all day suddenly sounded closer, one room over now, like the puzzle was almost finished, almost assembled.

We hope it was worth it!!

The caption repeated in her head, the post from the morning after, her walking home with her red bottom heels in one hand and a look of determination written across her face, one the camera didn't catch because she hadn't known she was being photographed. Two exclamation points a sentence, the punctuation of fake warmth, then there was the caption from this morning, bounce back. She could see the devious smile wiped across Danielle's lips as she uttered, "wow, you look rough. Long weekend?" before she'd giggled out, "I'm glad you made such a quick recovery," in the tone of someone that seemed to know everything without being told a word.

Some girls really do bounce back better than others.

Her reflection was peering back at her from the mirror, the sticky note at the corner. She watched her own face do what she could feel herself do in scrims when a fight was turned around to winnable, the stillness, the narrowing.

It wasn't proof, per se, she knew what proof was. After all, she was the daughter of a man whose lawyers billed in six minute increments, and a shared fondness for exclamation points and some vocabulary overlap was not proof, it was pattern, and pattern was what people called evidence when they didn't have any yet. It was no lie to say half of the campus wrote like that, used the same slang, the entirety of Alpha performed warmth in that exact font. From her eyes, if she said it aloud, to anyone other than Hanni and Hyein, it'd just sound like a girl who'd had a bad two weeks, assembling a villain out of a mean treasurer and a gossip account. She could see it then, Danielle tilting her head, saying something fake sweet with sharp corners, before walking out of the exchange looking like the reasonable one.

He seems to find out either way, though, doesn't he?

The words tasted like venom on her tongue.

-

Very rarely did Sigma Chi ever quiet down, which was something everyone learned the moment they stepped into the house. Although it might be a different frequency with the added presence of Jaehyun compared to the chaos of the Friday night parties, it was still just as loud.

The kitchen at eleven that night had many scenes to it, Renjun at the cracked window with what he deadpanned would be his last cigarette of the day, Jeno eating cold pasta out of the container standing up, and Chenle, who had been talking for eleven consecutive minutes. It wasn't that this was unlike him, despite how he appeared to the general public, he liked to talk when it was about something he felt passionate about, especially around people he was close with, like his housemates.

It was safe to say that Chenle felt very passionate about Overwatch.

"The point is," he was saying, mid-explanation of informing the boys about the game, as if they had any idea what he was talking about in the first place, as none of them knew much more than a single thing about the likes of Overwatch, "their Widow is an issue, we're all clearly aware of such. And the solution is practically laying in front of us, yet, no one wants to listen to me," he shook his head, clearly rather frustrated at the thought, "All we have to do is have our backline track the early rotations of the supports." His words were exasperated, the way a professor talked to a student they thought was lesser than them. To add to this display, he began drawing diagrams in the air with a random fork that was lying in front of them, "it's quite literally free, blatant free real estate in front of our faces."

"Sounds rough," Renjun voiced from the window after taking another drag, he was obviously not paying attention.

"Dude, I have no clue what you just said," Jeno giggles, mouth full of pasta, "but yeah bro."

Chenle exhaled, shaking his head in disappointment at the lack of intelligence from his friends. "Well, nonetheless, Saturday is going to be a movie," he announces firmly.

"You already said that," Renjun mumbled.

"It's the first tournament in program history, after all," he continues, not looking at either of them and just mindlessly staring at the ceiling in thought. By now, he was more talking to himself, until he'd seem to finish his thought, making eye contact with the two of them, "It's Saturday, be there or nothing."

It wasn't often times Chenle invited people anywhere, he was someone who did not make himself known, he was reserved until he was around people he enjoyed and trusted into his very small circle. He didn't originally want to be in a fraternity, at least in the beginning, it was something his parents forced him into, telling him it would be good for him, to help him get out of his bubble. At first, Chenle was pissed, the last thing he wanted to do at college, he thought, was be around a bunch of drug addicted psychopaths.

However, he soon came to learn, despite how long it took, that life in Sigma Chi wasn't so bad at all. Sure, there were some people who made rather questionable decisions, the party culture was frankly disgusting and appalling compounded, but at the end of the day, they were right. Being a member of Sigma Chi did help him get out of his shell, and he'd made some friends, whether they were questionable or not, in the process.

So, he decided, he would enjoy if they attended the first thing on campus he had really cared about.

"Dang man, I have a thing at noon," Jeno monotones, but he was a terrible liar, which was written all over his face.

"Dude, you do not have anything at noon," Renjun scoffed at him, before looking back to Chenle and shrugging, putting out his cigarette, "I'll come, Le."

Chenle smiled from that, close lipped, "you know, I appreciate you, Renjun," he nods solemnly, "and fuck you, Jeno."

Haechan had entered somewhere in the middle of the conversation, the way he entered rooms nowadays, without changing the pressure of them. His hood was down, a glass of water in his hand that Renjun's eyes went to but didn't comment on, the same way Renjun's eyes had been going to things and not commenting on them for weeks now. For once, he didn't say anything, didn't make any kind of comment, he just crossed to the fridge, no one making an effort to make him apart of the conversation, which was the current arrangement of the house.

No one was exactly surprised that he had begun acting like himself again. That was how he'd always been, since the first day he and Mark arrived to Sigma Chi. For all they ever knew, that was Haechan, or so they'd thought.

But it was clear that the boy they'd noticed gradually shift over the last few months seemed more genuine and real than the one before him.

Chenle briefly knew what was happening, just by his own conclusions, based off the way Solar had been recently, combined with him seeing Haechan back in his old ways. He found himself, unconvincingly, casually continuing his talk as if he didn't know Haechan was there, "Solar's been impressive in recent scrims, unfair, actually. Doyoung had her shot call the entire runback on Tuesday, she sees stuff before it happens, dude. I'd be surprised if we lose a single map on Saturday."

The fridge closed, sound echoing through the tiled kitchen. It wasn't loud, necessarily, it was a complete ordinary fridge sound, leaving Haechan standing there with his water and an orange he'd had no memory of deciding on. Though, recently, he was struggling with remembering anything in the first place. His back was half to the room, but none of the boys were looking at him, which was the reason nobody saw whatever his face did or didn't do for the half-second it did or didn't do it.

"Noon," he deadpans suddenly, not as a question, causing Chenle to pause whatever he was talking about now.

The blonde boy had stopped, slowly setting his fork down. "Uhuh, why're you asking?" Of course, he knew why he was asking. He didn't have a reason for doing what he did, at least, that's what he would tell himself. But Chenle was smarter than most people, and perhaps that meant he was better at seeing their character, at seeing the truth, than others. He knew what happened between Mark and Haechan, the way everyone else in the house knew because they witnessed it with their own two eyes, they watched it happen in realtime, the chaos unfolding, the drama that ensued, and most of all, the aftermath.

It wasn't something any of them spoke about. Jaehyun didn't want them to, it was a private matter so private they weren't supposed to even talk about it amongst themselves. For the most part, everyone had forgotten about it, it was simply dirty laundry no one cared about anymore.

That was, until Solar entered the picture, until Jaehyun sat four of them down, looked at Mark and Haechan, and reminded him that he evidently vividly recalls what happened last year. Naturally, in a place like Sigma, the word travelled fast between brothers. Only then, did the boys begin to process the fact that history's never really forgotten.

It was repeated instead.

And Chenle, at least, could see it clear as day. Maybe that was why a part of him did it, at the end of the day.

"No reason," the boy deadpans, sipping his water a single time, "just shocked people are so excited about it." He doesn't seem like he had anything more to say then, nodding his head at Jeno, "blunt?"

Jeno had brightened up then, setting his container down instantly, "fuck yeah, bro!"

The two of them left then, Haechan with the orange he didn't want, upstairs where they'd be smoking. He was doing it for a reason, he needed to stop his thinking, to get out of his room, which was dark, where the laptop was open like it always was, the same project file sitting in its silence, the cursor parked mid-bar like a held breath.

He told himself he was going to numb himself until he forgot completely, that he was going to go back to the way it always was.

But that track wouldn't stop staring at him.

He hadn't said much to Jeno when they smoked, which Jeno didn't mind or note in the first place. He was too busy talking about the girls he'd hooked up with recently, something the two boys had once bonded over.

Now, it just made him feel nauseous.

By the time he got back in his room, he stood in the middle of it for a second, brain in between racing and being empty. He still had the orange in his hand, padding across the carpet and setting it down on the desk, next to his headphones.

Haechan had nothing to say, because who would he say it to?

His best friend, who'd seemed to of won the war, who'd hammered it into his skull that the last thing he would ever be was in love with someone? The words alone were enough to make him want to run away and never look back. Mark was the only one he had ever had the urge to try to talk to, but even he wasn't who he thought he was, or maybe he never was the person Haechan imagined in his head.

The truth was, saying things aloud was how they became real, and Haechan had always been very skillful at keeping things unreal. It was his entire persona at Syracuse, after all, one he had originally never thought to question.

Something compelled him to sit down at his desk anyway. His eyes landed, briefly, on the bag of powder next to his mousepad.

He pursed his lips in contemplation, before his dark eyes moved to the screen instead. Suddenly, the urge went away.

And his cursor moved in return.

44: the project

Friday morning, Danielle was back in the kitchen, which Solar decided was somehow almost preferable to the silence. The reason being, she'd thought about it in bed last night, staring at the ceiling with the tournament comps running on one track of her brain and the sticky note on the other. Then, somewhere around midnight, she'd landed on a conclusion that felt very much like a shot call.

It wasn't that she was going to accuse, not yet, that's what Danielle wanted, a girl unraveling in the kitchen with no proof, easy to tilt her head at. But there was a difference between an accusation and a mere test, the same difference between committing to a fight versus poking one, checking cooldowns, seeing what the enemy backline did when pressure brushed up against it.

So, when she came downstairs and found Danielle at the coffee machine, mug already under the spout, posture already perfect at seven in the morning, she took her sparkling water out of the fridge, twisted the cap, and leaned against the counter like everything was normal and entirely fine.

For once, Danielle didn't speak first, because Solar beat her to it, before she could conjure whatever pointed comment she was going to make.

"Saw a post yesterday," she hums, conversational, eyes on the bubbles rising in the bottle, "the caption was funny, like so familiar, like I literally just heard it the other day..." She trailed off, pretending to be in deep thought about this fact.

The coffee machine trickled on, Danielle didn't turn around. There was no visible stress in her shoulders, nothing in the angle of her neck, no pause in the administrative motions of her hands, it was the thing Solar was learning about her, that she was never caught anywhere, that every room she stood in seemed to have been prepared in advance.

"Well," she exhales, "people repeat things, Solar, it's what makes them people, phrases get around." Her tone was pleasant, like a parent gently advising their child, reaching for the sugar in the cabinet.

"Mm," Solar returns, taking a sip of her sparkling water obnoxiously loud, "I don't know, it sure was awfully fast."

"It's a small campus," Danielle advises. She turned then, mug in both hands, and her smile was already assembled, the corners of it sitting exactly where they always sat, "you'd be surprised what travels."

"Would I?"

"Mhm," she nods, taking a slow sip of her coffee, peering at Solar over the rim. She met her gaze, and for a second, the kitchen held the specific silence of two people agreeing not to name the thing they were both holding. "You must be excited for tomorrow," she pivots then, like they were two sisters who actually liked one another, "the whole school's talking about it," she was almost gushing, before her tone became more sinister, "and Mark will be there, I assume? You're practically a package deal this week."

She had to bite the inside of her cheek. "He's a friend," she affirms, trying to hide how much it annoyed her.

"Course," she agrees, "I do wonder if Jaehyun sees it that way, you being glued to Mark, I mean, especially after that whole meeting."

The bottle stopped halfway to Solar's mouth. She set it down on the counter, slow and deliberate, buying her face the half-second it needed to regain it's composure. The smoke detector in the back of her skull wasn't chirping now, it was ringing, a continuous note. Test the backline and see what pressure does, she reminded herself, except the pressure had come back the wrong direction, and it knew things it had no path to knowing.

"What meeting might you be talking about?" she pends, innocently.

Danielle was unimpressed, "Solar," she chides, gently, the way you'd correct a child's spelling, which was ten times worse than any answer could've been.

Giving up on the dumb act, Solar crosses her arms over her chest, leaning against the counter and propping her foot against it behind her. "Alright, I'll bite, Danielle," she deadpans, "how would you know anything about a Sigma meeting?"

Danielle pondered it for a second, before she shrugged, one shoulder, unhurried, and picked off an invisible piece of lint off her sleeve. "I know things," she states, simply.

And then, she was moving, mug in hand, footsteps soft on the tile, the floral spray lingering behind her like a signature. She paused at the doorway, the way she always paused at doorways, because exits were where she kept her best material.

"Good luck tomorrow," she coos, without turning around, "noon, isn't it?"

She was gone before Solar could say anything, leaving her standing in the kitchen with her water going flat by now, doing the thing she swore she wasn't going to do, which was rethinking through each moment frame by frame as if it was a lost fight. She'd poked the backline, and something had reached out of the dark before tapping her twice on the shoulder, politely, mockingly, just to show her that it could.

But there was more than that. Underneath the ringing, a colder observation was quietly surfacing, nothing from that living room had ever surfaced, not a word, not on SUnews or the newer SUanon account, not in the whispers, not anywhere, in a school where her coffee order was now public record. Whatever Jaehyun had sealed, he'd sealed it completely, which either meant Danielle didn't have the details, not in full, or she was saving them for the perfect time to use them.

-

She stared down at the smiley face for a second longer than it deserved. It was the right response, that was the thing, always the right response. He was gracious about the no, supportive about the reason, funny in the right dosage. If she showed the messages to anyone on campus, they'd tell her she was nuts for the thing her stomach did anyway, like a note played one key off.

He'd folded the rejection into a plan to show up somewhere completely more public instead.

Exhaling, she shook it off, about to put her phone down to prepare for her classes that day, before it'd buzzed again in her hand.

-

Friday wasn't a practice day, which was precisely why Doyoung had scheduled one anyway, not that anyone was complaining. It wasn't a real one, per se, he'd been clear about such in the team Discord, no scrims, no queue, just mandatory VOD reviews at seven, then everyone goes home and sleeps like professionals.

The lab at seven had a different texture than usual, the overheads on instead of only the RGB, projector pulled down over the far wall, chairs dragged out of their rows into a loose half circle that made the whole thing feel less like practice, more like a huddle. Doyoung stood at the front, with his clipboard and the particular calm of a man who'd prepared for every outcome except his team being normal.

Still, he cleared his throat, opening, "Tomorrow, you're going to be nervous, that's fine, it means you care," he taps his pen a single time against the clipboard, "but what you're not going to do is let it change how you play for that first fight, because that first fight sets the temperature for the whole map, do we understand?"

Every mutters sounds of agreement. Chenle, who had one leg crossed over the other, raised his hand for a split second before he started talking anyway. "Their Widow is who sets it," he informs, "she's going to be on the high ground before the doors even open, we need to be aware of this."

"We track her rotation," Doyoung nods, as this was something he had thought about before, turning back to the projector, "like we've drilled all week, yes? Are there any other concerns or comments?"

Haerin, who hadn't spoken in the last few slides, raised her hand with the solemnity of a scholar. Doyoung pointed at her with his pen, raising a brow in question.

"So, what if we just shoot her?"

The room took a second to let those words sit. Olivia broke first, the laugh detonating out of her loud enough to rattle the padded walls, before Chenle started chuckling, citing that he couldn't help it because of how irritating Olivia's laugh was. Partly, he was laughing at Haerin's idiocy, but he swore, only partly.

Doyoung put the clipboard in front of his mouth for a moment, before he'd regained his composure, and returned, "yes, Haerin, that's part of the plan."

The rest of the review ran warm in that same register, the nerves burning off into the specific looseness of a team that'd done the work and knew it, Minji calling out one of her own misplays before Doyoung could, "I inted, I know!" she admits loudly, "Captain's gotta make a mistake every now and then, won't happen again." She wasn't just saying it to say it, she meant it.

Olivia narrated the enemy teams kill feed in a sports announcer voice while Chenle mentioned one final Widow point he swore no one else was thinking about, before three of them shushed him at once and he went, "Damn, my bad, incels," and continued looking at the projector like nothing happened.

Solar, meanwhile, sat in the middle of it all, legs pulled up in the chair, letting the goofiness wash over her. Somewhere in the noise, she registered, distantly, that this was the only room on campus where none of the drama could reach her, there were no captions, meetings, doorway exits here. It was just the bracket, compositions, the people who wanted her cooldowns and skill rather than her family name and what came with it.

The thought made her smile to herself.

At eight thirty, Doyoung killed the projector, turning to them with his version of a smile. "Go, hydrate, sleep, as long as you're here and prepared at ten," he instructs. That was supposed to be it, but as the chairs scraped against the floor, he'd softly muttered, "you've earned tomorrow, kids, go get it."

Some of them smiled to themselves, but Olivia couldn't help herself. She saluted him as she walked out, "we got it, coach!"

Solar couldn't help but chuckle as she grabbed her own bag, watching as Minji did the same. Her bag was already sitting on her shoulder, in fact, she'd been waiting patiently for Solar to finish grabbing her things.

"Walk?" she rose a brow, the one word being enough.

She'd only nodded in response, like it should've been obvious, and the two girls leave the lab after waving to their coach goodbye.

Moments later, the night had gotten cold in the way October nights did when they stopped pretending, the kind of cold that made the amber lamps along the path look warmer than they had any right to. Solar and Minji had been walking home through it, their breaths showing up faintly in the air between them, shoulders gently grazing every third step, both unhurried in their paces.

The brunette stole a glance at her then, not entirely hiding the fact, but just let herself take in the likes of Minji Kim. She was simplistic, in the easiest of ways, the way her straight dark hair flowed down her back, how she'd walk with her hands in the pockets of her cargo jeans, a red jacket on her shoulders, one she wore often for no particular reason. Her cap was on frontwards today, which was a bit different for her, and Solar felt a bit embarrassed that she watched her enough to notice.

"So," Minji starts, noticing well that the girl next to her had been staring but not mentioning it, "technically, the debt's settled now."

"What debt?" she blurted, confused.

She chuckled, shaking her head in disbelief that Solar failed to remember. "The walk I cancelled on you," she answers, tipping her head back toward the lab, vaguely, "few weeks ago, film group emergency. You looked so sad."

"I did not look sad," Solar defends immediately.

"You looked like a wet cat, Sol."

"I was tired," she corrects, causing Minji to make a low, skeptical ts sound, the ghost of a smile at the corner of her mouth. The two girls walked on anyway, past the dining hall with its late stragglers, the bike racks, the leaves scraping along ahead of them in the wind like they had somewhere to be.

And the quiet came the way it always did with Minji, needing nothing done about it. That was the thing about her, one that nobody else on the campus seemed to be capable of, the way silence was a place she'd walk with you rather than a gap to be filled or a lead in to a question Solar didn't want to answer. She could feel the week sitting on her, the kitchen, the smiley face, the note under it all like a rock in her shoe, and it was evident Minji felt it, too, the way she read her sideways the way she read angles. But she was kind, genuine enough, to leave it alone.

Instead, she just nudges Solar's shoulder with her own as they walk. "Whatever it is," she offers into the chilly air, "it'll keep until Sunday, tomorrow we win, then celebrate and eat."

"Wow," Solar hums, "sounds very coach-y of you."

She was smiling, biting down on her bottom lip with a careless shrug, "Doyoung's rubbing off on me, it's a problem," she deadpans dramatically, "next week, I'll be buying a clipboard."

Solar giggled, rolling her eyes at Minji's sense of humor that she enjoyed more than she would admit. The wind rustled the leaves above their heads, the concrete scraping against their feet. In the distance, two students could be heard yelling at each other, somewhere in the grass valley.

"You'll be nasty tomorrow," Solar tells her finally, which in the native language of their friendship, was crossed between a prediction and an I love you, and Minji received it with a small nod, like a weather report she agreed with.

"Duh," she monotones, "you will, too," she beams, "always are."

Alpha Phi came up on them sooner than the conversation was ready for, the porch light doing its dull golden thing, the door that never failed to stick. The two slowed at the bottom of the walkway out of ritual, the place where Minji would peel off toward the honors dorm. They stopped next to that same lamp post, the one that flickered and no one cared enough to fix.

And neither of them seemed to know what to say for a minute.

"Sleep, superstar," Minji finally instructs, "ten AM, you know."

"Don't act like you're not gonna go home and queue," Solar retorts, playfully.

"I might," she shrugs, "wanna join?"

Her smile was gleaming down at her from her taller height. Solar had to bite her lip to try to contain her smile, and maybe herself. "What happened to the captain energy?" she wrinkled her nose, "Now you're sounding like a bad influence."

She chuckled, "did it work on you?"

Solar shook her head instantly, extending both of her arms to playfully push Minji towards the path, where she'd decided she desperately needed to start walking down. "Shut up, Ji," she hisses in fake annoyance, "you might need that sleep more than I do."

"Hey, don't talk to your captain that way," she defends, innocently, before something else seemed to pop into her head. "Oh," she gasps, "aced the project, by the way."

The sudden subject change caught Solar off guard, causing her brows to furrow. "Which one?"

"The one of you," she explains, casually, and only then did the memories start to intrude on her thoughts like a virus. The feeling of her lips began to feel too prominent, combined with the way she was looking at her, causing Solar to have to break their eye contact.

But when she looked up again, Minji was already walking away, she was half turned to glance back at her, but didn't slow down. "Night, Sol!" is all she calls back, not allowing her to get another word in because she was still trying to figure out what to even say.

She was gone, then, behind the hedge, her camera bag bumping against her hip. Only then, did she even notice she had it, and she was beginning to realize the fact she always had it on her. The girls footsteps faded up the path, as if the sentence she'd uttered was nothing at all.

It sure felt like something.

Solar stood at the bottom of the walkway for a while after that.

The cold pressed at her cheeks, breath fogging and disappearing. Somewhere in her chest, in the exact spot the week had been sitting on, something warm bloomed and spread through her sternum, uninvited and unexamined. She knew about the project, she remembered the footage that lead to another thing she pretended didn't happen. It was something, simply, that on top of all of the other things, had fell to the distant back of her mind, somewhat forgotten. She just hadn't thought about the fact other people were going to witness it, to watch it and ponder, what does this mean? Minji had never really mentioned it again, she hadn't posted about it, performed it, it was like rain to her, something that would surely come again, more flourishing than the last.

Her brain shifted, unhelpfully, to drink carriers, granola bars, carefully conjured captions that made it worse, about all the gestures that arrived gift wrapped with the receipt showing. She pushed it away then, because the comparison was doing something she didn't have the capacity for, especially not with the tournament twelve hours away.

She wrestled the door open on the second shoulder, making her way up the stairs, past the too floral hallway and into her painfully pink room. Throwing her phone on the nightstand, she set an alarm for eight, flopping onto her bed in the dark and running through the comp one last time in her head. That obnoxious smoke detector was shockingly quiet.

Tomorrow at noon, she reminded herself as if it was needed.

And then she fell asleep like she'd already won.

45: frequency

Solar woke up four minutes before her alarm, which her body had apparently decided was its final gift to her. She laid there for a while, in the dark of her room, staring at the ceiling, listening to her own heartbeat doing something that wasn't quite nerves yet not quite excitement, either. With a deep exhale, she turned off the alarm that hadn't rung yet, starting her day like it was any other Saturday and not the first tournament in program history.

A dramatic yawn left her lips as she stretched her arms out, before reaching for her phone. Though, she didn't expect what was waiting for her on the screen.

jaehyun:\ I've been informed that you've been playing on a team.

jaehyun:\ This is something I won't tell father.

jaehyun:\ It's good for you

jaehyun:\ Good luck today, sister.

His texts irritated her in the way they tended to, but she still couldn't help but grin, only slightly. It wasn't the reaction she would've expected from her older brother, eSports was something he would shake his head at, call silly and insist that it had no real purpose. But it was like, for once, he was letting her have one thing.

One thing.

\@hyein:\ good morning CHAMPION

\@hyein:\ TODAY IS THE DAY WHOS FUCKING HYPE? I AMMMM

\@hyein:\ how r we feeling my COOL WINNER BESTIE

\@hyein:\ be honest but only if ur feeling good

\@solar:\ kinda feeling like throwing up

\@solar:\ but like... in a productive way ig

\@hanni:\ that's the spirit i think?

\@hyein:\ YEAHHHH THATS THE SPIRIT ALRIGHT

\@hyein:\ also small update

\@hyein:\ the sign is DONE peeps

\@solar:\ oh u mean the sign i said no to

\@hyein:\ ya

\@hyein:\ wait no

\@hyein:\ the MEDIUM sign

\@hyein:\ hanni supervised u will be so proud

\@hanni:\ don't throw me into this

\@hanni:\ but it's tasteful, sol!

\@hyein:\ its not tasteful its fabulous tyvm

\@solar:\ good god

\@solar:\ ykw wtv do what u wanna do just dont distract me during the game pls

\@hyein:\ we'll be there at 11:30 SHARP

\@hanni:\ by that she means 11:45, we know how she is

\@hyein:\ HEY

She was grinning to herself as she got dressed, which she registered the way she registered most good things lately, distantly, as if it'd notice her noticing and run away.

The walk to the lab at nine forty-five had a texture she'd never felt on this campus before, the cold doing it's usual October work, but there were more people around than typical. Some were walking in the same direction as her, with lanyards, energy drinks, and outside the building, someone had propped a folding sign with the university crest and the words ESPORTS INVITATIONAL - DOORS OPEN AT NOON.

Something in her chest twisted with pride, she was about to head inside, when she overheard a kid she'd never met in her life, doing a double take before whispering to his friend, "yo, that's the Kiriko," at a volume he seemed to believe was quiet.

The Kiriko, she thought, beaming then as she swung the door open and headed inside. Not Jaehyun's sister, Mark's girl, Haechan's piece, Minji's lesbian lover, she wasn't the girl from the posts then, and it felt new and refreshing in a way she wished would stay forever.

She held onto that feeling through the halls, into the lab that'd been rearranged overnight into something between an arena and a fire hazard, with folding chairs in rows, a projector screen the size of a garage door, a card table near the front where two boys from the broadcasting club where taping down cables and arguing about which one of them was the play by play. Doyoung was already there, clipboard and pen in hand, the particular calm of a man who'd slept four hours and refused to admit such a thing.

"Warm up at 10:30," he greets, without looking up from whatever he was writing, "aim trainer, two customs, light ones, nobody plays ranked this morning," he looks up then, eyes raking across the five of them, "I mean it, I will know."

"He'll know," Chenle echoes, half jokingly, pretending he was a ghost in a child's film as he spun back around to ensure his settings were perfected.

By eleven, the team was loose in a way Friday night had promised. Olivia narrated her own aim warm up as if it was a nature documentary, Haerin ate a granola bar with the stillness of a monk, Minji rolled through her mechanics with her cap on backwards, the way it was on game days because she couldn't be bothered with her hair, which Solar noticed and had to force herself to not think about.

At eleven forty, the doors opened, and the noise came in waves.

The crowd was bigger than anyone had planned for, that much was clear, from the way the folding chairs ran out in the first ten minutes, people beginning to stand along the walls, filling the back, pressing in around the caster table. Students, mostly, the exact demographic Solar expected, but more of them, with the lanyards and hoodies. Shockingly, even a couple professors were there. A boy in the front row was wearing a jersey from an Overwatch League team, as if it was the same thing.

And at the front row center sat Mark Lee, holding a Dutch Bros drink carrier like a bouquet.

"I told you," he beams as her footsteps stopped in front of him, eyes immediately falling to the drinks in his hands. That dorky smile was on his lips as he held out the drink to her, "front row, got here at eleven. There's a whole line out there."

She chuckled as she took the drink from him, already taking a sip because she needed that caffeine more than anything. "You're insane, Mark," she told him anyway.

"Nah, just supporting you," he corrects casually, pleased with himself, before leaning slightly to look past her at where the PCs were set up gloriously, "so, explain it to me one more time, the objective is the payload or..."

"Uhuh, sometimes it's the payload."

"Sometimes," he repeats, gravely, clearly not understanding what that meant, which was something that would never change no matter how many times she tried to tell him. Instead of saying anything else, Solar stood there, trying to ponder how to explain control points to a music major who had never touched a video game in their life, when the noise near the doors changed, a specific pitch of arrival, and she directed her attention to see Hanni and Hyein pushing through the crowd, with a sign tucked under Hyein's arm.

Well, she thought, it is medium.

The sign, in fact, was a pink poster board, edged in silver glitter that was already shedding a trail through the crowd like a comet. In lettering that was clearly Hanni's careful hand, it read, LET SOLAR COOK. Around the words, in Hyein's messy manner, were no fewer than eleven drawn stars and a small cartoon rendering of what was probably meant to be Kiriko but looked charitably like a cat in a scarf.

"I said no signs," Solar deadpans, even though she wasn't too mad at it, "remember?"

"Yeah, that works on Mark," Hyein returns, "not me, I operate under a different jurisdiction."

"Nice to see you, too, Hyein," Mark offered at the sound of his name from where he was sitting in the chair behind them.

"If it makes you feel any better," Hanni offered, after waving to Mark in her friendly way, "this one is much more tame, the first one glowed in the dark."

Behind them, the casters were doing a mic check, the projector flickering to life with the bracket line up on it. Doyoung appeared at Solar's shoulder with the specific gravity of a coach about to reclaim his player. "Five minutes," he informs her firmly, before nodding a single time at her friends, at Mark, at the sign, which he looked at for a moment longer than anything else, and made a sound that in Doyoung was likely laughter.

"Go!" Hyein claps in excitement, not afraid to show it, both hands on Solar's shoulders, spinning her toward the setups, "go be famous for the right thing!"

The first map of the finals, however, was a disaster in slow motion. They'd cut through the early rounds the way the seeding claimed they would, two matches, clean, the crowd learning the team's names map by map, learning when to gasp, learning that the small quiet one on the end was apparently a demon in disguise. But finals meant they were going against the team Chenle had been prophesying about all week, meaning that the Widow would be present, and she was exactly as advertised.

She was on the high ground, of course, positioned somewhere no one seemed to think of, the adrenaline too present in their veins, messing up their once careful thinking. First fight, she took Olivia's head off before she'd finished leaving spawn, and the crowd made the wrong kind of sound in response, causing something in the team's rhythm to hiccup. They never quite got their footing back the rest of the map, Solar could feel it in her hands, the half-second of extra thought before every teleport, the way nerves turned reflexes back into decisions, it was exactly the thing Doyoung warned them about and had been trying to prevent, the first fight sets the temperature.

Nonetheless, they dropped the map, the room deflating around them.

There were sixty seconds between maps, enough for Doyoung to crouch behind their row, clipboard against his knee, and whatever speech was in him, he compressed into one sentence, delivered flat, "you've already seen everything she can do."

No one said anything at first, perhaps they were ashamed, embarrassed to have lost in front of so many people. Until Minji cleared her throat, captain voice on, mic half down, "we're done being nice about it, Haerin..."

"...Shoot her first!" Haerin cheers, finishing Minji's sentence for her.

By the second map, they were a different animal, and the room felt it before the kill feed proved it. Chenle's bubbles arrived like punctuation instead of hesitation again, Minji's Ashe opened sightlines with that horrible yet gorgeous confidence, and Solar finally began to play the way she did at 2 AM in ranked, when nobody was watching and her name didn't exist, threading her teleports through fights that had no business being threadable, taking off angles to poke their supports with impeccable aim. They took it, and the crowd took it with them, the casters giving up on professionalism somewhere in the middle.

It was settled, almost. Some of them were breathing hard after the Victory screen flashed. Solar bit down on her lip as she exchanged glances with her teammates.

"Good?" Minji rose a brow at her, tilting her head to meet her eyes.

"Mhm," she nods, "you like that suzu last fight?"

"I don't know," the girl shrugs, playing dumb, "do it again and I'll tell you."

-

Haechan hadn't planned on being there, which was the version he was telling himself, and he was sticking to it.

He'd been in the studio since eight that morning, not letting himself think about the reason too much. The file had opened without ceremony, that was the thing, the door was never locked, that the two weeks of the cursor sitting frozen mid-bar had been a decision he'd renewed everyday, and whatever had undecided it on Thursday night was still present.

He worked the way he used to work, before, which meant time moved wrong. By the time he surfaced, it was 11:40 and his neck hurt, though the melody had a spine at least, and he'd sat there in the soundproof room, listening to it with his eyes closed. Underneath it, so subtly, in the place where a track kept its secrets, there was still that stem he never deleted, a few seconds of someone singing, barely above speaking, quiet enough it almost didn't count.

Of course, he knew it was happening, the way he knew things without purpose. He told himself he was walking home, but the lab happened to be between the studio and everywhere, which wasn't entirely true. He could've taken the side exit if he really wanted to, it was faster, anyway.

But he didn't.

The noise reached him from a hundred feet down the hall, a crowd sound with a shape to it, rising and dropping in waves, and his feet made the decision the way his hands had been making decisions all morning. He'd thrown his hood up again, dark hair falling in his eyes, and slipped in through the door without a soul noticing during a break in play, along the back wall where the standing crowd was three deep, behind a tall kid in a jersey who blocked the sightline from the front of the room entirely.

She was 20 rows of strangers away, tiny behind her monitor, headset on. His eyes found her instantly, the way you find middle C without looking, the way your heart knows before your eyes do.

He knew the stillness, it was the thing about watching her that nobody else in this room could've known, that the focus on her face right then, the flat eyes, the jaw, was the same concentration from a gaming chair in his bedroom, from a rooftop ledge, from across a lecture hall pretending not to see him. There were times when she let herself go, felt the freedom of not worrying so much about the eyes of others, about what anyone else would think, about being who she was without growing concerned over the consequences later.

Like that night.

Everyone else was watching the screen, but he watched her hands for a while, the way they effortlessly flew across the keyboard, how she'd bite the inside of her cheek, the way she'd pout when she missed a kunai that could've otherwise been a pick. He had to make himself tear his eyes away.

The last map came down to the last fight.

He didn't follow the game the way the crowd did, off the casters and the kill feed. Instead, he followed it based off the room, the pitch, the held breath, the way a hundred people inhaled at once when the enemy Widow reappeared on the high ground with the fight on the line, and the casters' voices climbed over each other, the room's note bent sharp with the specific sound of hope starting to fold.

It wasn't that he couldn't understand the game, he was the same rank as Solar. If he wanted to, he could be on that team right now, sitting next to her, just like Minji.

If things were easier.

The room abruptly changed key then. His eyes locked onto the projector a half-second after the crowd's sound told him it was coming, the Kiriko taking an off angle behind the Widow, like she'd teleported out of the future, the wall climb nobody had tracked because every eye in the building had been on the fight below. The enemy team was pressuring the others, badly, and if Solar didn't do something, her flank would be a throw.

Two kunais fly in the air, and the Widow drops dead, the caster screaming something that was far from a coherent word. Still, one pick wasn't going to be enough to turn the fight around, Haerin was dead, and the other DPS was still making moves. Solar, however, was the one with the high ground now. Before the enemy team could register, she'd taken out the Ana the second she tried to shoot a sleep dart at her.

They were still going, as the point percentage kept ticking, and she threw a suzu into her team the millisecond the Rein ulted, deeming it entirely and utterly useless.

The room detonated, her team was screaming in her ears, the tall kid in the jersey was jumping, everyone was jumping. Soon, the folding chairs became a distant memory, somewhere near the front a neon pink sign was being waved so violently it was shedding glitter like a struck pinata, and the caster table had abandoned the broadcast entirely. And in the very back, behind all of it, a tired looking boy with his hood over his face stood completely still with his hands in his pockets, and smiled for the first time in awhile. It wasn't the one covered in a facade, it spread over lips slowly and without his permission, the way the melody had come back, the way everything real arrived, sideways, uninvited, while he was telling himself he was walking home. On the screen, the girl who had no idea he existed in this room took off her headset and disappeared under a pile of her teammates.

He let himself watch for a moment, the way they'd all hugged her, the way everyone was yelling in complete ecstatic joy. Hyein and Hanni ran over to tackle her in their own loving hug, being the supportive friends they were even when they had no idea what they'd just watched.

And then there was Mark.

The final kill feed was still clearing when he slipped out the doors.

Nobody saw him come in, no one saw him go. He walked home through the cold with his hands in his pockets, the crowd's roar still ringing in his ears, except it wasn't the roar he was keeping, it was the half-second right before it, the inhale, the room full of a hundred strangers all discovering at the same instant the thing he'd known since August.

That Solar Lee was the only frequency in the room.

-

Solar emerged from the bottom of the team pile with her hair wrecked and someone's elbow print on her cheek, the specific deafness of a room that had been screaming for ten seconds straight.

It didn't feel real yet, that was the honest report from inside her chest. It felt like the moment after a scrim win, but the sound continued, and when she stood up the projector had her play frozen on it, the kunai mid-fight, the casters saying her handle into microphones and talking about how she had single handedly turned the entire game around. Chenle was standing on a folding chair being told to get down by Doyoung, whose clipboard, for the first time in recorded history, was on the floor.

"MVP," Minji informs her, a proud grin on her face, like the whole day had gone exactly the way she called it, "What'd I tell you, superstar?" She hooked an arm around Solar's neck, knuckling the top of her head like a big brother in a way that made the nearby crowd coo, and Solar shoved her off, giggling, as the cameras from the broadcasting club caught the entire instance.

Hyein arrived like a projectile, the sign with her, mostly destroyed by now, hugged between the three of them, shedding it's last glitter into Solar's hair. Hanni was saying something that kept dissolving into cheerful screeches, and over Hyein's shoulder, she could see Mark in the front row, beaming, phone already out and raised, and she found herself looking away from the phone camera before her face could do anything about it.

"We're eating good tonight!" Hyein bellows, to the group, perhaps to the room, to the sport of eSports itself, "Everything, I tell you... but, like, I'm paying for nothing, you know, cause I'm a student... BUT WE'RE STILL EATING GOOD!"

Before anyone could say anything in response, she'd turned to look at Mark, "Mark, can I borrow your credit card? Like, as a congrats to Solar?"

"Uh, probably not, Hyein," he calls back, chuckling in disbelief.

Hyein scoffed as she turned back to the group, "wow, and here I thought he had to have it like that."

Doyoung gathered the team after that for exactly 90 seconds before releasing them to the crowd, and his speech, delivered over the noise, clipboard recovered and clinging to his chest, was enough to nearly make them start squealing again, "I've coached for six years, that was the best play I've ever seen in person, impeccable. Please hydrate, and I'll see you all on Monday, good? Get out of my lab and have fun, kids."

Though, before they could leave, some nerds in the crowd wanted pictures, the casters wanted an interview to use for the school's media. They'd gotten half of one before Hyein physically pulled her away. Somewhere in the middle of it, being passed from person to person, her cheeks aching from a smile she had zero control over, Solar felt it. An instinct that lived at the back of her spine, the one that'd announced him on a diagonal path two days ago, and she found herself scanning the back of the room for no reason she could find words for.

Strangers, lanyards, the doors swinging shut behind the first wave of people leaving, nothing out of the ordinary that she could see, but the feeling in her chest didn't lessen. Hyein was pulling her arm by then, causing it to fold away.

It's nothing, she assured herself, and let the day have her back.

Meanwhile, Sigma Chi was dark when Haechan got home, the boys either out of pretending Saturday didn't apply to them. His room, on the other hand, was exactly where he'd left it, laptop open, the file staring back at him expectantly.

He'd sat down at the desk without turning on the light, hand gripping the mouse, playing the track back once, all the way through. The spine of it was present now, at the very least. When it reached the quiet place near the end where the old stem lived, that voice barely above speaking, he didn't skip past it, he let it play, leaning back in his chair and crossing his arms behind his head.

Pursing his lips in thought, he leans forward again to pull the fader up on it, two decibels, just enough.

And in the private of his room, he almost let himself smile again before he pushed it away, deciding the feeling felt far too unnatural.

I'm not recording you, he'd told her, which was a lie, but not one coated with bad intent, because Haechan was not the same person he was before August.

He just couldn't accept that yet.

Outside, faintly, from somewhere across campus, he would still hear the screams and cheers of celebration.

He shook his head then, reaching for the bottle of whiskey he had next to his setup. It wasn't the baggy, but it was something that helped him take the edge off.

Then, he looked back at his screen.

The cursor didn't stop moving until morning.

46: celebration time

NEW POST\ \@SUanon\ \ guess solar is more than mark and minjis piece of the week, shes actually GOOD at overwatch! has anyone figured out what happened with haechan tho?

NEW POST\ \@SUesports\ \ We're so proud of our Overwatch SU Team, who placed first place in the collegiate invitational! Check out the stellar play by the match MVP, main support player Solar Lee! [Video link]

-

The clip had 12,000 views by the time they'd left the building to walk to the restaurant they'd decided on going to. Solar knew this because her phone refused to stop going off in her pocket, the low constant buzz like that of a living being. When she'd finally checked it, outside in the October cold with her teammates and friends, the notifications had stopped being readable and instead became a scrolling wall of tags, follows, and reposts, her handle being passed around by strangers like a good rumor. Some sort of compilation account asked the eSports page for permission to use the play, while someone else already clipped the nerdy casters screeching over the music.

Though, the strangest part, the one she found herself stuck on, as they began their stroll off of the campus, and she processed, that this was maybe the first time she'd been off this campus since she'd gotten here, was what the comments didn't say. No one cared that she was Jaehyun Lee's little sister, there was no talk about her dating life under the eSports name, no speculation about who she was with at the current moment, about what she wore, no one cared. All anyone was worried about was the play, commenting things like clean and kiri goes nuts, pro league material 4sure.

It was one of the first nights she felt like she simply got to be good at something without any strings attached, not privately in the lab, but outwardly.

And as much as she tried not to, she couldn't help but look at the SUanon post. Her friends were in deep conversations around her, Chenle still talking about one of his ults he thought was much better than Solar's play, Haerin asking Olivia something philosophical that neither of them had the capacity to think about, and she somehow couldn't seem to stop her urges. She could feel that someone still had something to say, that someone was going to ruin her streak of peace with some idiotic comment that no normal person should reasonably care about.

Has anyone figured out what happened with Haechan though?

She puffed her cheeks up before dramatically exhaling into the air at that, turning her phone back off and shoving it into her pocket. She was annoyed with herself for doing such a thing, but she couldn't seem to help it, either.

Nope, she thought, not exactly.

-

The Korean barbecue place off campus took the eight of them at a corner table built for six, which was how Solar ended up wedged between Hyein and the wall with the grill smoke rising in front of her like a curtain, which she would later proclaim passionately was one of the best dinners of her life.

Olivia commandeered the tongs immediately, declaring herself the grillmaster, a role she performed at full volume and with occasionally justified confidence. Haerin, on the flip side, constructed lettuce wraps with the focus on a watchmaker, saying absolutely nothing while somehow managing to eat the most. Hanni, being the sweet angel she was, made it her mission to refill everyone's water, and Chenle sat at the far end with his glasses fogging faintly over the grill heat, phone face up next to his plate, quietly refreshing the clip's view count between bites while switching over to his mobile game every now and then. He wasn't saying the numbers aloud, though, just watching them the way you'd watch a stock you owned.

"At fifteen now," he muttered eventually, to no one in particular, in the tone of a man confirming a hypothesis, "thousands, looks like the algorithm picked it up," he continues dully, "mine was better, though, just being honest here."

"Holy shit!" Olivia cried, loud enough that some other customers, even in the noisiness of the place, turned to look at her disapprovingly. Of course, she couldn't care any less, "that's fucking nuts, dude."

"You need to stream now," Minji hums, "that's money."

Solar wrinkled her nose, "nah, I don't think that's for me."

Before anyone else could add to the conversation, the teams phones had seemed to buzz at the exact same time. Chenle saw it first, since he was already on his phone. It was a Discord ping, arriving in the middle of the second round of meat. He'd looked bored, uninterested, at first, narrowing his eyes down at the screen as he read it carefully.

Then, his face changed, he abruptly went very still, setting down his chopsticks that had been in his other hand. But he didn't stand up or yell, he just pushed his glasses up his nose with one finger, looking around the table under he had a quorum of attention, and cleared his throat, the way Doyoung would've, like a weather report that happened to conveniently alter their lives, "we're going to regionals in December."

"We're WHAT?!" Olivia screamed that time, ten times louder than the last, and now other customers were really staring at them like they were crazy people.

"Bro," Chenle scoffs, annoyed, followed by a deep sigh, "I said it quietly on purpose."

His words didn't matter, because the entire table detonated in excitement anyway. Olivia's squeals rattled the ventilation hood, Hyein started chanting something with no discernible words in it, Minji reached across three people to grab Solar's arm, and underneath all of it, Chenle calmly turned his phone over and continued to chow down on his meat like this was totally casual.

Somewhere in between the screams, speculation, and hypotheticals, Mark had arrived as Olivia was putting on the third round of meat, sliding into the end of the table with that dorky wave and the specific ease of someone who'd been invited in a way nobody could exactly source, Hyein having mentioned the afterparty at the tournament but not really expecting him to come. In the moment, he simply appeared, Hanni and Hyein welcoming him with the warmth they were known for, Chenle blankly peering at his fellow Sigma member before deadpanning, "man, you're like a mosquito."

But Mark was good at it, laughing in the right places, asking Haerin a sincere question about wrap architecture that earned him one full sentence in response, which was a knighthood by Haerin standards. He kept a polite distance from Solar the entirety of the meal, attentive without hovering, an attitude that read as respectful and perfectly boyfriend material on camera.

She almost felt bad for thinking it, Mark wasn't like that, it was what she assured herself of.

The thing that took them longer than typical to realize, was that the bill never came.

Hyein eventually managed to flag down the server, her card brandished, announcing her intention to contribute no less than eleven dollars, and the server politely explaining with the diplomatic blankness of a man who'd been tipped to say nothing sooner, that it'd been taken care of. Everyone paused in confusion, exchanging glances with each other, waiting for someone to give it away.

Down at the end of the table, Mark had become extremely fascinated with stacking the empty plates into a neat tower.

"By WHO?" Hyein demands, and then, her eyes slowly raked across the table, following the thought process of her peers, "Oh my God, MARK!"

The smile on his face seemed to give it away, despite how he shook his head. Chenle was grumbling in disgust from how obvious he was, and Hanni reached out to place a hand on his shoulder, "that's very kind of you, Mark."

"You guys won," he shrugs, palms up, ears growing pink as the table turned on him with affectionate violence, Olivia reaching across two people to shake him by the shoulder, "it's really not that deep, you earned it, for real."

"Told you guys he had it like that," Hyein proclaims to the entire building, standing on the rung of her chair until Hanni hurriedly pulled her back down into her seat, "I KNEW he had it like that!"

It was the nicest thing anyone had done all night, and Solar found herself smiling at him from the other side of the table. He raised his glass of water at her in return, modest and easy, that nerdy smile on his lips. He'd raised his phone then, taking a photo of the entire table mid chaos, for the memory, he told them, which no one thought much of.

-

NEW POST\ \@markylee

![\@markylee: shoutout to the best support at SU @sollee (i just watch lol) thanks for letting me come along for the ride! ](media/image3.png){width="6.5in" height="4.875in"}

\@markylee: shoutout to the best support at SU \@sollee (i just watch lol) thanks for letting me come along for the ride!

She didn't think much of the post at first, not really, she'd read the caption twice on the walk out, best support was sweet, it warmed her heart. More than that, it was clever, and it had a second meaning folded into it as neatly as a receipt in a gift bag.

It was when her eyes went to the comments, there were a few, mainly from her teammates and friends, Chenle commenting, god this guy is so fucking cringe. But then, her eyes caught the sight of an all too familiar username.

@SUnews: our gamer girl really CAN do it all, can't she? did we hear a little something about mark lee footing the bill? wow, like a TRUE gentlemen... hm, what a "lucky" girl our solar is!

Something about that comment felt very wrong. She stared down at it, mind wandering back to everything else she'd heard, to the way Danielle peered straight into her irises as she told her they've all got their dark sides, the deja vu everyone on the anonymous account kept mentioning, the way Haechan texted her he does it on purpose, you know.

All she knew for sure was about the scholarship, no one wanted to tell her the real thing, whatever it was everyone kept hinting at. At the very least, she had learned Danielle wasn't wrong, not entirely, about Haechan.

But Mark?

She didn't want to think he had real bad intent, he didn't seem like that guy. Yet, her stomach kept twisting as she stared down at that post.

Lucky girl, with quotations, the phrase repeated in her head like poison. She'd read it again in the doorway of Haerin's apartment building with the cold at her back, feeling the machine do what it did, take the one night that'd belonged to her and reattach it back to a boy, to luck, and she stood there for a second with her thumb over the caption while the others piled up the stairs. Hyein turned to call out to her, dramatically waving her hands for her to come on, causing her to exhale and send her a soft smile before trailing up the steps behind them.

"You alright, Sol?" Hanni had turned to ask her, waiting in the middle of the stairs for her. She'd been watching the entire thing, it'd seemed.

"Yeah," she nods, voice low so no one else heard, "just the stupid gossip accounts."

"Oh, Sol," she pouts, "ignore that noise, this is your day, okay?"

Solar nods then, thinking about how she had yet to tell the girls the conversation she'd had with Danielle, how she was becoming fairly certain that this was all her. They were the only ones who would hear those words and not be surprised, who would listen to her and go, we knew it.

Hanni smiled softly at her, placing a comforting hand on her arm and walking up the steps by her side. Hyein was still at the top, waiting for her two friends, whilst everyone else had already entered the apartment.

"Good?" she asks once they reached the top, and Solar send her a creepy smile that caused her to make a face of disturbance, "Kay, gonna take that as a strong maybe."

Tonight was still hers, she told herself, surrounded by the compassionate warmth of her friends. She wasn't going to let someone like Danielle and a bunch of speculation about someone who clearly cared for her ruin it.

As it appeared, Haerin was the only one of them who had an apartment that wasn't exactly on campus, since everyone else was a part of a chapter. It was still student housing, but it was located a block over from Syracuse University, far enough that you couldn't see the glow of the amber lights anymore in the distance. It was a simplistic one bedroom she shared with a roommate who was, per Haerin, back in Manhattan, don't worry about it, and by 11 PM, the living room had become a warm yet loud ecosystem, with someone's playlist arguing with itself, Chenle and Haerin running back the tournament on the coffee table using soy sauce packets as team comps while Hanni and Hyein taught Mark a drinking game they'd come up with on the spot.

She sat on the edge of the couch for awhile, not by herself exactly, but just watching the scene before her with a small smile on her face. Mark, who was closest to her, still mid-game with her two friends, looked over to her, raising a brow, "Care to join? I feel like it's a little unfair here, you know, two against one."

"Maybe you shouldn't suck then," Hyein chimes.

"Feel free to join, Sol!" Hanni offers.

The game, as far as Solar could tell, had no name, no objective, and a rulebook that existed exclusively inside Hyein's head, where it was currently being written in real time, amending whenever the current rules failed to make Mark drink.

"New rule!" Hyein announces proudly, holding up a playing card that was irrelevant to the game, "If you've ever paid for eight people's dinners and lied about it, you drink!" Her eyes, naturally, fall on Mark.

"That's not a rule," he defends, playfully, "that's a callout," he was chuckling to himself, picking up the poured shot and downing it anyway.

"All roles are callouts, Mark," Hanni softly explains, before whispering as if Hyein was deaf, "it's better to just go with it."

Solar had folded herself into the circle, somewhere between the second and third rule, cross legged on the floor with her own poured shot and a separate mixed drink that Haerin made pink for no reason other than she knew her, and she'd stopped keeping track of how much of it she'd had around the time the warmth stopped being the drink and instead became a part of her. It was a specific kind of drunk she was becoming, not the sloppy kind, but the type where the room became a bit more colorful and softer on the edges, where everything her friends said we a little funnier, and the once loud noise in her chest was suddenly silent.

"New rule!" Hyein cries again, drunk with legislative power, pointing a mischievous finger at Solar, "if you were the MVP of a collegiate invitational today, you drink!"

"Alright, this is starting to sound rigged," Solar deadpans, though she wasn't mad at all.

"Well duh," Hyein retorts.

"All rules are rigged," Haerin chimes from the couch, not looking up from the soy sauce packet she was repositioning on the coffee table, where her and Chenle were still running back the grand finals like the outcome might change.
