She drank anyway, because that was the entire point, the room cheering like she'd done something impressive, Olivia screaming her name loud enough that somewhere in the building a neighbor was surely composing a noise complaint. But Solar laughed into the rim of her mixed drink, as it occurred to her, that she was happy, genuinely happy in the uncomplicated way she'd stopped expecting from Syracuse University.

Though, her eyes kept trailing to a certain dark haired girl.

Minji had spent most of the party on the armrest of the couch with her camera out, filming in the way she always filmed, ten seconds here, ten there, and everytime the lens drifted towards Solar it stopped asking to be a camera and just became Minji peering at her. It was no surprise, to anyone, that she had the camera with her, by then, it was a packaged deal. Where Minji was, a camera probably was, too.

She still had her cap on, frontwards this time, the brim throwing a shadow over her eyes so that Solar could only see the bottom half of her face, her mouth, the way it curved when Chenle made another monotone comment that was hard to not giggle at.

Realizing she had been staring at the shape of her lips for far too long, her eyes drifted back down to her drink, focusing back on whatever rule Hyein had made up now.

"Okay, wait," Mark was saying, hands up, deep in negotiation with Hyein over whether a rule could be retroactive, and there was a version of this night where Solar sat with that, with how easy he was in a room, how it bent around him like he'd been invited by the architecture itself. But the pink drink had turned the rational thoughts off, and she'd promised herself the night was hers, so she let him be a boy losing a card game instead of a caption, rising to her feet to go refill a cup that didn't need refilling.

The kitchen was two steps from the living room, which in this apartment was a matter of opinion. Minji was already there then, filling a glass of water with the deliberate focus on someone who'd had exactly enough to know she needed to slow down.

"Hey, my duo MVP," she hums, without turning around, since she never needed to turn around to know it was her.

"Don't you start, too," Solar huffs.

"Start what?" Minji turns, leaning her hip against the counter, "I've been calling you that since tryouts, I earned the right."

"Oh, did you?" Solar raises a brow, crossing her arms over her chest, "you've also been calling me washed since tryouts."

She smiles then, innocently, and shrugs, "both things can be true."

A comfortable silence fell between them for a split second, the window above Haerin's sink was cracked open an inch, the October air threading through it, thin and cold. Solar became aware all at once that she was overheated, that the apartment was a small warm box packed with noise, that she'd been performing celebration for four hours straight. It wasn't that she wasn't excited or happy, but she was simply tired of keeping it up for so long.

"There's a balcony," Minji mentions, watching her the way she watched a kill feed, "you look like you're about to melt."

"I'm not gonna melt."

"Okay," she shrugs, "I believe you," she picks up her water without another word, before spinning on her heel and walking towards the sliding door anyway, because Minji Kim never once needed Solar to say yes aloud.

It was only expected that the brunette was going to follow her.

The balcony was barely a balcony, a concrete lip with a railing and enough room for two people if they didn't mind being a little crammed. Behind them, through the glass, the tiny party glowed like a television with the sound off, Hyein mid-proclaimation, Mark laughing at whatever verdict had come down against him. He looked so normal, so entirely innocent, that no one who didn't already know would ever think twice about it.

Outside, the cold landed on her flushed skin as if they were spikes of ice.

"God damn," she mutters, her breath coming out visible, curling up past the railing into the dark, "this might be worse." She was lying, though, because the cold did feel nice.

"Sure, don't act like you don't have that look," Minji snorts, leaning her forearms on the railing, glass of water dangling from her fingers over the drop.

"What look?"

"The one from the tournament, before they called the map," she explains, tipping her head to recall it better, "like... you're standing in the middle of something loud, trying to find something worth paying attention to."

"Oh," Solar hums dryly, almost sarcastically, "so, you're a poet now."

Minji chuckled then, looking away to stare down at the roads below them, watching the cars go by in the dark of the night. "Kinda, film is an art, too, after all."

She wasn't wrong, really, about any of it. Solar didn't know what else to say then, which Minji seemed to expect, letting the silence float there between them, unbothered. It was the thing about her that nobody warned you about, Minji was loud in game, all about shot calls, timing windows, getting on the payload with her flat captain authority, but outside of it, she had these silences that didn't ask for anything; they just made room, and Solar had spent two months walking into that room without ever asking herself why the door was always open.

Something sat in the pit of her stomach, and she was too nervous to confront it then, focusing on the buzz at the top of her head instead, the way things were spinning when she'd focus on them for more than a few seconds. There was a slight whoosh in the air from the October breeze, cars continued to zip past beneath them, all apparently having important places to be at such a late hour.

"Twelve thousands views," Minji remarks, eyes still on the street beneath them.

"Well, sixteen now," Solar corrects lightly, "Chenle keeps announcing it like a stock ticker."

She giggles, "he's proud of you, in his own way, he'd rather be waterboarded then say it," she takes a sip of her water, pausing, "we all are, that's allowed, you know, people being proud of you without it coming with a consequence."

Her system didn't know how to process kindness that didn't invoice it, was the issue at hand. It kept scanning for the catch, and the only catch it found all night was a comment with quotation marks around the work lucky and a question at the end of an anonymous post that she was pretending wasn't on her mind.

has anyone figured out what happened with haechan tho?

"You're going somewhere else again," Minji observes, without accusation, the same way she'd call out a flank, "you've been doing it all night, between the good parts."

"Huh?" she almost got defensive, letting her face fall flat again, "I'm not... I'm fine, Ji."

"I know you're fine," she nods in agreeance, straightening up off the railing, only a couple of inches closer, or perhaps the balcony had grown smaller, "you're always fine, all week, at practice, you're the finest person I've ever met, it's your worst stat."

There were multiple meanings to it, Solar picked that up, and the goofy smile on Minji's lips only gave it away further. "Gee, thanks," she shakes her head, chuckling to herself. Minji looked glad, though, to hear her laugh with sincerity, seeming quite pleased with herself, as if she'd hit a shot she'd been lining up for a while.

Another couple of minutes passed, the cold began to stop feeling like cold, or at least she thought, yet the railing was still ice under her hand and her breath was still showing up in the air. But there was a warmth coming off of Minji's shoulder, where it was almost touching hers, her body apparently decided that this warmth was relevant data, ignoring the rest of the temperature. Then again, it'd seemed as if her body had made lots of decisions recently without consulting her first, like the shadow of the cap brim, the curve of her lips, the way she'd look down when she laughed. There were many things that made someone stop and stare at Minji Kim, all without realizing they were doing so.

And now, those things were all presenting to her at once, drunk, on 11:40 PM on a concrete block off campus, with the miniature party glowing behind the glass, the echo of laughter coming through it faintly. None of the friends seemed to really notice their missing presence, not even Mark, who was drunk himself by now, too engrossed in playing the game with her friends, who he had come to like more than he ever planned to.

Haechan used her, the thought popped into her brain like a nightmare, one that reoccurred no matter what you changed, or planned her, whatever the genuine shape of it was. Everyone kept telling her, there were little things to it, it had nothing to do with you, that's just what he does. He'd played her a song with his whole chest open, then closed like a door, gone radio silent, and she'd spent a week pretending it was no big deal.

But Minji, Minji had never used her for anything, who filmed her like she was worth keeping and asked twenty times beforehand. Minji, who rewatched her VODs for fun, never posting a frame without permission, who was standing in the cold in a stupid frontwards cap, making room, cheeks tinted red from the weather, dark hair falling in her eyes from the wind.

She was staring again, at her side profile, and her eyes kept flicking downward before she could stop herself.

It wasn't a realization that came with trumpets, not a name for herself she was ready to say in daylight. There was the party she pretended didn't happen, the makeout they were both entirely sober for, atop Minji's bed in the fancy honors dorm where only nerds lived, where you only got to live if you were serious about your studies, and Minji was utterly serious about being simple, easy, and completely free minded and unbothered about what anyone seemed to think about her or her preferences. She was herself, unapologetically and beautifully, in a way Solar could only gawk at and think, will that ever be me?

The difference was, Minji felt nothing like Haechan. It was the part that made it safe to think, Haechan was gravity, something that happened to her, a pull with teeth. This was something opposite, something soft, the aspiration to step into a cozy room, to see the top half of her face in the moonlight and run her fingers across the tenderness of her cheek. Different, yet real, and nobody on the balcony was going to post about it. And out here, blocks away from SU, there was no way Danielle would somehow catch wind, either.

"Sol," Minji tilts her head, her smile fading into something more careful, "what is it?"

She'd caught her staring, and finally said something about it, leaving Solar standing there like a deer in headlights. Her lips form a straight line, trying to figure out the proper response, trying to process what to do in that moment.

Until she decided, she didn't care anymore.

"Ji..." she softly whispers, her hands slowly reaching up, so slow that if Minji wanted to, she could stop it. But she didn't, she let her fingers pick up a piece of her dark hair that flew down her shoulders, feeling the soft of it underneath her fingertips.

She watched Minji hear it then, watched it move through her, the nickname, tone, or even the half-step Solar had taken without officially authorizing her feet to do so. Her eyes raked across her face a few times, pondering, deep in thought, before she'd murmured, "you're drunk."

Yet, it wasn't a no, it was a door being held open with a warning taped to it.

"Not that drunk," Solar shakes her head, countering, which was partly a lie, "you're the one chugging water."

"Somebody has to remember this," she snorts.

"So, remember it," she deadpans, before stepping another inch forward and wrapping her arms around the girl's neck, pulling her forward without another word. Instinctively, Minji tilted her head to the side, angling so their lips met in an indescribable blossoming of bliss.

Her lips were soft, tasting faintly of nothing at all, of water, of being slightly less intoxicated than her. She kissed the way she played, so patient, waiting, letting Solar come to her, but when she had her, she stopped the waiting. The day in her room, she was asking a question, but on the balcony, the sharp corners weren't as prominent anymore. The cap brim bumped Solar's forehead, causing her to reach up with one hand without breaking their kiss to turn it backwards, one motion, muscle memory.

The cold pressed at her back while the warmth pulled her in, and for a moment, her chest had nothing to say. It was just the railing under one hand now to steady herself, and the back of Minji's neck in the other. Minji made a small sound when Solar tipped her head, a sound she was going to hear again at some inconvenient future moment, she could only assume, and she understood such a fact and kissed her with all of her might anyway.

It was Minji who pulled away, easing back an inch with her hand still warm at Solar's cheek, her thumb moving once along her jaw like she was smoothing something closed. She stared down at her for a second, her lips forming a straight line, something soft yet delicate moving through her brown irises.

"Okay," she breathes, not quite steady, which Solar registered with something embarrassingly close to pride, "let's go inside, before Olivia sends a search party and finds evidence."

Solar giggled, drunk and warm, lighter than she'd been all week, before turning to wrestle the sliding door. She hadn't really registered the look on Minji's face, not fully, she wasn't in the right mind to do so. Minji, behind her, in the second she thought no one was looking, took the cap off her head and stared down at it, exhaling deeply into the cold. There was something on her face, underneath her face, the one that'd been holding the door open knowing exactly what it costs to do so, and the practiced quarter second where she folded the whole thing up, putting it somewhere, the way she archived footage.

By the time Solar opened the door, Minji was wearing her cap again, frontwards.

"After you, duo," she quips, her voice had every wall back up somehow again, friendly, dry, entirely normal, like nothing had happened.

And Solar, three drinks and a few shots in, was far past reading anything correctly.

47: all of it and more

\@hyein:\ if anyone speaks to me before noon ill press charges no joke this is serious

\@hyein:\ and WHOOOO let me play that game

\@hanni:\ hyein you invented the game

\@hyein:\ irrelevant hanni!

\@hyein:\ and screw this stupid meeting

\@hyein:\ who schedules a meeting after a fricking tournament

\@solar:\ girl you know who

\@hyein:\ UGHHHHHHH

\@hyein:\ mark seemed to have a good time, speaking of

\@hanni:\ yeah he is sweet, he asks about you a lot, solar

\@hanni:\ he REALLY likes you! like really really

\@hyein:\ agreed, we arent wrong about this one

\@hyein:\ promise

\@solar:\ idk i didnt spend much time around him

\@solar:\ i dont think

\@hanni:\ i think you guys were both very... under the influence

\@hyein:\ NAHHHHH

\@solar:\ true

\@solar:\ but it was fun even if i barely remember anything

Sunday arrived at Alpha Phi the way Sundays tended to after a good Saturday, slow, tender, smelling faintly of someone's burnt toast and the candle Hanni lit whenever the house needed saving from itself. Solar came downstairs a little before 11, her blanket wrapped around her shoulders still, finding the kitchen occupied by three separate girls nursing three separate regrets, and retrieved her sparkling water from the fridge without a word, taking her place against the counter like clocking into a shift.

Her head hurt in the dull, manageable way that suggested she'd gotten lucky. The rest of her felt strangely light, in a way she'd been very avoidant of all morning.

The team Discord had been going since nine, with Chenle posting the final view count like a market close, 22000, followed by some kind of bar graph he'd made for reasons no one cared to question anymore, because someone like Chenle would never make sense. Olivia replied in all caps, and even Doyoung sent one message, practice tomorrow, bring your legs, which was somehow the most threatening text in the thread.

And then, there was a message from Minji, sitting there lonely in her DMS.

\@minji:\ chenles really passionate abt that view count

\@minji:\ practice tmrw tho, dont be washed duo :]

She'd read it six times, which she was far too aware of. It was a completely normal text, the exact text Minji would have sent her on any other Sunday of the season, funny in the right places, bossy in the middle, signed off the way she'd long decided on back when they were new to each other. There was nothing to find in it, which was precisely the problem.

The night before, on the other hand, came back to her the way drunk nights did, out of order and missing it's connective tissue. She could recall the cold of the railing, the cap brim bumping her forehead, somebody has to remember this.

She'd kissed her, that part wasn't a question, and standing there in the kitchen of Alpha Phi she made herself face that fact, as her memory failed to give her mercy then. Though, what she couldn't recover was the math around it, whether she'd been obvious all night, whether anyone ever saw them slip out, whether Minji walked home afterward replaying it or simply shrugged it off the way she tended to, casually, simply.

Her thumbs hovered over her phones keyboard, before she shook her head and decided that this could wait until a later date. Right now, she had to prepare for a chapter meeting.

By now, she'd been to a few of them since August. They usually had one once a month, unless there was something else going on, some kind of event that came up. The notice being in sticky notes around the house that were clearly written by Danielle, however, was an abnormal occurrence that had her questioning what this meeting could really be about.

The living room at 1 PM looked less like a sorority chapter meeting and more like a field hospital, with girls folded into the couches in various states of recovery, water bottles clutched. Olivia was horizontal across an entire loveseat until Chaeyeon walked in, causing her to suddenly fix her posture and rearrange herself into something resembling attendance. Hyein pattered in with her blanket in open defiance of the handwritten note upstairs, with Hanni following behind her who sat cross-legged and bright-eyed, waving at a few of the girls.

Chungha didn't acknowledge the state of her chapter, she stood at the front of the room with her folder and pusture, Chaeyeon seated to her right, and began to run the agenda with the graceful efficiency of a woman who'd learned long ago that acknowledging the chaos only encouraged it. Heejin gave a recuitment update, Yerim and Yeojin were reminded about the chore rotations they kept conveniently forgetting about, the house's package situation was addressed with the gravity of a UN resolution.

Solar heard maybe half of it, she'd taken her usual spot on the arm of the far couch, close enough to Hanni to share her warmth but angled so the room stayed in front of her. She'd have described her own condition as politely present, if asked, until Chungha turned the page in her folder and spouted the thing that suddenly made everyone sit up straight.

"Before we close," she begins, eyes narrowed down as she reads over the page, "chapter survey, the exec board has been discussing a chapter event, camping trip, upstate, likely November." Her words were in the tone she reserved for ideas she'd already decided on, but was doing her due diligence anyway, eyes scanning across the girls that sat before her.

The room's energy changed shape immediately, Hyein sat up so fast her blanket flew to the floor. Everyone exchanged glances, some leaning over to whisper to each other.

When no one says anything after a few seconds, she looks back down at her paper, clearing her throat to continue, "nothing is finalized," she begins, but when the noise of murmur got too loud, she rose one hand, causing the noise to halt almost immediately. "It's in the works, it would entail two nights, cabins, tents, full itinerary. Our service would be working with the conservation group that does trail restoration in the area, which would give it a purpose that the university will recognize," she pauses then, the kind she used instead of underlining things, "I know you've all had a long semester," her eyes trail again across the girls, though they lingered particularly long on Solar, "I think the house has earned some time that's ours."

It was, Solar had to admit, an expertly built sentence. Bonding for the girls who wanted bonding, resume for the girls who wanted resume, and a Dean-shaped justification for the university, all in one graceful package. Around her, the room was already off, Hyein loudly announcing she was a professional fire builder, Hanni asking about smores logistics with the seriousness of a caterer, Yerim asking if there will be showers for no reason at all.

"This would come out of dues, correct?" Yeojin pipes, flatly, the first practical question of the afternoon.

"Girl, who cares," Olivia muttered, clearly annoyed someone asked a real question.

The once bright chatter suddenly faded again, into a darker quiet than before. "Danielle," Chungha prompts simply, turning to face the girl who was also standing, and had been the entire time, though she'd been minutes late.

She had a folder in her hands, which had already been opened, like she was waiting for her moment. She walks then, to stand in front of the girls, covering up where Chungha was.

"It wouldn't touch dues," she assures the room, pleasantly and entirely too fake, "we came out of the tailgate season ahead of budget, and I've priced three sites within two hours of campus. If we hold deposits by Friday, we lock the group rate," she flips a page without needing to look down at it, "food, fireword, and transport are already line-itemed, the fund covers it comfortably."

There was a moment before the room accepted this as good news, everyone exchanging another look and shrugging, and the chatter slowly began to pick back up, focused on the word smores.

Solar didn't join the fun. Instead, she sat very still on the arm of the couch, watching Danielle closer her folder, letting the wrongness of it arrive on it's own schedule. Chungha had floated the trip as an idea, gauging interest, nothing finalized, in the works, but Danielle answered with deposits, dates, and a group rate, complete with a folder that'd already clearly been prepared before anyone in this room knew there was anything to prepare for. For a proposal that'd supposedly just left the exec board, her numbers sounded eerily close to that of a receipt.

Comfortably, the word repeated in her head, the fund covers it comfortably.

Covers what? Solar found her thoughts wandering, a girls trip or something already sized bigger than the room it was announced to?

She became aware then that this was the new shape of her Sundays, sitting in her own living room doing forensic accounting on the treasurer's tone. The bathroom mirror had started it, the handwriting, the pattern that lined up too well to unsee but too poorly to prove. Now, every word that came out of Danielle's mouth got weighed twice, once for what it said and once for what it rhymed with.

Some girls really do bounce back better than others.

What a 'lucky' girl our Solar is.

The captions were loud under the budget report, the same voice wearing a different outfit, and all she had to prove it was her gut.

Across the room, Danielle sat down by the window and smoothed her skirt, and without searching for it, found Solar's eyes, as if she'd known exactly where the stare had been coming from. But she didn't smirk, that was the thing people got wrong about her, she simply held the look for a moment, pleasant, unbothered, the expression of someone updating a chart, before returning her attention to Chungha at the front.

Solar's eyes fell to the carpet, pulse tapping at her throat, hating the way it worked on her every single time.

"We'll circulate an interest form this week," Chungha concludes, closing her folder in a way that closed the meeting with it, "that's all, ladies, and congratulations to our winners," she nods towards Solar and Olivia with a smile, "you've made the house very proud."

The room then broke apart into noise and blankets, with Hyein already listing everyone who'd be sharing her tent, which appeared to be a unilateral decisions, and Hanni leaned into Solar's shoulder with her whole genuine weight, her way of a hug without it being a hug.

"A camping trip," she sighs, dreamily, "that's exactly what this house needs, doesn't it sound lovely?"

"Sounds cold," Solar bluntly states, still unsure about the way it tasted in her mouth, watching Danielle's folder disappear under her arm as she left the room first, "just wondering how long it's been in the works."

-

Meanwhile, 1 PM at Sigma Chi was a very different kind of monster. Renjun and Jisung were already on one of the couches, talking about something irrelevant, there on time for the meeting half on purpose. Chenle came in next, a rather grump look on his face due to his lack of sleep and because he always hated having to attend what he called worthless wastes of his rather valuable time. Jeno and Jaemin stumbled in from the kitchen, both of their eyes blatantly red with a blunt literally still in between Jeno's fingers. They were obviously hungover, despite the fact they were currently high, flopping onto one of the couches and both dramatically panting like they'd ran a marathon.

"Dude," Renjun hisses, "can you put that up? It fucking stinks, and Jaehyun is literally about to be in here."

"Bro," Jeno snorts humorously, "lighten up, man."

Renjun just sighed in disappointment, but Jeno eventually listened, only because Jaemin did him the liberty of taking the blunt, putting it out, and shoving it somewhere in his pocket.

"Where's everyone at?" Jisung spoke up, tapping his foot impatiently against the carpeted floor, "Man, I have plans today, dude."

"Yeah right," Jeno snickers, "with what, your pocket pussy?"

"Fuck you," the boy retorted in offense, pointing a finger at Jeno, who was just snickering to himself, before Jaemin joined in because his laugh was painfully contagious.

In the midst of the jokes, Mark entered, looking shockingly put together despite how late he had gotten home last night. There were minor eyebags under his eyes, and he seemed rather focused on something on his phone, angrily tapping on it every few seconds, narrowing his eyes down at the screen. He was so engrossed he didn't look up as he fell onto a couch, next to Jaemin.

"Fuck you doing?" Jaemin nosily blurted, leaning over Mark's shoulder to look down at his screen. At the sight of it, the boy starts giggling like a school girl, "this guy is whipped, bro."

"I wanna know!" Jeno whined, trying to lean over Jaemin to look at Mark's screen himself.

"He's ordering flowers to Alpha Phi," Jaemin announced to everyone, "God, I sure do wonder who they're for."

"Yo, Mark is a fucking nut job," Jeno is clapping now, finding this absolutely hysterical, "for real, man? You're still going after that?"

"Can you guys shut up?" Mark finally snapped, looking up at the boys for a mere second before he looked back down to his phone, "Just keep your mouths shut, seriously." For once, he sounded genuinely angry, like it was serious to him and he was going to get pissed if they didn't keep it quiet.

So, they listened, which was a rarity. But everyone knew what it looked like when Mark got really mad, and no one wanted to deal with such.

Renjun made a ts sound of disappointment anyway, but Mark didn't hear, since his thumbs were flying across the screen then.

Only then, did the sound of footsteps echo again, though it still wasn't Jaehyun, Johnny, or Yuta. It was Haechan, who was evidently blatantly hungover himself, much like Jeno and Jaemin, but he managed to look worse than even them. His hair was an unkept mess, skin pale, almost grey, his lips were chapped solid, nose red like he'd caught a cold. He looked bad, so bad that Renjun, Chenle, and Jisung just found themselves gawking at him like he was some sort of mythical creature.

"Damn, how much did you do last night?" Jeno asked, "Coulda fucking invited me."

Haechan didn't say anything as he fell into a chair near the window, isolated from everyone but close enough to socialize. "Fuck off," he grumbled, voice hoarse, "you just suck up my supply and never pay me back."

"Ay, there he is!" Jeno cheered, as if he was happy that Haechan was back to being his old self. Maybe he was, because he didn't see the issue with it, since it was exactly how he was. It wasn't that Jeno hated Solar, but he himself didn't ever think about the possibility of having real feelings for anyone.

Renjun and Chenle watched Haechan carefully. Chenle found himself pursing his lips, trying to not care, but yet, something in him was telling him to. He thought about Solar recently, the way Mark kept showing up, kept inviting himself, kept posting her and letting all of the comments speculate and assume.

Yet, Haechan, who was known around school for sleeping with different women, for using girls and then pretending he'd never met them, that every girl threw themselves at, had stopped doing all of those things the first day he met her.

He wasn't an idiot, after all. Chenle was the smartest one in Sigma Chi, and he had a feeling what was happening. He just didn't know what to do about it.

But, there was no time to think about it then, because Johnny and Yuta entered, with Jaehyun following behind. He had a stack of papers in his hand, a button up on like he'd just come from some fancy event, hair perfectly gelled. It was a wonder why someone would dress themselves up like that at 1 PM on a Saturday, but none of them had the slightest clue what the life of Jaehyun Lee really looked like.

"I'll make this quick," he begins, without greetings, because he didn't have time for things like that, "as you recall, we typically take a chapter vacation around the end of the year."

"Fuck yeah!" Jeno screamed immediately, "Are we going skiing again?!"

Jaehyun looked up from his paper, no expression written on his face, but his tone was entirely unimpressed, "No, and do not interrupt me again."

"My bad."

"We'll be camping this year," he continues then, not looking at his paper anymore, "two days, but unlike last year, we will be partaking in a service project this year."

The entire room collectively groaned in complete agony.

Meanwhile, Jaemin had a smirk on his face, like he'd already known this was coming, like he had more information than anyone else. His eyes, in particular, fell on Haechan and Mark, like he was thinking about the possible chaos and loving it.

"This is with Alpha, isn't it?" Renjun deadpanned, offering the obvious in the voice of someone who already knew it was not a question.

"I'm not yet permitted to share," Jaehyun admits, honestly, "We're working on it, I'll share more soon, but be aware it's coming. Clear?"

Mark had looked up from his phone finally, seeming to finish with whatever he had been doing that entire time. The words from the conversation he'd just heard repeated in his head. Camping trip with Alpha Phi.

He turned then, as everyone else began to bolt out of the room, his eyes catching Haechan's for the slightest of seconds.

This could either go very badly, he thought then, or very well.

-

By six in the evening, Alpha Phi had thawed into something much more peaceful. Someone's speaker played from the kitchen where a pasta situation was developing. Hyein, Yerim, and Yeojin were watching a horror movie with the volume too low to actually be scary, and Solar had claimed the good corner of the couch an hour ago to answer bracket replies she was three days behind on, her legs over Hanni's lap, who was busy crocheting atop of them. Finally, the Sunday felt like a Sunday should feel.

That was, until the doorbell rang at exactly 6:40 PM.

"Not it!" Hyein chimed instantly, not glancing away from the television, where the killer was chasing a man through a cornfield.

Yerim and Yeojin followed her, causing Hanni to sigh out in disappointment at their childish antics. She shakes her head, gently picking up Solar's legs that were on her and moving them, before she scooted off the couch, leaving her crochet carefully placed on the cushion behind her. Solar didn't look up, though she heard the front door opening in the entryway, followed by a pause of contemplation from Hanni, before she'd gasped, which then turned into a squeal, which was enough to cause everyone's head in the living room to snap up, and for Heejin and Olivia, who were in the kitchen, to pop their heads out in confusion.

"Sol, it's for you!" Hanni sing-songed.

The delivery man was already down the front steps by the time Solar got to the door, just a van door sliding shut, followed by taillights, and Hanni stood in the entryway holding an arrangement of flowers roughly the size of a healthy toddler.

Objectively, they were beautiful, cream paper, wrapped and tied like it had somewhere better to be, stems of soft blush gathered fat and full around deeper ones, ranunculus layered in little paper lanterns, a few blooms so darkly red they read almost black against all that pink. The girls arrived from three directions at once, drawn by the same frequency, some of their hands covering their mouths, Olivia piping oh my GOD in the doorway of the kitchen.

Knowing she had no choice, Solar took the arrangement out of Hanni's arms, because if she didn't the girl was going to force her to. It was heavier than it looked, was her first thought, as she struggled to flip the cream card over, looking down at the one line of handwriting. She didn't need to recognize the handwriting to know who it came from.

for the best support at su, proud of you sol - m

"Mark, Mark sent them?!" Hyein was shrieking, shamelessly over her shoulder, "oh my God, the dinner, and now this? He's so down bad!"

"That's so romantic," Yeojin offered, genuinely seeming moved by the action, holding a hand to her heart.

"It's... a lot," Solar musters, which was true in every direction she could think of.

The house opinion arrived loud and unanimous, and somewhere under it, Hanni had grown very pink with a pride she was visibly deciding whether to confess, rocking once on her heels before the confession won.

"Okay, I have to say it!" she cries, pressing her hands together, "he messaged me yesterday asking what kind you liked... and I told him peonies, because you said they were your favorite that night we did face masks... he was really excited, he wanted it to be this big surprise," she was glowing, at her own supporting role in it all, "how thoughtful is that? He was so scared he would get it wrong!"

All of the girls still standing there started audibly gushing at the heartfelt story. Solar's lips formed a straight line as she nodded, "so thoughtful."

Her mouth did the smiling, but the rest of her had gone somewhere else entirely, as she was looking at the fat blush heads nested in the paper, and a fact seemed to be floating up from a former life, uninvited, the way they seemed to these days. Peonies had a season, she pondered, it ended in June. Yet, her mother always kept them on the entry table year round anyway, flown in from Chile from October on. Solar knew this because she'd once listened to her complain, at length, about what winter stems cost per flower, in the tone of a woman for whom the price was privately the whole point.

It was the end of October, in upstate New York, and she was holding a bouquet of peonies.

Nobody else in the entryway would ever run that math, it was the odd, stomach churning part. The girls simply saw a sweet boy who'd asked her friend what flowers she liked, and they weren't wrong, which was truthful. Solar was the only person in the house who could hear the noise underneath it, the one that said these had been sourced, that somewhere between Hanni's message and this doorstep, a boy her age had made the same kind of phone call her mother made, casually, to solve October, because getting it right on the first try mattered to him that much.

It was the nicest thing anyone had ever done for her, perhaps, in a grandiose sense. More so, however, there was something that tasted sour about the bouquet, the way the captions did, as well as the bill that never came, kindness with excellent production value. She hated herself a little, the way it sounded, he asked Hanni what you liked, she scolded her brain, that's what people do when they like someone.

Still, standing in the warm doorway light, with the entire house cooing around her, the question finally seemed to surface, the one she'd been stepping around since the day Haechan texted her he's doing it on purpose and Mark shrugged off posting her like no big deal but continued to do it anyway, from the way she watched him shrug off the bill and make the plate tower.

The question was, what does it cost, and who's keeping the receipt?

\@sollee:\ someones REALLY a romantic

\@sollee:\ the flowers are pretty mark, you really didnt have to

\@markylee:\ glad they made it :)

\@markylee:\ yeah i totally did btw, MVPs get flowers those r the rules, invented them rn

\@markylee:\ you earned all of it and more sol

48: mondays are the worst

The only place Solar would hate being in more than Intro to Business was Media & Society. She decided, in the midst of her dreadful walk to the class, that it was indeed worse to be stuck in a classroom looking at a drunk or entirely very high Haechan with girls piling all over him like a trampoline. And she only thought this because of the fact it brought up the most uncomfortable knot in her stomach she had ever felt.

Though, the site of Danielle slipping into the chair next to her was a close second.

Still, Solar learned to manage it the way you managed the weather, forcing a small smile on her face and mixing her iced coffee with her straw, a Dutch Bros Mark brought her that morning with that dorky smile on his face and his caramel hair perfectly styled underneath the dim October sun. She knew Danielle saw him, because they'd arrived at class around the same time, and Mark had been standing outside the building doors tapping his foot against the ground nervously. She'd felt Danielle's eyes on them, as much as she tried to pretend she didn't.

So, she tried to mentally prepare herself for whatever the girl was going to spout today. Her one earbud seemed to be the only thing keeping her sane, her eyes focusing on the professor as much as she could. Danielle's silence in class had become its own institution over the past week, but she knew she wouldn't be able to help herself today, from the way her eyes kept snapping over every couple of minutes, like she was waiting to strike.

But yet, she didn't say anything through most of the lecture, which was the craft of her, it seemed. She annotated her slides, highlighting her color system, becoming a portrait of academic diligence right up until the professor dismissed them, the sound of laptops shutting echoing throughout the room.

Just as Solar closed her binder, it was when she heard the deepest exhale from besides her.

\"Haechan's looked rough recently, hasn't he?\" she sighs in complete fake sadness, \"Mm, has he been keeping up in Media & Society?\"

Solar tried to ignore her at first, concentrating on packing her things, not wanting to offer her anything. Danielle noticed this, rising from her seat and smoothing her skirt, pausing the way she paused when she'd just remembered something minimal.

\"You've gotta feel for boys like him, though, hm?\" she muses, more to the ceiling than to Solar, as if she'd thought about it for awhile, \"it's easy to forget who you really are, good thing he seemed to remember, though,\" she tapped her chin with her finger a few times, pondering again, before her dark eyes snapped back to Solar, \"you know, Solar, you make me think of a girl I used to know.\"

It was such a weird and random thing to say, one that caused all of the deja vu comments to snap back into her head. Before she could stop herself from giving in, she snapped, \"Danielle, what the hell are you talking about, seriously?!\"

But the girl was already halfway down the steps. Though, she still heard her, though didn't care enough to turn around. Instead, she just called behind her, casually, \"just remember, some things never change.\"

Then she was gone, out the doors before any answer could've caught her. Solar sat there with her half zipped bag, running the sentence back twice in her head, the way she now ran back everything Danielle said in that creepy tone of hers. Some things never change, she'd heard those words before, that night at the party. The way Mark kept telling her there's so much she doesn't know about him, he's not a good person. The way everyone kept looking at her and talking about deja vu, how a girl kept seeming to come up at times that made no sense.

She forced herself to get up and leave before she could think anymore about it.

As bad as she wanted to ask Mark in Statistics, she didn't have the guts to. That was her problem, she was bold until she wasn't. Maybe, deep down, she was afraid of what the answer, of what knowing it could mean. She was afraid of knowing the truth behind all of the comments, so much so that a part of her almost decided she'd rather stay in the confusion.

\-

NEW POST\ \@SUnews

![\@SUnews: looks like everyone's favorite recruitment chair is hard at work!! good thing our beloved haechan remembered who he REALLY is, some things never change, do they? we sure do hope the recruiting is going better than the relapsing!](media/image2.png){width="6.5in" height="8.125in"}

\@SUnews: looks like everyone's favorite recruitment chair is hard at work!! good thing our beloved haechan remembered who he REALLY is, some things never change, do they? we sure do hope the recruiting is going better than the relapsing!

She told herself she wasn't going to look at it.

It was so bad that even Hyein and Hanni didn't mention it in their group chat, she knew because they usually let her know of new posts that related to her. And while she wasn't explicitly mentioned in this one, she felt that it obviously related to her by association.

But her eyes dared to trail to the comments.

just glad hes finally letting girls in again

god i missed him

he inv me tonight!!! we're so back ladies ;)

omg im so jealous

how to be invited to sig?? ugh just gimme 1 chance

She started to feel nauseous, clicking out of the comments before the vomit became too real. It took her a second to process the boy in the photo, was that really Haechan? The same Haechan she knew? The boy in it looked more like a costume, grey under the flash, folded forward over his own knees, like the night had set down on top of him. Her eyes went where anyone's eyes would go, you'd think, to the bottles, the mess, the caption already sharpening its little knives, her stomach seeming to drop through the floor of the house.

She knew that nightstand, knew the bedspread underneath him, the exact grey in the low light, the wall behind his shoulder, what the room smelled like with the door closed, vanilla under everything, the faint lingering of smoke, and she knew precisely how far that doorway was from where he was sitting because she'd stood in it, one morning that no longer felt real, holding her shoes in one hand and the feeling the faintest ache in her chest. His room, she pondered, someone had stood in the door of his room and taken such a photo, and twelve thousand strangers were currently standing in that doorway with her, judging him like he was some sort of zoo animal.

Something in her chest felt oddly uncomfortable, wrong, a foreign object making itself know. It was older than the hurt, hotter and simpler, and it didn't matter what he'd done or hadn't done to her, what he had or hadn't been playing, whether every rumor about him was gospel, nobody deserved this. No one deserved to have their worst moments blasted online to thousands of people, to be used for content, it was fucked up. She could accept when it happened to her, over and over, because it'd been that way her entire life, because she was groomed to believe that was just how it had to be as a Lee.

It felt so wrong.

And all too familiar.

good thing he seemed to remember, some things never change.

She read the caption again, staring down at the double exclamation points. There was no version of her fourth read where the words came out different, the more times she thought back to their Business class that day, the more sour it tasted on her tongue. Word for word, she realized, she outed herself word for word to her face.

The feeling that arrived wasn't necessarily surprise. It was worse, more like recognition, the specific vertigo of watching a suspicion you'd been carefully keeping in the maybe pile stand up and introduce itself.

Her body moved before her mind could catch up.

In a matter of seconds, she was padding out of her bedroom door, down the carpeted pink hallway, her footsteps slamming against the plush.

Danielle's door was open, because it always was, transparency being one of her many performed virtues. She was at her desk with a highlighter, some spreadsheet fanned out in front of her, her curly hair flowing down her back, lips smirked even as she was simply staring down at her notes.

She didn't look up right away, which told Solar she'd been expecting her.

\"Take it down.\"

\"Hm?\" Danielle pipes, unbothered, pretending she was far too engrossed in her spreadsheet. She taps her highlighter a single time on the paper, before spinning around in her chair, a pleasant smile on her lips, \"you're going to need to provide some context here, Solar. Take what down?\"

Solar could only scoff in response, crossing her arms over her chest, \"You know what,\" her voice came out low and even, though it was shaking underneath, a combination she hadn't produced since she was sixteen and standing in her father's office, \"your stupid fucking post, Danielle, take it down.\"

The girl chuckled then, shaking her head in disbelief, \"I think you have me confused with a gossip account, Solar, I'm the treasurer,\" she giggles, high pitched and orchestrated, \"I don't have time for petty drama like that.\"

\"You're not fooling anyone, Danielle,\" Solar snaps, \"you said it to me this morning and now your exact words are sitting under a photo of him. Do you really think I'm that fucking dumb?\" She was getting more heated than she meant, but it was like the emotion was taking over her, like she was defending herself to her older brother that decided her life was his to manage. And there was nothing she hated more than being treated like something to manage, like a story to write.

Something moved behind Danielle's expression then. It wasn't fear, nothing like that, it was more of what appeared to be interest, a chart being updated. She set her highlighter down, parallel to the spreadsheet's edge and slowly folded her hands in her lap.

\"That sure is a coincidence, isn't it?\" she sighs out, \"though, it's quite a common phrase, I'm sure I've heard it somewhere myself\... maybe from\...\" she pauses for a second, looking up at the ceiling before the lightbulb pops, \"oh, perhaps a friend of yours, actually.\"

Solar had to bite the inside of her cheek to stop herself from blowing up further. Mark, she was obviously talking about Mark. She was trying harder and harder to dig under her skin.

\"I'm not falling for it,\" she hisses, \"just take the fucking post down.\"

Her head tilts, the almost-kind one, lips pursing in thought, \"wow, Solar, you seem really upset,\" she observes, \"is he that important to you? Because that's\... well, interesting, you know, given everything\...\" She trails off, making a sound of evident disappointment.

\"He's a person,\" Solar seethes, \"whatever else is true about him, that photo is fucked up, someone stood in his doorway and sold him at his worst for what? Likes?\" she snorts, \"And you put a smiley little caption on it like it's funny\... Even if I can't prove it's you, I'm telling you I know, Danielle. I fucking know it's you.\"

For a moment, the room held perfectly still, the highlighter, the spreadsheets, the afternoon light falling in neat stripes through the blinds Danielle likely dusted weekly, and the curly haired girl looked at her with what was, horribly, almost adjacent to respect.

\"Okay,\" she sighs, softly, \"since we're doing the whole to-your-face today,\" she rises from her chair, unhurried, crossing the room past Solar. Her hand reaches out, shutting the door with a click that sounded like it'd been rehearsed, before she'd spun back around with her arms crossed, loosely, comfortably, a woman on her home floor of the building. \"Here's what I know, Solar,\" she clears her throat, that big creepy smile on her lips, \"I know you didn't sleep in this house that Friday night, and I know your brother sat both of those boys you sure like so much down about you, and ten days later, Mark Lee is buying your friends dinner and sending you flowers,\" she taps her foot on the floor a few times, shaking her head in sympathy, \"and you know what else? I know those two boys are not as innocent as you think they are, and it's just killing you to find out why, huh?\" She took a single step closer, her nose almost touching Solar's, her breath smelling faintly of mint, \"But you know who else isn't innocent? Jaehyun Lee's little sister.\"

She giggles then, like she'd been saving that one and waiting to use it. Then, she steps back a few feet, clapping her hands together, \"but, maybe that's why you all like each other so much, hm? One and the same.\"

The floor of the house was gone. Solar stood there with all of her fury still in her hands and nowhere to put it, because every road out of this room ran through her brother, her father, the machine, the irritated and disgusting architecture of her entire life, and Danielle watched her do the math in real time, that smile still wiped across her lips in the name of victory, because the math was the entire point.

\"Like I said,\" she finally exhales, \"I don't have time for that kind of petty drama, Sol, but whoever does sure seems well informed, don't you think?\"

She sat back down at her desk as if nothing happened, uncapping the highlighter in one motion, eyes already back on the spreadsheet. \"Thanks for stopping by, really, but some of us have real things to worry about.\"

\-

Solar didn't have the energy to go to practice. She'd texted Doyoung when she walked back into her room, and the team Discord, telling them she wasn't feeling well and wouldn't be of any use coming. Doyoung, who was probably sighing down at his clipboard, eventually said it's alright, take care before adding double VOD review duty tho to which she'd replied i'll take it, thx coach and called it a day.

She knew Minji was going to be sad she missed it, and her teammates would make fun of her, but she needed the rest then. She needed to try to work through everything going through her head. But more so, she just wanted to lay in bed and sleep the rest of her life away.
